target_output.title: FHIR R4 Interoperability Broker Architecture v2
target_output.executive_summary: A FHIR R4-compliant REST API broker built with Java 21 Spring Boot 3.2 and HAPI FHIR on GKE Autopilot, replacing a $480K/year vendor middleware solution. Normalizes clinical data from Epic FHIR R4 into a PostgreSQL JSONB resource store with indexed search parameter extraction for sub-50ms query latency. SMART on FHIR OAuth 2.0 via Keycloak with mTLS for partner connections (GCP Certificate Manager partner CA). Nightly reconciliation validates broker state against Epic source, logging conflicts to reconciliation_conflicts with Alertmanager notification for clinical informatics. ONC 21st Century Cures Act compliant with Touchstone FHIR conformance suite in CI.
target_output.architecture_overview: Headless FHIR R4 REST service deployed as three GKE Autopilot workloads: fhir-api (Spring Boot + HAPI FHIR JPA Server, 3 replicas behind Cloud Armor WAF), epic-adapter (CronJob polling Epic FHIR R4 API every 15 minutes), and reconciliation-job (Spring Batch CronJob nightly at 01:00 UTC). PostgreSQL 16 Cloud SQL stores FHIR resources as JSONB in fhir_resources with extracted search parameters in resource_search_params for indexed FHIR search queries. Keycloak 24 serves as SMART on FHIR authorization server with token introspection cached in Redis for 60 seconds. Partner connections require mTLS certificates issued by GCP Certificate Manager partner CA. FHIR AuditEvent resources generated by HAPI interceptor for every PHI access, stored in fhir_resources and replicated to Cloud Logging.
target_output.technology_stack: Language: Java 21 with virtual threads (Project Loom) for high-concurrency FHIR request handling. Framework: Spring Boot 3.2 with Spring Security OAuth2 Resource Server. FHIR library: HAPI FHIR 6.x with JPA Server and custom PostgreSQL JPA resource provider. Auth: Keycloak 24 (SMART on FHIR profile). Database: PostgreSQL 16 on Cloud SQL (db-standard-4, HA). Cache: Memorystore Redis 7 (STANDARD_HA). Infrastructure: GKE Autopilot us-central1, Cloud Armor WAF, GCP Certificate Manager, Artifact Registry. Batch: Spring Batch for reconciliation and search param extraction. CI/CD: GitLab CI with Touchstone FHIR conformance suite. Monitoring: Prometheus + Grafana + Alertmanager.
target_output.functional_feature_map: FHIR R4 Read Endpoints: GET /fhir/r4/{ResourceType}/{id} for Patient, Encounter, Observation, MedicationRequest, Condition. FHIR R4 Search Endpoints: GET /fhir/r4/Observation?patient={id}&category=vital-signs, GET /fhir/r4/MedicationRequest?patient={id}&status=active using indexed resource_search_params. CapabilityStatement: GET /fhir/r4/metadata returning HAPI-generated CapabilityStatement declaring supported resources and search parameters. SMART on FHIR Auth: Keycloak authorization server with client credentials and authorization code flows; scope enforcement per resource type. Epic Ingestion: every 15 minutes, EpicAdapter fetches updated resources via Epic FHIR R4 API, normalizes Epic extensions, populates fhir_resources and resource_search_params. Nightly Reconciliation: Spring Batch compares broker fhir_resources.last_fetched_at against Epic source, re-fetches stale records, logs field discrepancies to reconciliation_conflicts.
target_output.system_components: fhir-api Deployment (HAPI FHIR JPA Server + Spring Security SMART scope enforcement + HAPI AuditEvent interceptor), Keycloak 24 StatefulSet (SMART on FHIR authorization server with Redis token introspection cache), epic-adapter CronJob (Epic FHIR R4 API client + FhirSearchParamExtractorStep), reconciliation-job CronJob (Spring Batch with FhirReconciliationStep + conflict logging), PostgreSQL 16 Cloud SQL (fhir_resources JSONB + resource_search_params indexed), Memorystore Redis 7 (Keycloak introspection cache + reconciliation state), Cloud Armor WAF (partner-facing HTTPS load balancer), GCP Certificate Manager (partner CA and mTLS certificate lifecycle), Cloud Logging (AuditEvent replication and Keycloak token events).
target_output.workflows: Partner FHIR Read Workflow: Partner clinical app sends GET /fhir/r4/Observation?patient=P12345&category=vital-signs with Bearer token in Authorization header and mTLS client certificate. Cloud Armor WAF validates mTLS certificate against GCP Certificate Manager partner CA. Spring Security intercepts request, calls Keycloak introspection endpoint (cached in Redis for 60s). SMART scope user/Observation.read verified. HAPI FHIR JPA Server executes FHIR search via resource_search_params table: SELECT r.resource_json FROM fhir_resources r JOIN resource_search_params p ON r.id = p.fhir_resource_id WHERE r.resource_type='Observation' AND p.param_name='patient' AND p.param_value='P12345' AND p.system='vital-signs'. Returns FHIR Bundle. HAPI AuditEvent interceptor writes AuditEvent resource recording agent (partner app), entity (Observation bundle), action=R. Nightly Reconciliation: Spring Batch ReconciliationJob loads all fhir_resources records with last_fetched_at older than 23 hours. For each: calls Epic FHIR R4 API GET /Observation/{id}. Compares returned resource against stored resource_json field by field. On mismatch: writes reconciliation_conflicts record with field_path, broker_value, source_value. Updates fhir_resources with fresh resource_json from Epic. Alertmanager fires P2 alert to clinical-informatics channel when unresolved conflict count exceeds 100.
target_output.data_model: fhir_resources (id UUID PK, resource_type VARCHAR(50) NOT NULL, fhir_id VARCHAR(100) NOT NULL, patient_id VARCHAR(100) NULLABLE, resource_json JSONB NOT NULL, source_system VARCHAR(50) DEFAULT 'epic', source_system_id VARCHAR(100), last_fetched_at TIMESTAMPTZ, version_id INT DEFAULT 1, is_active BOOLEAN DEFAULT true, created_at TIMESTAMPTZ) PARTITIONED BY LIST (resource_type). resource_search_params (id UUID PK, fhir_resource_id UUID FK REFERENCES fhir_resources(id), resource_type VARCHAR(50), param_name VARCHAR(100), param_value TEXT, system VARCHAR(200) NULLABLE) -- B-tree index on (resource_type, param_name, param_value). reconciliation_conflicts (id UUID PK, resource_type VARCHAR(50), fhir_id VARCHAR(100), field_path TEXT, broker_value TEXT, source_value TEXT, detected_at TIMESTAMPTZ, resolved_at TIMESTAMPTZ NULLABLE, resolution VARCHAR(50) NULLABLE CHECK IN ('source_wins','broker_retained','manual_review')). partner_certificates (id UUID PK, partner_id VARCHAR(100), certificate_subject TEXT, expiry_at TIMESTAMPTZ, issued_at TIMESTAMPTZ, revoked_at TIMESTAMPTZ NULLABLE).
target_output.api_design: FHIR R4 REST API base URL /fhir/r4. Read: GET /fhir/r4/Patient/{id}, GET /fhir/r4/Encounter/{id}, GET /fhir/r4/Observation/{id}, GET /fhir/r4/MedicationRequest/{id}, GET /fhir/r4/Condition/{id}. Search: GET /fhir/r4/Patient?identifier={system}|{value}, GET /fhir/r4/Observation?patient={id}&category={code}&date=ge2026-01-01, GET /fhir/r4/MedicationRequest?patient={id}&status=active, GET /fhir/r4/Condition?patient={id}&clinical-status=active. Metadata: GET /fhir/r4/metadata (CapabilityStatement). Auth: Bearer token (SMART on FHIR) + mTLS client certificate. Error: FHIR OperationOutcome with ONC-required error codes. All responses include X-Request-ID correlation header and X-Data-Freshness-Warning header when last_fetched_at > 6 hours.
target_output.security_and_compliance: mTLS: GCP Certificate Manager private CA (partner-ca.hospital.org); partner certificates issued with partner_id SAN extension; 90-day TTL; automated managed renewal via Certificate Manager; 30-day expiry Alertmanager notification to partner onboarding team. Partner onboarding: partner submits CSR; hospital team issues certificate via gcloud certificate-manager certificates create; certificate fingerprint stored in partner_certificates table; Cloud Armor policy restricts API access to clients presenting valid partner CA certificates. SMART on FHIR: Keycloak introspection called on every request; introspection results cached 60s in Redis to limit Keycloak load; patient/* scopes restricted to patient_id from token context claim; system/* scopes require explicit allowlist per partner_id. HIPAA: PHI encrypted at rest via Cloud SQL KMS CMEK; TLS 1.3 in transit; AuditEvent FHIR resources for every PHI read stored in fhir_resources and streamed to Cloud Logging; 7-year retention. ONC information blocking: no access delay for any patient's EHI; Consent.provision.deny support for patient-restricted sharing; CapabilityStatement accurately declares all supported resources.
target_output.deployment_and_operations: GKE Autopilot us-central1: fhir-api Deployment (3 replicas, HPA min 3 / max 8 on CPU 70%, PodDisruptionBudget minAvailable 2), Keycloak StatefulSet (2 replicas with PostgreSQL backend), epic-adapter CronJob (every 15 minutes, job deadline 10 minutes), reconciliation-job CronJob (01:00 UTC, job deadline 4 hours). Cloud SQL db-standard-4 (4 vCPU, 26GB RAM, 500GB SSD, HA with automatic failover, automated backups to GCS, PITR enabled). Memorystore Redis 7 STANDARD_HA 4GB. Cloud Armor WAF: OWASP Top 10 rules + rate limit 1000 req/min per partner_id. GitLab CI pipeline: mvn verify (unit + integration tests with Testcontainers PostgreSQL), Docker build, Artifact Registry push, GKE rolling update, Touchstone FHIR R4 conformance suite against staging (blocks production on any failure). Database migration: Flyway with staged migration scripts; resource_search_params backfill migration runs as Spring Batch job on first deployment.
target_output.observability: Prometheus via Spring Actuator Micrometer: fhir_request_duration_seconds histogram (labels: resource_type, operation, http_status, partner_id), fhir_request_total counter, epic_adapter_fetch_duration_seconds histogram, epic_adapter_resources_ingested_total counter (labels: resource_type), reconciliation_conflicts_total counter (labels: resource_type, field_path), reconciliation_unresolved_gauge. Grafana dashboards: FHIR endpoint p50/p95/p99 latency by resource type and partner, Epic adapter freshness lag per resource type, reconciliation conflict rate and unresolved conflict count. Alertmanager rules: fhir_request_error_rate > 0.5% for 5 minutes (P2), epic_adapter_circuit_open (P2), reconciliation_unresolved_conflicts > 100 (P3 to clinical informatics Slack), partner certificate expiry < 30 days (P3 to partner onboarding team).
target_output.cost_and_scaling: GKE Autopilot fhir-api (3-8 pods at ~$0.10/hr each) ~$215-520/month. Cloud SQL db-standard-4 HA ~$310/month. Memorystore Redis 7 STANDARD_HA ~$150/month. Keycloak pods ~$50/month. Cloud Armor ~$20/month. Total ~$745-1050/month, equivalent to ~$9-13K/year versus $480K/year vendor middleware -- 97% cost reduction. Epic adapter polling at 15-minute intervals generates ~8,000 API calls/month per resource type; Epic FHIR API has no per-call cost for hospital customers.
target_output.phased_implementation: Phase 1 MVP (12 weeks): Patient, Encounter, Observation, MedicationRequest, Condition read and search endpoints with resource_search_params indexed extraction; Epic FHIR R4 adapter (15-minute polling); SMART on FHIR client credentials flow via Keycloak; mTLS with GCP Certificate Manager partner CA; nightly reconciliation with reconciliation_conflicts logging; FHIR CapabilityStatement; Touchstone conformance suite in GitLab CI; Cloud Armor WAF; Alertmanager for reconciliation and certificate expiry alerts. Phase 2 v1.1 (8 weeks): Cerner FHIR R4 adapter with multi-source resource merge strategy; DiagnosticReport and CarePlan endpoints; authorization code + PKCE flow for user-facing partner apps; Consent.provision.deny support. Phase 3 Year 2: FHIR Subscriptions (WebSocket-based push to partner apps on resource updates); FHIR Bulk Data Export ($export operation) for population health queries; Meditech adapter.
target_output.development_guidelines: Java 21 with virtual threads (server.tomcat.threads.max disabled; use spring.threads.virtual.enabled=true). Maven with enforcer plugin preventing SNAPSHOT dependencies in main branch. HAPI FHIR configuration: disable HAPI default interceptors that conflict with Spring Security; override JPA resource provider with custom PostgreSQL-backed implementation. Testcontainers PostgreSQL for integration tests; no H2 in-memory DB (JSONB not supported). Flyway migration naming: V{timestamp}__{description}.sql. All FHIR OperationOutcome error codes documented in API README per ONC requirements. Touchstone test credentials stored in GitLab CI variables; conformance test results uploaded as GitLab artifacts for audit trail.
target_output.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: Epic FHIR R4 API response extensions change between Epic versions, breaking EpicAdapter normalization logic. Mitigation: EpicAdapter includes FHIR resource version detection; unknown extensions passed through to resource_json without normalization rather than failing ingest. Risk: resource_search_params backfill migration on initial deployment takes 2-4 hours for large existing fhir_resources tables. Mitigation: backfill runs as background Spring Batch job; API serves from JSONB GIN index during migration with degraded search performance warning in CapabilityStatement. Tradeoff: HAPI FHIR JPA Server over custom FHIR layer for faster ONC conformance compliance at cost of reduced control over database query patterns -- mitigated by custom JPA resource provider override. Tradeoff: Keycloak introspection on every request over stateless JWT validation to honor prompt token revocation for partner applications, adding ~10ms per request -- mitigated by 60-second Redis cache.
target_output.open_questions_resolved: Confirmed resource_search_params extraction table with B-tree index closes FHIR search performance gap (ISS-001). GCP Certificate Manager partner CA with 90-day TTL and automated renewal closes mTLS specification gap (ISS-002). Reconciliation conflict resolution: Epic is authoritative source; broker overwrites on conflict; clinical informatics notified via Alertmanager at 100 unresolved conflicts threshold (ISS-003). Keycloak introspection cache TTL set to 60 seconds balancing security (prompt revocation) and performance.

metadata.notes: FHIR R4 broker round 2: resource_search_params indexed extraction and mTLS partner CA specification closing ISS-001 and ISS-002.

project_goal.value: Build a clinical data warehouse consolidating structured data from 12 hospital EHRs, billing systems, pharmacy platforms, and lab systems into a unified Snowflake analytics store for population health and HEDIS quality reporting
project_goal.rationale: Core business need to unify siloed health system data for analytics

target_users.value: Population health analysts, quality reporting teams, CMS compliance officers, and clinical data engineers across a 12-hospital health system
target_users.rationale: Primary consumers identified in stakeholder discovery

project_class.rationale: Greenfield infrastructure build focused on data platform and ETL pipeline, not application UI

capabilities.rationale: No frontend or auth capabilities needed; pipeline and infrastructure work only

complexity_level.rationale: 12 source systems, OMOP CDM transformation, multi-hospital scale with strict HIPAA requirements

risk_level.rationale: Protected health information from 12 hospital systems; pipeline failures impact CMS reporting deadlines

data_sensitivity.rationale: Clinical encounters, lab results, diagnoses, and medication administrations constitute PHI

external_exposure.rationale: Data warehouse accessible only to internal health system staff via private network

access_model.value: Okta SSO with role-based Snowflake RBAC and column-level security; DATA_STEWARD, ANALYST, ETL_ENGINEER, and ADMIN roles scoped by data domain and sensitivity tier
access_model.rationale: HIPAA minimum necessary principle requires role-scoped access to clinical data

feature_scope.value: Nightly ETL from 12 source systems, OMOP CDM v5.4 transformation, Snowflake data warehouse, HEDIS quality measure computation, population health Tableau dashboards, data quality scoring per batch
feature_scope.rationale: Full scope identified across analytics and compliance workstreams

mvp_scope.value: 3 source systems onboarded (primary EHR, lab system, pharmacy), core OMOP CDM patient and encounter domains, 5 pilot HEDIS measures for CMS dry-run submission
mvp_scope.rationale: Achievable in 6 months to meet upcoming CMS reporting cycle

security_baseline.value: Snowflake encryption at rest and in transit, VPN-only access, Okta SSO, column-level security masking for direct identifiers, no PHI in job logs, all S3 buckets private with SSE-KMS
security_baseline.rationale: HIPAA technical safeguards and health system security policy

frontend_stack.value: No frontend; analytics delivered via Tableau connected to Snowflake ANALYST schema via Partner Connect
frontend_stack.rationale: Infrastructure project; BI tool handles visualization

backend_stack.value: Python 3.12 with Apache Spark 3.5 on EMR Serverless for ETL transformation jobs
backend_stack.rationale: Spark handles high-volume OMOP CDM transformations; EMR Serverless eliminates cluster management overhead

data_platform.value: Snowflake Enterprise multi-cluster warehouse with S3 raw landing zone and Apache Iceberg for staging
data_platform.rationale: Snowflake for governed analytics with column-level security; S3 Iceberg for cost-efficient staging with ACID and time-travel

hosting_target.value: AWS EMR Serverless for Spark jobs, Snowflake hosted, Apache Airflow on MWAA for orchestration, S3 for raw and staging data
hosting_target.rationale: Managed services reduce operational overhead at 12-hospital scale

privacy_retention_policy.value: Raw PHI in S3 retained 7 years per state law; OMOP CDM warehouse data retained 10 years for longitudinal population health analysis; audit logs retained 6 years
privacy_retention_policy.rationale: Healthcare data retention statutes and HIPAA record retention requirements

future_scope.value: Real-time streaming ingestion from FHIR R4 APIs, clinical NLP concept extraction from notes, predictive analytics model outputs published as Snowflake ML functions
future_scope.rationale: Year 2 roadmap after core warehouse stabilization

constraints.value: CMS HEDIS pilot submission within 6 months; all PHI must remain in us-east-1; Snowflake compute budget capped at $15,000/month
constraints.rationale: Regulatory deadline and data residency requirements with budget constraint

observability_baseline.value: Grafana and Prometheus for EMR job metrics, Snowflake Query History API for warehouse observability, GitHub Actions alerting via PagerDuty for ETL failures
observability_baseline.rationale: Health system standard monitoring stack

execution_preference.value: Iterative source-system onboarding with 6-week sprints; each sprint onboards one source system and adds corresponding HEDIS measures
execution_preference.rationale: Reduces integration risk compared to big-bang ETL migration across 12 systems

compliance_context.value: HIPAA and HITECH; CMS Conditions of Participation for quality reporting; NCQA HEDIS audit requirements for complete and reproducible measure calculations
compliance_context.rationale: Health data at scale with mandatory CMS reporting obligations

project.goal: Consolidate clinical data from 12 hospital systems into Snowflake for population health and HEDIS quality reporting
project.target_users: Population health analysts, CMS compliance officers, clinical data engineers
project.timeline: 6-month MVP for CMS pilot submission

backend.etl_pattern: Medallion architecture: S3 raw Parquet -> Iceberg staging -> Snowflake OMOP CDM

security.access_control: Okta SSO federated to Snowflake via SAML 2.0; Snowflake RBAC with column-level masking
security.encryption: Snowflake AES-256 at rest, SSE-KMS for S3, TLS 1.3 in transit
security.compliance: HIPAA, NCQA HEDIS audit trail, no PHI in job logs

data.transformation: OMOP CDM v5.4 target schema via PySpark domain modules
data.quality: Great Expectations 0.18 suites gating staging-to-production promotion

devops.iac: Terraform 1.7 for S3, EMR Serverless, MWAA, Snowflake objects

open_questions.source_connectivity: HL7 v2 feed vs FHIR R4 API per source system TBD per vendor capability

reasoner_reviews.product: OMOP CDM v5.4 is the right canonical target for HEDIS measure computation, providing standardized representations of clinical encounters, diagnoses, and procedures that map directly to NCQA measure specifications. The 6-month MVP scoped to 3 source systems and 5 pilot HEDIS measures is achievable and gives the compliance team enough data for a dry-run CMS submission before full onboarding.
reasoner_reviews.architect_reasoner: Medallion architecture on S3 Iceberg for raw and staging tiers before promotion to Snowflake is sound and provides ACID guarantees on intermediate data. EMR Serverless Spark eliminates cluster provisioning overhead. MWAA Airflow with per-source DAG isolation is critical so a failing HL7 feed from one hospital does not block transformation of data from others.
reasoner_reviews.security: HIPAA minimum necessary principle must be enforced via Snowflake column-level security masking policies on MRN, patient_name, SSN, and DOB fields. Analyst roles must never see direct identifiers. EMR Spark jobs must log job metadata (record count, job ID) but never log actual PHI field values to CloudWatch.
reasoner_reviews.constraints: Snowflake compute budget of $15,000/month is achievable with auto-suspend policies: 60-second timeout on analyst warehouses, 5-minute timeout on ETL warehouses. EMR Serverless is typically 40% cheaper than provisioned EMR for batch workloads, keeping total AWS spend under $1,500/month.
reasoner_reviews.critic: OMOP concept mapping fidelity across 12 heterogeneous source systems is the highest technical risk. A source-to-OMOP mapping registry tracking field-level lineage per source system must be built in Phase 1, and Great Expectations concept_id coverage thresholds (90% minimum) must gate promotion before any HEDIS measure computation.

backend.service_design: PySpark ETL modules organized by OMOP domain: person, visit_occurrence, condition_occurrence, drug_exposure, measurement, procedure_occurrence. Each domain module reads from S3 Iceberg staging, applies source-to-OMOP concept mapping via Athena vocabulary lookup tables, and writes to Snowflake via the Snowflake Spark connector using MERGE semantics.
backend.background_jobs: Airflow DAGs on MWAA: source_extract_{system} DAG per source system nightly at staggered 02:00-04:00 windows; omop_transform DAG runs domain loads in dependency order after all extracts complete; hedis_measure DAG computes HEDIS populations against OMOP_PROD; dq_report DAG runs Great Expectations suites and publishes scores to Grafana.
backend.failure_handling: Airflow DAG failure triggers PagerDuty on-call alert. Failed source extracts retry 3 times with exponential backoff. Partial OMOP domain loads roll back via Snowflake transaction. Iceberg staging tables use ACID properties to prevent dirty reads during parallel source extracts.
backend.backend_risks: Source system HL7 schema changes from vendor upgrades can silently break OMOP field mappings. Mitigation: schema fingerprinting in the extract layer detects new or renamed segments and alerts data engineers before the mapping layer processes them.

security.auth_design: Okta SSO federated to Snowflake via SAML 2.0 for human users. Snowflake ETL service accounts use RSA key-pair authentication with 90-day automated key rotation via AWS Secrets Manager. No password-based authentication on production Snowflake.
security.authorization_model: Snowflake RBAC: ANALYST role (read-only de-identified ANALYST schema, masked direct identifiers), DATA_STEWARD role (read-only identified OMOP_PROD schema), ETL_ENGINEER role (write to OMOP_STAGING only), ADMIN role (schema DDL management). Column-level masking policies on person_source_value, birth_datetime, and location fields restrict ANALYST role to pseudonymous data.
security.audit_and_logging_controls: Snowflake ACCESS_HISTORY view captures every query touching PHI columns. Nightly export job writes structured audit records to S3 audit bucket with 6-year retention. EMR job completion records (job_id, source_system, record_count, duration) logged to CloudWatch without PHI field values.
security.privacy_controls: De-identification applied in ANALYST schema materialized views using Safe Harbor method. ANALYST schema exposes only person_id (pseudonymous internal key), never MRN, name, SSN, or DOB.

data.entities: person (person_id BIGINT PK, gender_concept_id INT, year_of_birth INT, race_concept_id INT, ethnicity_concept_id INT, location_id INT, person_source_value VARCHAR, source_system_id INT FK). visit_occurrence (visit_occurrence_id BIGINT PK, person_id BIGINT FK, visit_concept_id INT, visit_start_date DATE, visit_end_date DATE, care_site_id INT, admitting_source_concept_id INT, discharge_to_concept_id INT). condition_occurrence (condition_occurrence_id BIGINT PK, person_id BIGINT FK, condition_concept_id INT, condition_start_date DATE, visit_occurrence_id BIGINT FK, condition_source_value VARCHAR ICD10). drug_exposure (drug_exposure_id BIGINT PK, person_id BIGINT FK, drug_concept_id INT, drug_exposure_start_date DATE, quantity NUMERIC, days_supply INT, ndc_source_value VARCHAR). measurement (measurement_id BIGINT PK, person_id BIGINT FK, measurement_concept_id INT LOINC, measurement_date DATE, value_as_number NUMERIC, unit_concept_id INT). source_system_feeds (feed_id INT PK, system_name VARCHAR, hospital_site VARCHAR, last_successful_extract TIMESTAMP, record_count BIGINT, dq_score NUMERIC, feed_type ENUM hl7_v2/fhir_r4).
data.storage_design: S3 raw zone: Parquet partitioned by source_system/extraction_date. S3 Iceberg staging: ACID tables per OMOP domain with 30-day time-travel. Snowflake OMOP_PROD schema: production OMOP tables with full retention. Snowflake OMOP_STAGING schema: pre-promotion staging truncated after successful promotion. Snowflake ANALYST schema: de-identified secure views materialized nightly.
data.retention_and_deletion: S3 raw Parquet: lifecycle transition to Glacier after 1 year, delete after 7 years. Snowflake OMOP_PROD: 10-year retention with Time Travel 90 days. Iceberg staging: truncated monthly after promotion confirmed. S3 audit logs: 6-year retention per HIPAA.
data.data_risks: OMOP concept mapping gaps where source codes have no Athena vocabulary match produce unmapped concept_id=0 records that fail HEDIS measure inclusion criteria. Mitigation: monthly concept coverage report with threshold alert at below 90% mappable rate per domain per source.

devops.deployment_topology: Terraform-managed AWS resources: S3 buckets (raw, staging, audit) with SSE-KMS and block public access, EMR Serverless application with auto-scaling 4-64 vCPUs, MWAA small environment, IAM roles with least-privilege per service. Snowflake objects (databases, schemas, roles, warehouses, masking policies) managed via Terraform Snowflake provider.
devops.ci_cd_design: GitHub Actions pipeline: flake8 lint and black format check, pytest unit tests on OMOP mapping functions with 85% coverage gate, Great Expectations sample suite on synthetic data, Spark job artifact packaging and S3 upload, Terraform plan on PR, Terraform apply on merge to main. Pull requests require 1 data engineer approval plus clinical informaticist sign-off on OMOP mapping changes.
devops.observability_stack: Grafana dashboards: EMR Spark job duration per domain (target under 45 minutes), record throughput per source system over 30-day trend, Great Expectations DQ score per domain (alert below 95%), Airflow DAG success rate (alert on any failure), Snowflake warehouse credit burn rate (alert at 80% monthly budget). Prometheus scrapes MWAA metrics via CloudWatch exporter.
devops.rollback_strategy: Snowflake Time Travel (90-day on OMOP_PROD) enables CLONE of pre-bad-run state in under 5 minutes. Iceberg time-travel restores staging tables to last known-good snapshot. Airflow DAG re-run from last successful task for extract replay.

target_output.thinking_summary: Designing a HIPAA-compliant clinical data warehouse for a 12-hospital health system using OMOP CDM v5.4 on Snowflake. Key decisions: medallion architecture on S3 Iceberg for cost-efficient staging with ACID guarantees before Snowflake promotion; EMR Serverless Spark for scalable OMOP domain transformations; Airflow on MWAA with per-source DAG isolation so feed failures are contained; Snowflake Enterprise RBAC with column-level masking to enforce HIPAA minimum-necessary access.
target_output.title: Clinical Data Warehouse Infrastructure for 12-Hospital Health System
target_output.executive_summary: A HIPAA-compliant clinical data warehouse on AWS and Snowflake consolidating structured clinical data from 12 hospital EHRs, lab systems, pharmacy platforms, and billing systems into an OMOP CDM v5.4 schema. Nightly PySpark ETL on EMR Serverless applies source-to-OMOP concept mappings, enforces Great Expectations data quality gates, and loads validated records to Snowflake for HEDIS quality measure computation and population health analytics via Tableau.
target_output.architecture_overview: Three-tier medallion architecture: S3 raw landing zone stores Parquet files per source system per extraction date; S3 Apache Iceberg staging tier provides ACID transactions and 30-day time-travel for rollback; Snowflake OMOP_PROD schema serves as the governed production analytics hub. PySpark jobs on EMR Serverless handle source-to-OMOP CDM transformation per domain (person, visit_occurrence, condition_occurrence, drug_exposure, measurement). Apache Airflow 2.8 on MWAA orchestrates per-source extraction DAGs independently, so a failed HL7 feed from one hospital does not cascade to other source DAGs. Great Expectations suites gate every staging-to-production promotion. Tableau Server connects to the Snowflake ANALYST schema (de-identified views) for population health dashboards and HEDIS reporting.
target_output.technology_stack: ETL: Python 3.12 with Apache Spark 3.5 on EMR Serverless (4-64 vCPU auto-scaling). Orchestration: Apache Airflow 2.8 on AWS MWAA. Warehouse: Snowflake Enterprise multi-cluster (column-level security required). Raw/Staging: S3 with Apache Iceberg 1.5 via PyIceberg. Data Quality: Great Expectations 0.18. IaC: Terraform 1.7 with Snowflake provider. CI/CD: GitHub Actions. Analytics: Tableau Server via Snowflake JDBC Partner Connect. Auth: Okta SAML 2.0 federated to Snowflake. Monitoring: Grafana + Prometheus with CloudWatch exporter.
target_output.functional_feature_map: Source Extraction Connectors: configurable HL7 v2 feed parsers and FHIR R4 API clients per hospital system, each writing Parquet to the S3 raw zone. OMOP CDM Transformation: domain-specific PySpark modules mapping source codes to standard OMOP concept IDs via OHDSI Athena vocabulary lookup. Data Quality Gate: Great Expectations suites per domain enforce concept_id coverage above 90%, null constraints on person_id and visit_occurrence_id, and record volume thresholds before staging-to-OMOP_PROD promotion. HEDIS Measure Engine: dbt models on Snowflake computing numerator/denominator populations per measure per payer cohort per hospital. Population Health Dashboards: Tableau workbooks connected to de-identified ANALYST schema for cohort analysis, chronic disease prevalence, and care gap reporting.
target_output.system_components: S3 Raw Zone: Parquet files partitioned by source_system and extraction_date, SSE-KMS encrypted, versioning enabled. S3 Iceberg Staging: one Iceberg table per OMOP domain per source, ACID, 30-day time-travel. EMR Serverless Application: auto-scaling Spark executor pool for nightly domain transformation jobs. MWAA Airflow: S3-backed DAG repository, 3-worker environment, per-source DAG isolation. Snowflake: OMOP_PROD schema (production), OMOP_STAGING schema (pre-promotion), ANALYST schema (de-identified secure views), AUDIT schema (access log exports). Tableau Server: ANALYST schema access via service account with no PHI access. Great Expectations: validation suites stored in S3, results surfaced in Grafana.
target_output.workflows: Nightly ETL Cycle: Airflow source_extract DAGs trigger per hospital at staggered 02:00-04:00 local windows. HL7 feed parser or FHIR API client extracts records since watermark timestamp, writes Parquet to S3 raw zone. EMR Spark job reads raw Parquet, applies source-to-OMOP concept mapping for each domain, validates with Great Expectations (90%+ concept_id match rate, no null person_id), writes to Iceberg staging. The omop_transform DAG waits for all source extracts to complete, then runs domain promotion MERGE jobs into Snowflake OMOP_PROD in dependency order (person -> visit_occurrence -> condition_occurrence -> drug_exposure -> measurement). HEDIS Measure Computation: After OMOP_PROD promotion is confirmed, the hedis_measure DAG triggers. dbt models compute HEDIS measure numerators and denominators (HbA1c control for DM, blood pressure for HTN, statin therapy, cancer screenings) against OMOP_PROD. Results written to HEDIS_RESULTS schema. Tableau extracts refresh at 07:00 for analyst access.
target_output.data_model: person (person_id BIGINT PK, gender_concept_id INT, year_of_birth INT, race_concept_id INT, ethnicity_concept_id INT, location_id INT FK, person_source_value VARCHAR, source_system_id INT FK). visit_occurrence (visit_occurrence_id BIGINT PK, person_id BIGINT FK, visit_concept_id INT, visit_start_date DATE, visit_end_date DATE, care_site_id INT FK, admitting_source_concept_id INT, discharge_to_concept_id INT). condition_occurrence (condition_occurrence_id BIGINT PK, person_id BIGINT FK, condition_concept_id INT SNOMED, condition_start_date DATE, visit_occurrence_id BIGINT FK, condition_source_value VARCHAR ICD10). drug_exposure (drug_exposure_id BIGINT PK, person_id BIGINT FK, drug_concept_id INT RxNorm, drug_exposure_start_date DATE, quantity NUMERIC, days_supply INT, ndc_source_value VARCHAR). measurement (measurement_id BIGINT PK, person_id BIGINT FK, measurement_concept_id INT LOINC, measurement_date DATE, value_as_number NUMERIC, unit_concept_id INT). source_system_feeds (feed_id INT PK, system_name VARCHAR, hospital_site VARCHAR, last_successful_extract TIMESTAMP, record_count BIGINT, dq_score NUMERIC(4,2), feed_type VARCHAR). OMOP clinical domain tables: condition_occurrence (condition_occurrence_id BIGINT PK, person_id BIGINT FK, condition_concept_id INT, condition_start_date DATE, visit_occurrence_id BIGINT FK, condition_type_concept_id INT), drug_exposure (drug_exposure_id BIGINT PK, person_id BIGINT FK, drug_concept_id INT, drug_exposure_start_date DATE, quantity NUMERIC, days_supply INT, prescriber_id BIGINT), measurement (measurement_id BIGINT PK, person_id BIGINT FK, measurement_concept_id INT, measurement_date DATE, value_as_number NUMERIC, unit_concept_id INT — includes lab_result and vitals domains), visit_occurrence (visit_occurrence_id BIGINT PK, person_id BIGINT FK, visit_concept_id INT, visit_start_date DATE, visit_end_date DATE, care_site_id INT).
target_output.api_design: No external REST API; internal orchestration interfaces only. Airflow REST API (MWAA-local): POST /api/v1/dags/{dag_id}/dagRuns to trigger manual reruns, GET /api/v1/dags/{dag_id}/dagRuns/{run_id} for status. Snowflake access: analysts via JDBC/ODBC with Okta SSO token; ETL service accounts via RSA key-pair. Great Expectations CLI: great_expectations checkpoint run {suite} for manual validation. Data engineers access S3 raw zone via presigned URLs for debugging, expiring after 1 hour.
target_output.security_and_compliance: HIPAA technical safeguards: Snowflake AES-256 encryption at rest and TLS 1.3 in transit. Okta SAML 2.0 SSO; no password auth on Snowflake. Column-level masking policies on person_source_value (MRN), birth_datetime, location fields visible to DATA_STEWARD only; ANALYST role sees masked nulls. Row-level security policies on care_site_id restrict hospital staff to their institution's records. EMR Spark jobs log only metadata (record count, job ID, source system) never PHI field values. Snowflake ACCESS_HISTORY nightly export to S3 audit bucket with 6-year retention. VPN required for all Snowflake and AWS console access. Annual HIPAA security risk assessment covers ETL pipeline and warehouse access controls.
target_output.deployment_and_operations: Terraform manages all AWS infrastructure: S3 buckets with SSE-KMS encryption and block-public-access policies, EMR Serverless application with IAM execution role, MWAA environment with S3-backed DAG bucket, IAM roles scoped per service component. Snowflake objects (databases, schemas, roles, virtual warehouses, masking policies) managed via Terraform Snowflake provider. GitHub Actions CI pipeline: flake8 lint, black format check, pytest unit tests on OMOP mapping functions (85% coverage gate), Great Expectations sample suite on synthetic data, Spark artifact packaging and S3 upload, Terraform plan on PR, Terraform apply on merge to main. Environments: dev (separate Snowflake dev database, EMR Serverless test application with 1/10th data volume), staging (production mirror with anonymized data for full pipeline testing), production. Rollback: Snowflake Time Travel CLONE restores OMOP_PROD to pre-bad-run state; Iceberg time-travel restores staging snapshots.
target_output.observability: Grafana dashboards: EMR Spark job duration per domain (SLA: under 45 minutes), record throughput per source system trending over 30 days, Great Expectations DQ score per domain per run (alert threshold below 95%), Airflow DAG success rate (alert on any failure), Snowflake credit burn rate (alert at 80% of $15,000 monthly budget). Prometheus scrapes MWAA metrics via CloudWatch exporter. PagerDuty on-call for: any ETL DAG failure, DQ score below 95%, Snowflake compute exceeding $12,000/month. Data freshness SLO: OMOP_PROD updated before 08:00 local time each day.
target_output.cost_and_scaling: Snowflake auto-suspend: analyst warehouses at 60 seconds, ETL warehouse at 5 minutes. Result cache eliminates repeat HEDIS dashboard query compute. MVP (3 sources): estimated $11,000/month Snowflake, $800/month EMR Serverless, $300/month MWAA, $200/month S3 storage, total $12,300/month well within $15,000 cap. Full 12-source scale projected at $13,800/month. EMR Serverless scales to 64 vCPUs per domain job on peak months without manual capacity planning.
target_output.phased_implementation: Phase 1 MVP (6 months): Terraform IaC for all AWS and Snowflake infrastructure. Onboard 3 source systems (primary EHR via HL7 v2, lab system via FHIR R4, pharmacy via HL7 v2). Implement OMOP person, visit_occurrence, condition_occurrence, drug_exposure, measurement domain pipelines. Deploy Great Expectations suites for all 5 OMOP domains. Compute 5 pilot HEDIS measures (HbA1c control, blood pressure control, statin therapy, breast cancer screening, colorectal cancer screening). CMS dry-run submission. Phase 2 (months 7-12): Onboard remaining 9 source systems in 6-week sprints. Expand OMOP to observation, device_exposure, death domains. Add 15 additional HEDIS measures. Launch population health Tableau dashboards per hospital site. Phase 3 (Year 2): Streaming FHIR ingestion for near-real-time OMOP updates. Clinical NLP concept extraction pipeline. Predictive model outputs as Snowflake ML functions.
target_output.development_guidelines: Python: black formatter, flake8 lint, mypy strict type checking for all ETL modules. pytest with 85% coverage on OMOP mapping functions; no coverage requirement on Airflow DAG definitions (tested via staging pipeline runs). Spark jobs run in isolated EMR Serverless job submissions, never sharing application context between source systems. OMOP concept mapping changes require review from a clinical informaticist plus a senior data engineer. Terraform production changes require 2 approvals. No hardcoded connection strings; all credentials via AWS Secrets Manager. ADR documents for every mapping decision affecting more than one source system.
target_output.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: Source system HL7 schema changes from hospital vendor upgrades silently break OMOP field mappings. Mitigation: schema fingerprinting in extract layer with automated alert on unrecognized segments before mapping is attempted. Risk: OMOP concept mapping gaps (unmapped concept_id=0 records) cause HEDIS measure numerator undercounting, potentially triggering CMS audit findings. Mitigation: monthly concept coverage reports with 90% threshold gate before production promotion. Tradeoff: Snowflake Enterprise over open-source warehouse (DuckDB, Trino) for column-level security and RBAC at the cost of higher monthly spend. Tradeoff: EMR Serverless Spark over AWS Glue for full PySpark compatibility, accepting slightly longer cold-start times on first daily job submission.
target_output.open_questions_resolved: Selected OMOP CDM v5.4 over FHIR as internal canonical model for HEDIS measure alignment with OHDSI community tools. Confirmed Snowflake Enterprise edition for column-level security (Standard edition does not support this). Chose MWAA over self-managed Airflow on EKS for managed upgrade cycle. Decided per-source HL7 parsers over universal middleware to avoid single-point-of-failure in the extraction layer across 12 independent hospital systems.

metadata.notes: HIPAA-compliant clinical data warehouse, OMOP CDM v5.4, 12-hospital scale on Snowflake and EMR Serverless.

project_goal.value: Replace costly vendor OR scheduling software with an in-house web application for surgical case scheduling and preference card management that surfaces supply utilization data to reduce per-case costs
project_goal.rationale: Core cost reduction objective replacing $280,000/year vendor license

target_users.value: OR scheduling coordinators, surgeons, scrub technicians, OR managers, and supply chain staff
target_users.rationale: All OR stakeholders identified in workflow discovery sessions

project_class.rationale: Browser-based scheduling tool with server-side data; no native mobile requirement

capabilities.value: frontend, backend, data, auth, analytics, integrations
capabilities.rationale: Scheduling UI, case API, analytics for utilization reporting, EHR integration for case data

complexity_level.rationale: Standard CRUD with scheduling logic, preference card management, and utilization analytics

risk_level.rationale: Contains patient-linked scheduling data; OR downtime from system failure has patient safety implications

data_sensitivity.rationale: Patient names, MRNs, and procedure types linked to surgical case records constitute PHI

external_exposure.rationale: Accessible only within hospital network via Azure Active Directory authentication

access_model.value: Azure AD SSO with RBAC: Coordinator role (schedule management), Surgeon role (own case and preference card view/edit), Scrub Tech role (supply logging), OR Manager role (all OR analytics), Admin role (configuration)
access_model.rationale: Roles match OR department organizational structure

feature_scope.value: Surgical case request and scheduling, OR block schedule management, surgeon preference card CRUD, post-case supply utilization logging, variance analytics vs preference card, EHR patient and procedure data integration
feature_scope.rationale: All features required to replace vendor tool

mvp_scope.value: Case request and scheduling with OR block management, preference card management, basic supply utilization logging, read-only EHR patient lookup
mvp_scope.rationale: Core scheduling and preference card workflow must be live before decommissioning vendor tool

security_baseline.value: Azure AD SSO with MFA, TLS 1.3 in transit, Azure SQL encryption at rest, no PHI in application logs, 15-minute session timeout
security_baseline.rationale: HIPAA technical safeguards and hospital security policy

frontend_stack.value: Next.js 15 with TypeScript
frontend_stack.rationale: Hospital engineering team expertise in Next.js; SSR for fast initial load of schedule views

backend_stack.value: Python 3.12 with FastAPI
backend_stack.rationale: FastAPI for type-safe API development; team familiar with Python for analytics integration

data_platform.value: PostgreSQL 16 on Azure Database for PostgreSQL Flexible Server with Redis 7 for session caching
data_platform.rationale: Managed PostgreSQL for relational scheduling data; Redis for auth session tokens

hosting_target.value: Azure App Service with Azure Database for PostgreSQL and Azure Cache for Redis
hosting_target.rationale: Consistent with hospital Azure-first cloud strategy

privacy_retention_policy.value: Patient-linked case records retained 7 years per state law; preference cards retained indefinitely as operational data; audit logs retained 3 years
privacy_retention_policy.rationale: Healthcare records retention law and OR operational requirements

future_scope.value: Real-time OR turnover time tracking via barcode scan integration, predictive demand forecasting for block schedule optimization, mobile app for surgeon preference card updates
future_scope.rationale: Phase 2 roadmap after vendor decommission

constraints.value: Must go live before vendor contract renewal in 9 months; integrate with existing Epic EHR for patient and case data; budget under $8,000/month Azure hosting
constraints.rationale: Hard vendor deadline and EHR integration requirement

observability_baseline.value: Azure Monitor for App Service and PostgreSQL metrics, structured application logging to Azure Log Analytics, PagerDuty for critical scheduling API failures
observability_baseline.rationale: Hospital Azure Monitor standard

execution_preference.value: Agile 2-week sprints with weekly OR team demos; feature flags for gradual rollout alongside vendor tool
execution_preference.rationale: Parallel operation with vendor tool required for safe cutover

compliance_context.value: HIPAA for patient-linked OR scheduling data; Joint Commission OR management standards for surgical case documentation; hospital BAA with Azure
compliance_context.rationale: Patient data in OR scheduling and Joint Commission audit requirements

project.goal: Replace vendor OR scheduling and preference card tool with in-house system
project.target_users: OR coordinators, surgeons, scrub techs, OR managers
project.timeline: 9-month hard deadline before vendor contract renewal

frontend.state: React Query for server state, Zustand for OR calendar local state

backend.api: RESTful with OpenAPI 3.0

security.auth: Azure AD SSO via MSAL

constraints.integration: Epic EHR patient and case data via Epic FHIR R4 API

open_questions.block_schedule_conflict: Conflict resolution rules for overlapping surgeon block time requests pending OR committee input

reasoner_reviews.product: The preference card variance analytics is the highest-value differentiator from the vendor tool. Surfacing per-surgeon and per-procedure supply utilization versus preference card quantities weekly gives OR managers actionable data for contract renegotiation. The 9-month deadline is tight but achievable if EHR integration scope is limited to read-only patient and case lookup in MVP.
reasoner_reviews.architect_reasoner: Next.js 15 with SSR is appropriate for the OR block schedule calendar view, which benefits from server-rendered initial state. FastAPI with SQLAlchemy 2.0 async is a good fit for the scheduling API load. The most complex schema element is the preference card with version history and per-case override tracking. Recommend a separate supply_utilization_records table per case to support variance computations without mutating the canonical preference card.
reasoner_reviews.security: HIPAA requires that patient identifiers (MRN, name, DOB) in surgical case records be excluded from application logs. Azure AD MFA must be enforced at the conditional access policy level, not just at application level. Preference card data linked to a surgeon but not a patient is not PHI; patient-linked case records are. The distinction must be enforced in the data model and access logs.
reasoner_reviews.constraints: Azure App Service P2v3 at $280/month handles the expected concurrent load of 50-100 OR staff. PostgreSQL Flexible Server General Purpose 4 vCores at $350/month is adequate. Redis Cache C1 at $55/month for sessions. Total Azure under $1,000/month for MVP. Budget headroom allows for staging environment parity without concern.

backend.service_design: FastAPI application with domain routers: /cases (surgical case CRUD and scheduling), /preference-cards (surgeon preference card versioning), /supply-utilization (post-case logging), /analytics (variance reports), /ehr (Epic FHIR R4 proxy for patient and case lookup).
backend.api_patterns: RESTful with OpenAPI 3.0 auto-generated docs. Async SQLAlchemy 2.0 with connection pool. Idempotent POST for case creation (idempotency key per coordinator session). PATCH for preference card updates creating a new version record.
backend.business_modules: SchedulingEngine: validates OR room availability, surgeon block time, and instrument tray availability before confirming a case. PreferenceCardService: manages versioned preference card records and computes per-case overrides. UtilizationService: accepts post-case supply logs, computes variance vs current preference card version, triggers weekly analytics refresh.
backend.backend_risks: Epic FHIR R4 API rate limits may throttle patient lookups during peak morning case scheduling. Mitigation: cache Epic patient search results in Redis with 5-minute TTL for active session.

frontend.app_structure: Next.js 15 App Router with SSR for the OR block schedule calendar (week/day views). Client components for real-time preference card editing and supply logging forms. Middleware for Azure AD MSAL token validation.
frontend.pages_and_flows: OR Schedule Calendar (week/month block view with drag-and-drop case placement), Case Request Form (surgeon/procedure/room/date with Epic patient lookup), Preference Card Editor (supply item list with quantity and substitution fields), Post-Case Supply Logger (scrub tech checklist of actuals vs preference card), Analytics Dashboard (variance heatmap per surgeon per procedure, weekly trend charts).
frontend.state_management: React Query for scheduling data, preference card versions, and analytics. Zustand for OR calendar view state (selected date, room filter). MSAL React for Azure AD token management.
frontend.frontend_risks: OR schedule calendar drag-and-drop requires optimistic updates with rollback on server-side conflict detection. Implement conflict indicator overlay before confirming a drop action.

security.auth_design: Azure AD MSAL for SSO. Next.js middleware validates JWT access tokens from Azure AD on every API route. FastAPI depends on JWT validation via python-jose. No local password storage.
security.authorization_model: RBAC enforced in FastAPI route dependencies: COORDINATOR and OR_MANAGER roles may create and modify cases; SURGEON role may only view/edit own preference cards; SCRUB_TECH role may only submit post-case supply logs; ADMIN manages reference data (rooms, instrument trays, vendors).
security.audit_and_logging_controls: All writes to surgical_cases and supply_utilization_records log user_id, action, timestamp, and resource_id to an audit_log table. Patient identifiers excluded from structured application logs. Azure Log Analytics receives structured JSON logs with correlation IDs.

data.entities: surgical_cases (id UUID PK, patient_mrn_encrypted VARCHAR, procedure_code CPT VARCHAR, surgeon_id UUID FK, or_room_id UUID FK, scheduled_start TIMESTAMP, scheduled_duration_minutes INT, status ENUM requested/scheduled/in_progress/completed/cancelled, created_by UUID). preference_cards (id UUID PK, surgeon_id UUID FK, procedure_code VARCHAR, version INT, effective_from DATE, is_current BOOLEAN). preference_card_items (id UUID PK, card_id UUID FK, supply_item_id UUID FK, quantity INT, unit VARCHAR, substitution_allowed BOOLEAN). supply_utilization_records (id UUID PK, case_id UUID FK, card_id UUID FK, supply_item_id UUID FK, planned_qty INT, actual_qty INT, variance INT, logged_by UUID, logged_at TIMESTAMP). or_rooms (id UUID PK, room_name VARCHAR, room_type ENUM main/endo/hybrid, active BOOLEAN). vendors (id UUID PK, vendor_name VARCHAR, contract_number VARCHAR).
data.analytics_events: Weekly utilization variance report aggregated per surgeon per procedure code per supply item. Supply waste rate (variance/planned_qty) trending over rolling 12 weeks. Top 10 preference card items by absolute variance value surfaced to OR managers.

devops.deployment_topology: Azure App Service P2v3 (Next.js and FastAPI via Docker Compose), Azure Database for PostgreSQL Flexible Server (4 vCores, zone-redundant HA), Azure Cache for Redis C1, Azure Container Registry for images, Application Insights for APM.
devops.ci_cd_design: GitHub Actions: pytest with 80% coverage gate, ESLint, Next.js build check, Docker image build and push to ACR, Azure App Service deployment slot swap (staging to production). Feature flags via environment variables for gradual OR department rollout.
devops.observability_stack: Azure Monitor for App Service CPU/memory/request latency. Azure Database Insights for PostgreSQL slow queries and connection counts. PagerDuty for: scheduling API error rate above 2%, PostgreSQL connection saturation above 80%, Redis eviction events.
devops.rollback_strategy: Azure App Service deployment slots enable instant slot swap rollback. PostgreSQL point-in-time restore with 7-day backup retention. Database schema migrations via Alembic with down-migration scripts tested in staging.

ISS-001.title: Preference card version history not included in Round 1 data model
ISS-001.detail: Round 1 plan defined preference_cards table without version tracking columns. OR managers require the ability to see what the preference card looked like on the date of any historical case for accurate variance attribution.

history.note: Missing version tracking identified in auditor review

ISS-002.title: Epic FHIR integration latency risk not addressed
ISS-002.detail: Round 1 plan mentioned Epic FHIR R4 integration but did not address API rate limits or caching strategy. During peak morning scheduling (07:00-09:00), coordinators may generate 200+ patient lookup requests, risking Epic FHIR throttling.

history.note: Rate limit risk flagged by architect reviewer

focus_issues.title: Preference card version history not included in Round 1 data model
focus_issues.detail: Round 1 plan defined preference_cards table without version tracking columns. OR managers require the ability to see what the preference card looked like on the date of any historical case for accurate variance attribution.

latest_recommendations: Add version history to preference_cards table so historical case variance can be attributed to the correct card version
Define Epic FHIR R4 caching strategy with Redis TTL to handle peak morning scheduling load without throttling
