Strangler Fig migration. NHSN CDA document generation not specified. HL7 routing during parallel operation undefined.
previous_plan.system_components: lab_ingest_service (Fargate), surveillance_service (Fargate), nhsn_submission_service (Fargate). Per-service PostgreSQL on RDS. Redis for HL7 dedup. Cognito MFA. No CDA generation spec. No HL7 routing toggle.
previous_plan.data_model: infection_events, organism_isolates, nhsn_submissions tables defined without full field list. surveillance_criteria and antibiogram_data tables missing.
previous_plan.workflows: Lab feed ingest to HAI candidate creation described. IP classification workflow outlined. NHSN submission triggered on classification approval but CDA generation process not described.

target_output.thinking_summary: Round 2 revision addressing both high-severity issues. For ISS-001: specifying nhsn_submission_service CDA generation using HL7 CDA R2 per NHSN Protocol v11, CDC NHSN REST API details, 3-retry exponential backoff, S3 archival of each CDA document, and dead-letter handling. For ISS-002: defining HL7 feed routing via an AWS Parameter Store feature flag per message type (ORM/ORU), implemented in the lab system HL7 router configuration to direct traffic exclusively to either the legacy system or the new lab_ingest_service, preventing dual HAI candidate creation during the Strangler Fig migration.

fix_report.action_taken: Fully specified nhsn_submission_service: CDA R2 document structure per NHSN Protocol v11 for CAUTI and CLABSI event types, CDC NHSN REST API endpoint and Bearer token auth via CDC facility credentials in Secrets Manager, 3-retry exponential backoff with dead-letter S3 bucket for failed CDA documents, success archival of every submitted CDA to S3 with CDC confirmation number, PagerDuty alert on retry exhaustion.
fix_report.expected_outcome: nhsn_submission_service fully implementable; NHSN CDA submission restored for CAUTI and CLABSI within 6-month deadline.

fix_report.action_taken: Defined HL7 feed routing toggle via AWS Parameter Store feature flag (/hai-surveillance/hl7-routing/{message_type}) with values 'legacy' or 'new_service'. Lab system HL7 router (Mirth Connect) reads flag on each message type before routing. During migration: ORU^R01 culture results routed to new lab_ingest_service; ORM^O01 orders remain on legacy until lab_ingest_service validated. Flag toggled per message type after 30-day parallel validation.
fix_report.expected_outcome: HL7 messages routed exclusively to either legacy or new service per message type, preventing dual HAI candidate creation during the Strangler Fig migration period.

target_output.title: HAI Surveillance Microservices Decomposition
target_output.executive_summary: A HIPAA-compliant microservices decomposition of a legacy HAI surveillance monolith, implemented via Strangler Fig pattern on AWS ECS Fargate with Go 1.22 and React 18. Three bounded-context services handle HL7 lab feed ingest with NHSN case definition evaluation, infection preventionist HAI classification workflow, and NHSN CDA v11 document generation with CDC API submission. HL7 feed routing via AWS Parameter Store feature flags ensures exclusive routing to either legacy or new services during parallel migration, preventing duplicate HAI candidate events.
target_output.architecture_overview: Three Go microservices on ECS Fargate behind internal ALBs: lab_ingest_service ingests HL7 v2 ORM/ORU messages from the lab system via Mirth Connect HL7 router, evaluates NHSN CAUTI and CLABSI case definitions against organism and device duration data, and creates HAI candidate events in surveillance_service via gRPC. surveillance_service manages the IP case review queue, classification workflow, and antibiogram data. nhsn_submission_service generates HL7 CDA R2 documents per NHSN Protocol v11 for each approved HAI event, submits to CDC NHSN REST API, and archives every CDA document to S3. Strangler Fig migration: Mirth Connect HL7 router reads AWS Parameter Store feature flags per message type to route exclusively to either legacy monolith or new lab_ingest_service, preventing dual processing. React 18 SPA communicates with surveillance_service REST API via ALB. Cognito MFA authenticates all infection control staff.
target_output.technology_stack: Backend: Go 1.22 with Gin (REST), gRPC with protobuf (internal service calls). Frontend: React 18, TypeScript, Tailwind CSS, React Query, Zustand. Auth: AWS Cognito (MFA TOTP required). Database: PostgreSQL 16 on RDS per service. Cache: ElastiCache Redis 7 for HL7 deduplication and session tokens. HL7 Router: Mirth Connect (legacy, reading Parameter Store flags). NHSN: HL7 CDA R2 per NHSN Protocol v11, CDC NHSN REST API. Infrastructure: ECS Fargate, ALB (internal), S3 (CDA archive, frontend assets), CloudFront. CI/CD: GitHub Actions. Monitoring: Datadog APM. Secrets: AWS Secrets Manager.
target_output.functional_feature_map: HL7 Lab Ingest and Case Definition Evaluation: lab_ingest_service parses ORU^R01 culture results from Mirth Connect, applies Redis deduplication by HL7 message control ID, evaluates NHSN CAUTI criteria (positive urine culture with matching UTI criteria and indwelling catheter duration over 2 days) and CLABSI criteria (positive blood culture with central line duration over 2 days), creates candidate infection_event records in surveillance_service. IP Case Review Queue: paginated React table of HAI candidates with organism, unit, device type, and auto-evaluated criteria status. IP classifies each candidate as confirmed HAI or excluded with coded reason. NHSN CDA Submission: approved events trigger nhsn_submission_service; CDA R2 document generated per NHSN v11 schema, submitted to CDC API, S3-archived with CDC confirmation number. SIR Computation: monthly batch computes Standardized Infection Ratio per device type per unit against NHSN baseline denominator data.
target_output.system_components: lab_ingest_service (Fargate): Gin REST API for Mirth Connect HL7 HTTP post receiver, Redis HL7 dedup cache, NHSN case definition evaluation engine, gRPC client to surveillance_service. surveillance_service (Fargate): Gin REST API for React frontend, HAI event queue and classification engine, SIR computation batch job, gRPC server receiving lab ingest events. nhsn_submission_service (Fargate): NHSN Protocol v11 CDA R2 document builder, CDC NHSN REST API client with Bearer token auth (credentials from Secrets Manager), retry queue in PostgreSQL (3 retries, exponential backoff), S3 CDA archive (submitted and dead-letter buckets). Mirth Connect HL7 Router: reads /hai-surveillance/hl7-routing/{type} from Parameter Store; routes ORU^R01 to lab_ingest_service, ORM^O01 to legacy during MVP. RDS PostgreSQL per service in private subnet. ElastiCache Redis C2. Cognito User Pool. Datadog Agent sidecar on each Fargate task.
target_output.workflows: HL7 Lab Culture Result to HAI Candidate: Lab system LIS sends ORU^R01 message to Mirth Connect. Mirth reads Parameter Store flag /hai-surveillance/hl7-routing/ORU_R01; if 'new_service', POSTs to lab_ingest_service HTTP endpoint. lab_ingest_service extracts HL7 message control ID, checks Redis dedup cache (24-hour TTL). If duplicate, returns 200 without processing. If new, parses organism (OBX segment), accession number, patient MRN, ordering unit. Queries surveillance_service gRPC for device duration data. Evaluates CAUTI or CLABSI NHSN criteria. If criteria met, calls surveillance_service gRPC CreateCandidateEvent. HAI Classification: IP opens HAI Case Queue in React app, sees candidate with criteria evaluation summary. Opens case detail to view culture organism, sensitivity profile, device duration, and NHSN criteria checkbox evaluation. IP checks criteria and selects Confirm HAI or Exclude with reason code. On Confirm, surveillance_service sets event status=confirmed, calls nhsn_submission_service gRPC QueueForSubmission. NHSN Monthly Submission: At month-end, epidemiologist triggers NHSN submission from the React dashboard. nhsn_submission_service aggregates all confirmed events for the reporting period, generates CDA R2 document per event type (CAUTI CDA, CLABSI CDA) per NHSN Protocol v11 schema. Posts to CDC NHSN REST API with Bearer token from Secrets Manager. On 200 success: stores CDC confirmation number, archives CDA to S3 submitted/ prefix, marks submission status=accepted. On failure: retries with 30s/60s/120s backoff. After 3 failures: moves CDA to S3 dead-letter/ prefix, triggers PagerDuty alert to on-call epidemiologist.
target_output.data_model: surveillance_service DB: infection_events (id UUID PK, patient_id UUID, clinical_unit_id UUID FK, device_type ENUM foley_catheter/central_line, event_type ENUM cauti/clabsi, onset_date DATE, nhsn_criteria_met JSONB, status ENUM candidate/confirmed/excluded, exclusion_reason_code VARCHAR, classified_by UUID FK, classified_at TIMESTAMP, created_at TIMESTAMP). organism_isolates (id UUID PK, event_id UUID FK REFERENCES infection_events, organism_name VARCHAR, organism_snomed_code VARCHAR, antibiotic_resistance_profile JSONB, culture_date DATE, lab_accession_id VARCHAR). surveillance_criteria (id UUID PK, event_type VARCHAR, nhsn_protocol_version VARCHAR, criteria_definition JSONB, effective_from DATE, effective_to DATE). nhsn_submission_service DB: nhsn_submissions (id UUID PK, reporting_period DATE, event_type VARCHAR, cda_document_s3_key VARCHAR, submission_status ENUM pending/submitted/accepted/rejected/dead_letter, cdc_confirmation_number VARCHAR, retry_count INT, submitted_at TIMESTAMP, submitted_by UUID FK). lab_ingest_service DB: hl7_messages (id UUID PK, message_control_id VARCHAR UNIQUE, message_type VARCHAR, lab_accession_id VARCHAR, received_at TIMESTAMP, processed BOOLEAN).
target_output.api_design: surveillance_service REST (internal ALB): GET /api/v1/events?status=candidate&unit_id=&page= (IP case queue), GET /api/v1/events/{id} (case detail with organism and criteria), PATCH /api/v1/events/{id}/classify (confirm or exclude with reason), GET /api/v1/submissions?period=2026-02 (NHSN submission history), POST /api/v1/submissions/trigger (epidemiologist triggers monthly submission). All routes require Cognito JWT; role checked per route. Internal gRPC: lab_ingest_service calls surveillance_service.CreateCandidateEvent(event_proto); surveillance_service calls nhsn_submission_service.QueueForSubmission(event_id) on confirmation. No external-facing API; NHSN submission is outbound to CDC only.
target_output.security_and_compliance: HIPAA: Cognito MFA (TOTP) required for all users. TLS 1.3 on all ALB endpoints. PostgreSQL RDS encryption at rest. No PHI (patient MRN, name) in Datadog log payloads; correlation via event UUID only. CDC NHSN API Bearer token stored in Secrets Manager with 90-day rotation; never in environment variables. Audit_events table records every IP classification decision with user_id, event_id, action, and timestamp for NHSN audit requirements. CDA documents archived in S3 with S3 Object Lock (WORM, 10-year retention) to satisfy CMS Conditions of Participation immutability requirements for NHSN submissions.
target_output.deployment_and_operations: GitHub Actions per service: go test (80% coverage), golangci-lint, Docker build and ECR push, ECS rolling update (minimum 50% healthy during deploy). Environments: dev (ECS on spot Fargate, RDS t3.medium), staging (production-parity, full HL7 routing toggle test), production (ECS Fargate on-demand, RDS db.r6g.large per service, Multi-AZ). Strangler Fig toggle: AWS Parameter Store /hai-surveillance/hl7-routing/ORU_R01 set to 'new_service' after 30-day production validation of lab_ingest_service; ORM^O01 toggled after surveillance workflow validated; legacy monolith decommissioned after all toggles set. Terraform: ECS clusters, RDS per service, Redis, ALBs, Cognito, S3 buckets with Object Lock, Secrets Manager entries. CDC NHSN sandbox environment used in staging for submission testing.
target_output.observability: Datadog APM: distributed traces across lab_ingest->surveillance->nhsn_submission gRPC calls. Custom metrics: HL7 message ingestion rate (per message type), HAI candidate creation rate (by unit), IP classification throughput (candidates classified per day), NHSN submission success rate, CDA generation duration, SIR values per unit per month. PagerDuty alerts: NHSN submission retry exhaustion, HL7 feed gap over 2 hours, lab_ingest_service error rate above 1%, Cognito auth failure spike above 10 failures in 5 minutes.
target_output.cost_and_scaling: ECS Fargate: 3 services at 0.5 vCPU/1 GB each: ~$150/month total. RDS PostgreSQL t3.medium per service (3 instances): ~$300/month. ElastiCache Redis t3.micro: $25/month. S3 CDA archive: negligible. Total: ~$500/month, well within hospital budget. ECS auto-scaling on CPU 70% target if HAI classification queue backlog exceeds 200 candidates. RDS read replicas on-demand for SIR analytics queries if primary CPU exceeds 60%.
target_output.phased_implementation: Phase 1 NHSN Restoration (6 months): Extract lab_ingest_service: HL7 ORU^R01 parsing, Redis dedup, CAUTI/CLABSI case definition evaluation. Extract surveillance_service: candidate event storage, IP classification workflow, Cognito RBAC. Extract nhsn_submission_service: NHSN Protocol v11 CDA generation for CAUTI and CLABSI, CDC NHSN API submission with retry and S3 archive. Mirth Connect routing flag for ORU^R01 (new_service) and ORM^O01 (legacy). 30-day parallel validation: verify new service HAI candidate counts match legacy. Toggle ORM^O01 to new_service. NHSN submission restored. Phase 2 (months 7-12): SIR computation monthly batch job. Antibiogram data entry workflow. SSI and MRSA bacteremia NHSN event types. Outbreak investigation tracking module. Legacy monolith decommission after all event types validated. Phase 3 (Year 2): NLP-assisted HAI classification from clinical notes. Antibiogram trend analytics. NHSN dashboards with benchmark comparison.
target_output.development_guidelines: Go: golangci-lint, gofmt, 80% test coverage on business logic (case definition evaluation, CDA generation). gRPC contracts defined in .proto files versioned in the repo; breaking changes require major version bump. NHSN case definition criteria changes require clinical review by infection control physician before merge. CDC NHSN API integration tested against CDC sandbox in staging before every production deploy. CDA documents must pass CDC NHSN validation tool before submission in staging. No PHI in Datadog log fields; use event_id UUID as correlation key. Secrets Manager references in code via AWS SDK; no env var fallback.
target_output.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: CDC NHSN Protocol version changes (v11 to v12) can invalidate CDA document structure without advance notice from CDC. Mitigation: NHSN protocol version parameterized in surveillance_criteria table; version upgrade requires clinical informatics review and staging validation against CDC sandbox. Risk: Mirth Connect Parameter Store flag read latency during high-volume morning lab batches could cause routing inconsistency if Mirth caches the flag. Mitigation: Mirth flag cache TTL set to 60 seconds; flag changes take effect within 1 minute. Tradeoff: gRPC between services over a message broker (SQS/Kafka) for MVP simplicity, accepting tighter coupling; add async queue in Phase 2 if throughput exceeds 500 HL7 messages/hour. Tradeoff: database-per-service over shared PostgreSQL for bounded context isolation, accepting 3x RDS cost but enabling independent scaling and schema evolution per service.
target_output.open_questions_resolved: ISS-001 resolved: nhsn_submission_service CDA generation specified per NHSN Protocol v11 with CDC REST API, Secrets Manager credentials, 3-retry backoff, S3 archive with Object Lock. ISS-002 resolved: HL7 routing via Mirth Connect reading Parameter Store feature flags per message type, enabling exclusive routing to legacy or new service during Strangler Fig migration. NHSN CDA schema version: NHSN Protocol v11 for CAUTI and CLABSI confirmed with CDC NHSN integration team.

metadata.notes: HAI surveillance microservices decomposition, round 2, resolving NHSN CDA spec and HL7 routing toggle issues.

project_goal.value: Design and build a greenfield zero-trust network infrastructure for a newly constructed community hospital campus, establishing network segmentation, device identity management, and encrypted communication for clinical IoT devices, medical equipment, workstations, and administrative systems
project_goal.rationale: New hospital construction provides a rare opportunity to design network security from scratch without legacy constraints

target_users.value: Network and security operations engineers, clinical biomedical engineers responsible for medical device onboarding, and hospital IT administrators
target_users.rationale: Internal IT and biomedical engineering teams who provision and operate the infrastructure

project_class.rationale: Network infrastructure build with Terraform IaC, Ansible device configuration, and Kubernetes control plane; no application UI

capabilities.rationale: Infrastructure automation, PKI/mTLS backend services, and Keycloak identity management; no frontend or data capabilities

complexity_level.rationale: 2,000+ endpoint hospital campus with clinical IoT, medical devices, and workstations across 8 clinical VLANs requiring mTLS and microsegmentation

risk_level.rationale: Network security failures could disrupt medical device communications with direct patient safety implications; ransomware risk to clinical operations

data_sensitivity.rationale: Network topology, device inventory, access policies, and certificate authorities are confidential security assets

external_exposure.rationale: Zero-trust control plane accessible only within hospital management network; no public internet exposure

access_model.value: Keycloak SSO for all operator access to control plane tools; network engineers have VLAN configuration rights; biomedical engineers have device onboarding rights only; security operations have read-only policy review access
access_model.rationale: Principle of least privilege for infrastructure administration

feature_scope.value: VLAN microsegmentation per device class, device identity certificate issuance via internal CA, mTLS for device-to-device and device-to-service communication, automated policy enforcement and anomaly alerting, medical device onboarding workflow, security event incident ticketing
feature_scope.rationale: Full zero-trust network controls required by HICP and NIST SP 800-66

mvp_scope.value: 8 VLAN segments for device classes, internal CA for certificate issuance, mTLS enforcement for clinical IoT to nurse call and EHR communication, device inventory database, automated firewall policy enforcement via Ansible
mvp_scope.rationale: Core zero-trust controls needed before hospital opens for patient care

security_baseline.value: All inter-segment traffic encrypted with mTLS 1.3; certificate issuance requires device identity verification; firewall deny-by-default between VLANs; no cleartext protocols (no Telnet, HTTP, FTP) on any segment
security_baseline.rationale: HIPAA Security Rule technical safeguards and NIST SP 800-66 zero-trust controls

frontend_stack.value: No frontend; infrastructure control via Terraform CLI, Ansible playbooks, and Keycloak admin console
frontend_stack.rationale: Infrastructure project with no browser-based UI requirement

backend_stack.value: Python 3.12 with Ansible for device configuration automation; Go 1.22 for certificate management API service
backend_stack.rationale: Ansible for idempotent network device configuration at scale; Go CA service for low-latency certificate issuance

data_platform.value: PostgreSQL 16 for device inventory and certificate registry; no caching layer required
data_platform.rationale: Relational device inventory with certificate lifecycle tracking

hosting_target.value: Self-hosted bare-metal Kubernetes on dedicated management server rack; no cloud dependency for critical control plane functions
hosting_target.rationale: Air-gapped capability requirement; cloud dependency for certificate issuance is unacceptable for medical device PKI

privacy_retention_policy.value: Device certificates retained for device lifetime plus 2 years; security event logs retained 3 years; network access policy changes retained indefinitely as audit trail
privacy_retention_policy.rationale: HIPAA Security Rule and NIST audit trail requirements

future_scope.value: Network Access Control (NAC) integration for 802.1X supplicant authentication, behavioral analytics for device traffic anomaly detection, automated quarantine workflows
future_scope.rationale: Phase 2 zero-trust maturity improvements post-hospital opening

constraints.value: Hospital opens for patient care in 8 months; all medical device network segments must be operational before clinical commissioning; no single-CA design (dual CA hierarchy required for resilience)
constraints.rationale: Hard opening date and resilience requirement for critical PKI infrastructure

observability_baseline.value: Grafana and Prometheus for Kubernetes control plane and network device metrics; syslog aggregation via Graylog for firewall and switch events; PagerDuty for segment policy violation alerts
observability_baseline.rationale: Self-hosted observability stack consistent with air-gap requirement

execution_preference.value: IaC-first: all network configuration as Terraform and Ansible code reviewed in GitLab CI before applying; no manual switch/firewall changes in production
execution_preference.rationale: Reproducibility and audit trail requirement for HIPAA and NIST compliance

compliance_context.value: HIPAA Security Rule technical safeguards (45 CFR 164.312); NIST SP 800-66 Rev 2 healthcare-specific controls; HICP (Health Industry Cybersecurity Practices) Practice 10 for medical device security; FDA medical device cybersecurity guidance for networked devices
compliance_context.rationale: Healthcare network infrastructure with medical devices subject to FDA guidance

project.goal: Zero-trust network for new hospital campus with mTLS device identity and VLAN microsegmentation
project.target_users: Network engineers, biomedical engineers, security operations
project.timeline: 8 months before hospital opening

backend.pattern: IaC-first: Terraform for network topology, Ansible for device configuration playbooks

security.auth: Keycloak SSO for control plane access
security.device_identity: Internal dual-CA PKI, mTLS 1.3 for all inter-segment traffic
security.policy: Deny-by-default firewall rules between VLANs; allow rules require documented justification

devops.iac: Terraform for Kubernetes and network topology
devops.config: Ansible playbooks for switch and firewall configuration
devops.environments: management (bare-metal K8s), staging (lab rack), production (hospital campus)

constraints.air_gap: Control plane must operate without internet dependency

open_questions.vlan_count: Final VLAN count pending biomedical engineering device census (estimated 8-12)

reasoner_reviews.product: Zero-trust network infrastructure for a greenfield hospital is an unusually clean design opportunity. The constraint of no single-CA hierarchy is correct: losing the root CA in a hardware failure during patient care operations would halt device onboarding and certificate renewal, potentially taking clinical systems offline. Dual CA hierarchy with offline root and online issuing CAs per NIST guidance is mandatory.
reasoner_reviews.architect_reasoner: step-ca (Smallstep) is the right choice for self-hosted PKI: it supports ACME protocol for automated certificate renewal, SCEP for legacy medical device enrollment, and webhook-based device identity validation. The bare-metal Kubernetes control plane for running step-ca, Keycloak, and monitoring workloads keeps critical certificate issuance air-gapped. Terraform Cisco IOS-XE provider manages VLAN trunk and access port configurations as code.
reasoner_reviews.security: Deny-by-default inter-VLAN firewall policy must be implemented in the network layer (Cisco ASA or Palo Alto NGFWs), not just in host firewalls. Medical device VLANs (clinical IoT, infusion pumps, imaging) must never have unrestricted internet access; outbound traffic restricted to specific vendor update server IP ranges. Certificate revocation via OCSP stapling required for all mTLS connections.
reasoner_reviews.constraints: 8-month timeline is achievable given greenfield construction. Critical path items: CA hierarchy setup in month 1, VLAN configuration in month 2-3, device onboarding playbooks in month 4-5, parallel commissioning testing with biomedical engineering in month 6-7, clinical handoff in month 8.

backend.service_design: Two Go services on Kubernetes: certificate_api (REST API for device certificate issuance, renewal, and revocation backed by step-ca; webhook endpoint for device identity validation before issuance) and device_registry (REST API for device inventory CRUD, VLAN assignment, and certificate lifecycle status). Python Ansible collection for network device configuration playbooks.
backend.api_patterns: certificate_api: REST with mutual TLS (management workstations authenticate with their own certificates). device_registry: REST with Keycloak JWT. Both exposed on internal cluster Ingress only.
backend.failure_handling: step-ca online issuing CA failure: certificate_api queues pending requests in PostgreSQL, retries on CA recovery. Kubernetes deployment health check triggers PagerDuty if certificate_api pod restarts more than 3 times in 5 minutes. VLAN misconfiguration: GitLab CI Ansible dry-run in staging lab rack before production apply.
backend.backend_risks: Medical device SCEP enrollment failures if device SCEP client uses legacy SHA-1 signatures unsupported by step-ca. Mitigation: biomedical engineering validates each device model SCEP capability in staging lab before production rollout.

security.auth_design: Keycloak realm for hospital infrastructure operators. LDAP federation to hospital Active Directory. MFA (TOTP) required for all Keycloak logins. step-ca admin API access restricted to certificate_api service account with pre-shared provisioner token.
security.authorization_model: Keycloak RBAC: NETWORK_ENGINEER (Terraform and Ansible apply rights via CI/CD), BIOMEDICAL_ENGINEER (device_registry write for onboarding, certificate_api read), SECURITY_OPS (read-only policy and event log access). No direct switch/firewall CLI access for any role in production; all changes via GitLab CI pipeline.
security.secrets_management: step-ca root CA private key stored in hardware security module (HSM, Thales Luna Network HSM). Issuing CA private key in encrypted K8s Secret with restricted RBAC. PostgreSQL credentials in Kubernetes Secrets managed by External Secrets Operator synced from HashiCorp Vault. GitLab CI service account token for Terraform stored in GitLab project CI/CD variables.
security.security_risks: Compromise of the online issuing CA signing key would allow forging of device certificates, potentially enabling unauthorized device impersonation. Mitigation: issuing CA key in HSM-backed K8s Secret; CA certificate pinned in all device mTLS configurations; certificate revocation via OCSP; 90-day certificate lifetime limits blast radius.

devops.deployment_topology: Bare-metal Kubernetes (3-node control plane, 2-node worker pool) on dedicated management server rack. Workloads: step-ca Deployment (2 replicas), certificate_api Deployment (2 replicas), device_registry Deployment (2 replicas), Keycloak StatefulSet (3-node cluster), PostgreSQL StatefulSet (primary-replica), Prometheus+Grafana, Graylog. All storage on local NVMe via Rook-Ceph.
devops.ci_cd_design: GitLab CI pipeline: Terraform plan (lint, validate, plan against staging lab rack), Ansible playbook syntax check and dry-run, go test (85% coverage gate) for certificate_api and device_registry, Docker build and push to self-hosted GitLab Container Registry, kubectl apply to K8s via GitLab agent. Production network changes require 2 approvals.
devops.observability_stack: Prometheus: certificate expiry countdown per device (alert at 14 days), step-ca issuance rate, Kubernetes pod availability. Grafana dashboards: device onboarding progress per VLAN, certificate issuance success/failure rate, inter-VLAN firewall block events per segment pair. Graylog: syslog aggregation from Cisco switches and firewalls, firewall deny events. PagerDuty: step-ca CA unavailability, certificate issuance failure rate above 5%, inter-VLAN policy violation spike.

ISS-001.title: Dual CA hierarchy not specified in Round 1
ISS-001.detail: Round 1 plan described a single step-ca instance with no CA hierarchy. The constraint explicitly requires dual CA (offline root + online issuing) for resilience. Single-CA design violates the contract constraint and NIST SP 800-66 PKI guidance.

history.note: Single CA design identified as constraint violation in auditor review

ISS-002.title: OCSP revocation not addressed in Round 1
ISS-002.detail: Round 1 mTLS design had no certificate revocation mechanism. In a hospital with 2,000+ networked devices, the ability to revoke a compromised device certificate within minutes is required to contain lateral movement.

history.note: Certificate revocation mechanism missing from PKI design

focus_issues.title: Dual CA hierarchy not specified in Round 1
focus_issues.detail: Single CA design violates the contract constraint and NIST SP 800-66 PKI guidance. Must implement offline root CA + online issuing CA.

latest_recommendations: Implement dual CA hierarchy: offline root CA (HSM-protected, air-gapped ceremony) signing two online issuing CAs for device and operator certificate domains
Add OCSP responder service for certificate revocation; configure all mTLS clients to staple OCSP responses; define emergency revocation runbook
revision_memory.latest_plan_title: Community Hospital Zero-Trust Network Infrastructure v1

convergence_state.reason: High-severity CA hierarchy constraint violation unresolved

previous_audits.summary: Good overall zero-trust architecture with correct VLAN segmentation approach and IaC-first discipline. Critical violations: single CA design directly contradicts the dual CA hierarchy constraint; no OCSP revocation mechanism defined for 2,000+ device fleet.
strengths: Deny-by-default VLAN microsegmentation correctly specified with 8 device class segments
IaC-first discipline with Terraform and Ansible and no manual changes in production is well-specified
Keycloak RBAC for infrastructure operators correctly mapped to biomedical and network engineering roles
concerns: Single step-ca instance with no CA hierarchy violates the explicit dual CA constraint
No certificate revocation mechanism for compromised device certificates
blocking_issues: Dual CA hierarchy is an explicit contract constraint; single CA design must be replaced before plan approval
recommendations: Implement two-tier PKI: air-gapped offline root CA (Thales HSM ceremony, cross-signed issuing CAs) with two online issuing CAs (device-ca and operator-ca) as step-ca instances on Kubernetes
Deploy OCSP responder as a K8s Deployment behind ClusterIP; configure step-ca to publish OCSP URLs in issued certificates; all mTLS peers configured with OCSP stapling

issue_updates.title: Single CA violates dual CA constraint
issue_updates.detail: Round 1 had single step-ca instance; contract requires dual CA hierarchy for resilience

issue_updates.title: No OCSP revocation mechanism
issue_updates.detail: No certificate revocation specified for 2,000+ device fleet in Round 1

previous_plan.title: Community Hospital Zero-Trust Network Infrastructure v1
previous_plan.executive_summary: Zero-trust network with 8 VLAN segments, step-ca PKI (single instance), mTLS for device communication, Terraform and Ansible IaC, bare-metal Kubernetes control plane. Single CA design violates dual CA constraint. No OCSP revocation defined.
previous_plan.architecture_overview: Bare-metal Kubernetes control plane running single step-ca instance, Keycloak, and Prometheus/Grafana. 8 VLANs for device classes. Terraform Cisco IOS-XE provider for switch config. Ansible for device enrollment. No CA hierarchy. No OCSP.
previous_plan.security_and_compliance: Deny-by-default firewall policy between VLANs. mTLS for device-to-device communication. Keycloak MFA for operators. No certificate revocation mechanism. Single CA instance with no offline root.

target_output.thinking_summary: Round 2 revision resolving the critical ISS-001 CA hierarchy violation by implementing a proper two-tier PKI: an offline root CA (Thales HSM-protected, air-gapped key ceremony) cross-signing two online issuing CAs (device-ca and operator-ca) as separate step-ca instances on Kubernetes. Also resolving ISS-002 by adding a step-ca OCSP responder K8s Deployment with certificate stapling configured on all mTLS peers, and defining an emergency revocation runbook for compromised device certificates.

fix_report.action_taken: Replaced single step-ca design with two-tier PKI hierarchy: offline root CA private key generated and stored in Thales Luna Network HSM during air-gapped ceremony, cross-signing two online issuing CAs (device-ca for clinical device certificates, operator-ca for management workstation and service account certificates). Both issuing CAs run as separate step-ca Kubernetes Deployments. Root CA never comes online; re-signing of issuing CAs is a scheduled air-gapped ceremony.
fix_report.expected_outcome: Dual CA hierarchy satisfies the explicit contract constraint and NIST SP 800-66 guidance. Compromise or failure of one issuing CA does not compromise the root or the other issuing CA domain.

fix_report.action_taken: Added OCSP responder Kubernetes Deployment backed by step-ca; OCSP responder URL embedded in all issued device certificates. mTLS configurations on Cisco switches and all K8s workloads configured with OCSP stapling. Emergency revocation runbook defined: security ops calls certificate_api DELETE /certificates/{device_id}, step-ca marks certificate revoked, OCSP responder serves revoked status within 60 seconds, affected device loses mTLS handshake on next connection.
fix_report.expected_outcome: Compromised device certificate revoked and effective within 60 seconds of revocation command. All mTLS peers reject the compromised device's connections via OCSP stapling check.

target_output.title: Community Hospital Campus Zero-Trust Network Infrastructure
target_output.executive_summary: A greenfield zero-trust network infrastructure for a new 400-bed community hospital campus, built on 8 VLAN microsegments, a two-tier internal PKI with offline root CA and dual online issuing CAs, and mTLS 1.3 for all clinical device and inter-service communication. Managed entirely via Terraform IaC and Ansible playbooks on a bare-metal Kubernetes control plane. Medical device onboarding via SCEP/ACME enrollment against the device-ca issuing CA. Certificate revocation via OCSP responder with stapling enforced on all mTLS peers. Complies with HIPAA Security Rule technical safeguards, NIST SP 800-66 Rev 2, and FDA medical device cybersecurity guidance.
target_output.architecture_overview: Zero-trust architecture built on three pillars: network microsegmentation (8 VLANs by device class with deny-by-default inter-VLAN firewall policy), device identity (two-tier PKI issuing mTLS certificates to every networked endpoint), and encrypted communication (mTLS 1.3 enforced for all inter-segment traffic). Two-tier PKI: offline root CA (Thales Luna HSM, air-gapped; cross-signs two issuing CAs), device-ca issuing CA (step-ca on Kubernetes; issues SCEP/ACME certificates to clinical IoT, infusion pumps, imaging, and workstations), operator-ca issuing CA (step-ca on Kubernetes; issues certificates to management workstations and K8s service accounts). OCSP responder K8s Deployment serves real-time certificate status. Bare-metal Kubernetes control plane (management rack, no cloud dependency) hosts PKI services, Keycloak identity management, device_registry service, and observability stack. All network topology and device configuration managed as Terraform and Ansible code reviewed in GitLab CI before production apply.
target_output.technology_stack: PKI: step-ca (Smallstep) for device-ca and operator-ca online issuing CAs; Thales Luna Network HSM for root CA key protection. Network IaC: Terraform with Cisco IOS-XE provider for VLAN and trunk configuration; Ansible for firewall ACL and switch port configuration. Device enrollment: SCEP for legacy medical devices, ACME for modern IoT. Identity: Keycloak (bare-metal K8s StatefulSet). Backend services: Go 1.22 (certificate_api, device_registry). Data: PostgreSQL 16. K8s platform: Rook-Ceph for storage. CI/CD: GitLab CI with self-hosted runners. Monitoring: Prometheus, Grafana, Graylog for syslog. Secrets: HashiCorp Vault on K8s with External Secrets Operator.
target_output.functional_feature_map: VLAN Microsegmentation: 8 segments (clinical_iot, infusion_pumps, imaging_modalities, workstations, admin, servers, building_systems, guest), each isolated by deny-by-default Cisco ASA firewall ACLs with documented allow rules only. Device Certificate Issuance: biomedical engineer submits new device to device_registry with device class and MAC address; certificate_api validates device identity via registry and issues certificate from device-ca via SCEP or ACME; certificate installed on device during commissioning. mTLS Enforcement: all inter-VLAN traffic requires valid device certificate; Cisco switch port ACLs block non-mTLS connections between segments. OCSP Revocation: security ops calls certificate_api to revoke compromised device certificate; OCSP responder serves revoked status within 60 seconds; affected device loses mTLS on next connection attempt. Security Event Alerting: Graylog detects inter-VLAN firewall deny events, triggers PagerDuty incident with device IP, source and destination segment, and policy violated.
target_output.system_components: Bare-metal Kubernetes Cluster (management rack): device-ca step-ca Deployment (2 replicas, SCEP and ACME endpoints, device-ca issuing certificate from offline root), operator-ca step-ca Deployment (2 replicas, operator certificate issuance), OCSP Responder Deployment (2 replicas, behind ClusterIP, OCSP status from step-ca revocation log), certificate_api Go service (REST API for device certificate lifecycle), device_registry Go service (device inventory and VLAN assignment), Keycloak StatefulSet (3-node cluster, LDAP to Active Directory), PostgreSQL StatefulSet (primary+replica), Prometheus+Grafana, Graylog+OpenSearch for syslog. Thales Luna Network HSM: hosts root CA private key, connected to management rack via dedicated HSM network segment. Cisco Catalyst switches: Terraform-managed VLAN trunk and access port configuration. Cisco ASA firewalls: Ansible-managed inter-VLAN ACL policies. Offline root CA: air-gapped laptop with HSM for CA ceremony operations only.
target_output.workflows: Device Onboarding: biomedical engineer logs into device_registry API (Keycloak JWT, BIOMEDICAL_ENGINEER role), creates device_identity record with device_type, MAC address, ward, and VLAN assignment. device_registry assigns device to correct VLAN per device_class policy. certificate_api webhook validates device exists in registry before issuing. Biomedical engineer initiates SCEP enrollment on medical device or ACME enrollment on IoT device, directed to device-ca SCEP/ACME endpoint. device-ca step-ca validates ACME/SCEP challenge, issues certificate signed by device-ca issuing CA (90-day lifetime, OCSP URL embedded). Certificate installed on device; device placed on assigned switch port access VLAN. Device Commissioning Test: network engineer verifies mTLS handshake succeeds between new device and its permitted service endpoints (e.g., nurse call server, EHR integration gateway). Firewall deny test: engineer verifies device cannot communicate with segments outside its allowed policy. Emergency Device Revocation: security ops receives alert of compromised or stolen device. Calls certificate_api DELETE /api/v1/certificates/{device_id} (SECURITY_OPS role required). certificate_api calls step-ca revoke API for the device certificate serial number. OCSP responder polls step-ca revocation log every 30 seconds; serves revoked status within 60 seconds of revocation. Device loses mTLS handshake on next connection attempt to any service. Network engineer issues Ansible playbook to disable the switch port as secondary containment. Security event logged to Graylog and incident ticket created.
target_output.data_model: device_identities (id UUID PK, device_name VARCHAR, device_type ENUM clinical_iot/infusion_pump/imaging/workstation/admin/building_systems, mac_address VARCHAR UNIQUE, ward VARCHAR, vlan_id INT FK, certificate_serial VARCHAR, certificate_status ENUM active/revoked/expired, certificate_expires_at TIMESTAMP, onboarded_by UUID FK, onboarded_at TIMESTAMP). network_segments (id INT PK, vlan_id INT UNIQUE, segment_name VARCHAR, device_class VARCHAR, allowed_destinations JSONB). access_policies (id UUID PK, source_segment INT FK, destination_segment INT FK, protocol VARCHAR, port INT, justification TEXT, approved_by UUID FK, effective_from DATE). security_events (id UUID PK, event_type ENUM policy_violation/cert_revocation/auth_failure, source_ip VARCHAR, destination_ip VARCHAR, source_segment VARCHAR, destination_segment VARCHAR, device_id UUID FK, detected_at TIMESTAMP, incident_ticket_id VARCHAR). certificate_authorities (id UUID PK, ca_name VARCHAR, ca_type ENUM root/issuing_device/issuing_operator, certificate_subject VARCHAR, expires_at TIMESTAMP, hsm_backed BOOLEAN).
target_output.api_design: certificate_api (internal K8s ClusterIP, Ingress with mutual TLS for management workstations): POST /api/v1/certificates/device (initiate SCEP/ACME enrollment, device identity validated against device_registry), GET /api/v1/certificates/{device_id} (certificate status and expiry), DELETE /api/v1/certificates/{device_id} (emergency revocation, SECURITY_OPS role), GET /api/v1/certificates/expiring?days=14 (pre-expiry report for operations). device_registry API (internal K8s, Keycloak JWT): POST /api/v1/devices (onboard new device, BIOMEDICAL_ENGINEER role), GET /api/v1/devices?vlan_id=&status= (device inventory), PATCH /api/v1/devices/{id}/vlan (VLAN reassignment, NETWORK_ENGINEER role), GET /api/v1/segments (VLAN segment list with allowed destinations). All APIs internal-only on K8s Ingress; no internet exposure.
target_output.security_and_compliance: Two-tier PKI: offline root CA with Thales Luna HSM-protected private key, cross-signing device-ca and operator-ca online issuing CAs. Root CA key ceremony documented with dual-control (two security officers required). Online issuing CA keys stored in K8s Secrets managed by External Secrets Operator from Vault. Device certificates: 90-day lifetime, OCSP URL embedded, SCEP for legacy medical devices, ACME for modern IoT. OCSP responder K8s Deployment updated every 30 seconds; mTLS peers configured with OCSP stapling (fail-closed: reject if OCSP unreachable). Deny-by-default inter-VLAN firewall: all ACL changes via Ansible playbook reviewed in GitLab CI, require 2 network engineer approvals. No cleartext protocols on any VLAN. Graylog security event log with 3-year retention. HIPAA Security Rule 164.312(a)(1): technical access controls via mTLS and VLAN segmentation. NIST SP 800-66 Rev 2 Control RA-5: PKI lifecycle management documented. FDA device cybersecurity: 90-day certificate rotation, revocation capability, encrypted device-to-service communication.
target_output.deployment_and_operations: Bare-metal Kubernetes: 3-node control plane (kubeadm, etcd TLS), 2-node worker pool on management server rack. Rook-Ceph for persistent storage (PostgreSQL, Keycloak). GitLab CI pipeline: Terraform validate and plan against staging lab rack, Ansible playbook syntax and dry-run, go test (85% coverage gate), Docker build and push to self-hosted GitLab Container Registry, kubectl apply via GitLab Kubernetes agent. Network changes: Terraform plan reviewed, approved by 2 engineers, applied by CI pipeline. No direct SSH to network devices in production; Ansible via GitLab CI only. Environments: staging lab rack (production hardware parity, 2 switches + 1 ASA, 50-device test fleet), production hospital campus (all 8 VLANs, 2,000+ endpoints). Root CA key ceremony: pre-opening day air-gapped ceremony with HSM, dual security officer signing, issuing CA certificates generated and loaded to K8s, ceremony documentation retained. Certificate expiry monitoring: Prometheus alert at 14 days, automated Ansible renewal job for devices supporting ACME, manual remediation runbook for legacy SCEP devices.
target_output.observability: Prometheus metrics: device_ca and operator_ca certificate issuance rate and error rate, OCSP responder response time (SLO: under 100ms), device certificate expiry countdown per VLAN (alert at 14 days), Kubernetes pod availability for all PKI services. Grafana dashboards: device onboarding progress per VLAN vs target count, certificate issuance success/failure rate per device class, inter-VLAN firewall block events per segment pair per day. Graylog syslog aggregation: Cisco ASA deny event parsing with source IP, destination IP, segment labels; PagerDuty alert on more than 10 policy violations per device in 5 minutes (indicates potential lateral movement attempt). PagerDuty: step-ca pod unavailability, OCSP responder error rate above 5%, certificate issuance failure rate above 2%.
target_output.cost_and_scaling: Self-hosted infrastructure. Server hardware: 5-node Kubernetes cluster on Dell PowerEdge R650 (one-time cost, capital expense). Thales Luna HSM: one-time acquisition. No ongoing cloud cost. Operational cost: GitLab (self-hosted, negligible), step-ca (open source), Keycloak (open source), Prometheus/Grafana/Graylog (open source). Estimated annual licensing cost: zero (all open-source stack). Scale: bare-metal K8s supports 4,000+ device certificate operations per day at expected hospital capacity without additional nodes.
target_output.phased_implementation: Phase 1 Foundation (months 1-2): Bare-metal Kubernetes cluster build and validation. Root CA key ceremony (Thales HSM, dual-control). device-ca and operator-ca step-ca deployments. OCSP responder deployment. PostgreSQL and Keycloak deployment. GitLab CI pipeline for certificate_api and device_registry. Phase 2 Network Segmentation (months 3-4): Terraform Cisco IOS-XE VLAN configuration for all 8 segments in staging lab rack. Ansible firewall ACL playbooks with deny-by-default policy. Validate mTLS enforcement between test devices across segments. Phase 3 Device Onboarding (months 5-6): Device onboarding playbooks for each device class (SCEP for infusion pumps and imaging, ACME for clinical IoT). Biomedical engineering commissioning tests for all device types in staging. OCSP revocation drill. Phase 4 Production Commissioning (months 7-8): Campus network hardware commissioned per Terraform plan. All medical devices enrolled and assigned to VLANs. 2-week parallel run with network engineers on-site. Hospital opening day handoff.
target_output.development_guidelines: All network configuration as Terraform or Ansible code; no manual switch or firewall changes in production. Go code: golangci-lint, gofmt, 85% test coverage on certificate_api and device_registry. PKI policy: certificate lifetime changes require security architect approval. CA hierarchy changes (new issuing CA, root renewal) require documented change control with dual-officer sign-off. Ansible playbooks: idempotent (check mode must succeed before apply). Terraform: no apply without passing GitLab CI plan job. ADR documents for all PKI design decisions. VLAN ACL changes require documentation of business justification and approving clinical stakeholder.
target_output.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: Legacy medical device SCEP clients may use SHA-1 or 512-bit RSA keys unsupported by step-ca's default configuration. Mitigation: all device models tested in staging lab against SCEP enrollment before production; step-ca policy configured to accept minimum 2048-bit RSA for legacy devices with documented exception. Risk: OCSP responder unavailability with fail-closed configuration blocks all mTLS connections hospital-wide if both OCSP pods fail simultaneously. Mitigation: OCSP Deployment with 2 replicas across nodes, PodDisruptionBudget preventing simultaneous eviction, Prometheus alert on single-replica state. Tradeoff: self-hosted PKI over cloud CA (AWS Private CA, Azure Key Vault CA) for air-gap requirement and long-term cost elimination, accepting operational burden of managing HSM and CA lifecycle in-house. Tradeoff: step-ca over EJBCA for simpler Kubernetes deployment and ACME support, accepting less GUI-based management capability.
target_output.open_questions_resolved: ISS-001 resolved: two-tier PKI implemented with offline root CA (Thales HSM) cross-signing device-ca and operator-ca step-ca issuing CAs on Kubernetes. ISS-002 resolved: OCSP responder K8s Deployment with 30-second revocation log polling and fail-closed mTLS stapling configuration; emergency revocation runbook defined. Final VLAN count confirmed at 8 with biomedical engineering device census complete.

metadata.notes: Hospital zero-trust network infrastructure, round 2, resolving dual CA hierarchy and OCSP revocation issues.

project_goal.value: Federated learning prototype to train shared sepsis prediction models across hospital sites without centralizing patient data, minimizing compute and data transfer costs
project_goal.rationale: Multi-site collaboration without PHI centralization is the core research hypothesis

target_users.value: Clinical data scientists and ML researchers at participating hospital sites
target_users.rationale: Primary operators of the federated training pipeline

project_class.rationale: Exploratory research system not yet production-hardened

capabilities.rationale: Federated training, gradient aggregation, and model evaluation analytics are core

complexity_level.rationale: Distributed training coordination, differential privacy, and multi-site orchestration

risk_level.rationale: Patient cohort data used for training even if never transmitted; gradient leakage risk exists

data_sensitivity.rationale: Local training datasets contain PHI from ICU encounters

external_exposure.rationale: Site aggregation server accessible only to authenticated site clients over mTLS

access_model.value: Per-site JWT with site_id claim; aggregation server verifies site certificates; no patient data leaves site boundary
access_model.rationale: Trust boundary is at the site gradient submission endpoint

feature_scope.value: Local training orchestration, differential privacy noise injection, gradient submission, federated averaging aggregation, global model distribution, per-round accuracy and privacy budget tracking
feature_scope.rationale: Full federated learning loop from local training through aggregation

mvp_scope.value: 3-site pilot with binary sepsis onset classifier, Flower framework orchestration, epsilon-delta DP per round, MongoDB model versioning
mvp_scope.rationale: Sufficient to validate federated accuracy versus centralized baseline

security_baseline.value: mTLS between site clients and aggregation server, gradient signing with HMAC-SHA256, differential privacy with epsilon<=1.0 per round, no raw patient features transmitted
security_baseline.rationale: Gradient leakage attacks require cryptographic and DP controls

frontend_stack.value: None; CLI-based site client and aggregation server, results visualized via Grafana dashboards
frontend_stack.rationale: Research prototype; no browser UI required

project.goal: Train sepsis prediction model federatedly across hospital sites without centralizing ICU patient data
project.target_users: Clinical data scientists at participating sites
project.timeline: 3-site pilot in 12 weeks

backend.framework: Flower 1.8 with PyTorch 2.2; gRPC for transport
backend.api: gRPC service definitions for gradient submission and model distribution

security.auth: Per-site JWT, mTLS certificates from research CA
security.encryption: Gradient signing HMAC-SHA256, TLS 1.3 in transit
security.compliance: HIPAA, IRB protocol, differential privacy epsilon<=1.0 per round

constraints.data_residency: No patient data leaves site perimeter

open_questions.gradient_compression: Evaluate TopK sparsification to reduce bandwidth between sites

reasoner_reviews.product: The federated approach directly addresses the IRB constraint that raw ICU cohort data cannot be centralized. Binary sepsis onset classification is a well-scoped first task. The 3-site pilot provides enough statistical power to compare against the centralized baseline.
reasoner_reviews.architect_reasoner: Flower with FedAvg is the right aggregation strategy for the prototype phase. Site client heterogeneity (different cohort sizes, class imbalance ratios) will require per-site weighted averaging. The gRPC transport layer must handle lossy site connectivity gracefully with retry semantics and round-timeout fallback.
reasoner_reviews.security: Gradient inversion attacks can reconstruct partial training samples from gradient updates even after DP noise injection at epsilon=1.0. HMAC-SHA256 gradient signing addresses integrity but not reconstruction. SecAgg+ (Flower built-in) secret-shares gradients before transmission so the aggregator reconstructs only the FedAvg sum without seeing individual site vectors.
reasoner_reviews.constraints: GCP budget of $2000/month is adequate for the 3-site pilot aggregation server scaling to zero between rounds. Per-round epsilon budget tracking must be enforced at the Opacus layer and logged in MongoDB for IRB audit evidence with site exclusion when cumulative epsilon reaches cap.
reasoner_reviews.critic: Round 1 plan lacked a concrete SecAgg implementation path and did not address unresolved gradient reconstruction risk. The current revision must close ISS-001 by implementing SecAgg+ and ISS-002 by specifying PrivacyBudgetLedger enforcement logic with site blocking behavior.

backend.service_design: Flower SuperLink aggregation server on GKE Autopilot; site clients are Flower ClientApp processes in local hospital Kubernetes with access to PostgreSQL cohort query layer
backend.api_patterns: gRPC with proto definitions for GradientSubmission (site_id, round_id, secagg_shares_bytes, hmac_sig, dp_epsilon_used) and ModelDistribution (round_id, weights_bytes, global_metrics)
backend.business_modules: FederatedRoundCoordinator (manages round lifecycle, site readiness checks, dropout tolerance), GradientAggregator (FedAvg with site-weighted averaging), PrivacyBudgetLedger (tracks cumulative epsilon per site, enforces participation block), ModelRegistry (MongoDB-backed version store with GCS weight objects)
backend.failure_handling: FedAvg proceeds if >= 2 of 3 sites submit within 30-minute round timeout; missing site gradients logged with reason; round recorded as partial in MongoDB with participation_ratio field

security.auth_design: Each site receives client certificate from research CA; Flower SuperLink verifies cert at TLS handshake; JWT contains site_id and round authorization scope
security.authorization_model: Sites may only submit gradients for the current active round; aggregation server rejects out-of-order or replayed gradient submissions via round_id monotonicity check
security.secrets_management: Site certificates and private keys stored in Kubernetes secrets at each hospital; aggregation server CA key in GCP Secret Manager with 90-day rotation
security.security_risks: SecAgg+ eliminates individual gradient inspection; residual risk at epsilon=10.0 cumulative DP is acceptable per IRB risk assessment; model poisoning from compromised site mitigated by gradient norm clipping at 1.0

data.entities: federated_clients (site_id, cert_fingerprint, cohort_size, status, enrollment_irb_ref), model_rounds (round_id, strategy, participating_sites, global_auc_roc, privacy_budget_consumed_per_site, started_at, completed_at), local_gradients (round_id, site_id, gradient_norm, dp_noise_scale, secagg_shares_count, hmac_valid, received_at), aggregated_models (round_id, weights_gcs_key, auc_roc, precision_at_threshold), privacy_budgets (site_id, cumulative_epsilon, cap_epsilon, alert_threshold, status)
data.storage_design: MongoDB 7 on GKE for round metadata and gradient audit records; model weights in GCS versioned bucket; local PostgreSQL at each site queries ICU admission and lab result tables for cohort construction
data.data_risks: Gradient archive in MongoDB contains metadata that could indirectly reveal cohort composition; access restricted to aggregation service account only; 90-day purge policy enforced via TTL index

devops.deployment_topology: GKE Autopilot cluster (us-central1) running Flower SuperLink Deployment, MongoDB 7 StatefulSet, Prometheus/Grafana; site clients deployed via Helm chart to each hospital on-prem Kubernetes
devops.ci_cd_design: pytest suite includes DP accounting unit tests verifying epsilon accumulation within 1e-4 tolerance, FedAvg correctness tests with synthetic gradients, SecAgg mock integration tests; GitHub Actions pushes site client image to GCR
devops.observability_stack: Prometheus scrapes Flower metrics (rounds_completed_total, site_participation_ratio, gradient_norm_p95, privacy_budget_remaining); Grafana dashboards for round convergence curves and privacy budget burn-down; PagerDuty if site participation below 2/3 for 3 consecutive rounds
devops.rollback_strategy: Model rollback by redeploying previous round weights from GCS; round state reset in MongoDB via admin gRPC call with irb_amendment_ref

ISS-001.title: Gradient reconstruction risk not mitigated
ISS-001.detail: At epsilon=1.0 Gaussian DP, gradient inversion attacks can reconstruct partial feature distributions. Round 1 plan acknowledged risk but provided no concrete SecAgg path or IRB exception documentation.

history.note: Raised by security reviewer; FedAvg plan did not address SecAgg or formal risk acceptance

ISS-002.title: Per-round epsilon budget enforcement not implemented
ISS-002.detail: IRB protocol requires cumulative epsilon tracked and capped per site. Round 1 plan described Opacus integration but did not specify PrivacyBudgetLedger enforcement or site blocking behavior.

history.note: Compliance reviewer noted missing enforcement logic

The FedAvg core is solid from round 1. Key decisions: integrate Flower SecAgg+ for gradient confidentiality so the aggregator sees only the FedAvg sum; define site exclusion trigger at epsilon=10.0 with PI Slack alert at epsilon=8.0; retain MongoDB and GCS architecture.
