target_output.title: Federated Sepsis Prediction Prototype - Revision 2
target_output.executive_summary: A federated learning research prototype training a binary sepsis onset classifier across 3 hospital ICU cohorts using Flower with FedAvg aggregation, SecAgg+ gradient confidentiality, and Opacus differential privacy (epsilon=0.5 per round, cumulative cap=10.0). Python 3.12 site clients run within hospital network boundaries, secret-sharing gradient updates via gRPC to a GKE Autopilot aggregation server. No raw patient data leaves any site; PrivacyBudgetLedger enforces per-site cumulative epsilon with automated PI notification.
target_output.architecture_overview: Three-tier federated architecture: site-local training layer (PyTorch 2.2 models training on PostgreSQL-sourced ICU cohort features within hospital Kubernetes clusters), gRPC gradient transport layer with Flower SecAgg+ secret sharing (gradients split into N shares so the aggregator reconstructs only the FedAvg weighted sum, never individual site vectors), and GKE Autopilot aggregation server running Flower SuperLink with MongoDB 7 for round metadata and GCS for model weight versioning. The FederatedRoundCoordinator orchestrates the full round lifecycle: site readiness check, SecAgg handshake, gradient collection within 30-minute timeout, FedAvg aggregation, global model evaluation against held-out validation set, and weight distribution. PrivacyBudgetLedger tracks per-site cumulative epsilon and blocks participation when the IRB-approved cap is reached.
target_output.technology_stack: Python 3.12, Flower 1.8 (SuperLink, ClientApp, SecAgg+ protocol), PyTorch 2.2, Opacus 0.15 (Gaussian DP noise), gRPC with protobuf, MongoDB 7 on GKE, PostgreSQL 16 (per-site local), GCS (model weight versioning), GKE Autopilot us-central1, on-prem Kubernetes (hospital sites), GitHub Actions CI, Grafana + Prometheus, Slack API for budget alerts.
target_output.functional_feature_map: FederatedRoundCoordinator: manages round start/stop, site dropout tolerance (proceeds with >=2/3 sites), round timeout enforcement. SecAgg+ Protocol Handler: Flower secret sharing preventing individual gradient inspection by aggregator. PrivacyBudgetLedger: tracks cumulative epsilon per site, blocks participation at epsilon=10.0, triggers Slack alert at epsilon=8.0. GradientAggregator: FedAvg with cohort-size-weighted averaging and gradient norm clipping at 1.0. ModelRegistry: GCS versioned weight objects with MongoDB per-round AUC-ROC and precision metadata. CohortQueryLayer: site-local SQL against PostgreSQL ICU admission, lab result, and vasopressor tables for feature construction.
target_output.system_components: Flower SuperLink (GKE Autopilot us-central1): gRPC server accepting secret-shared gradient submissions and distributing model weights, scales to zero between rounds. Flower ClientApp (per-site hospital Kubernetes): orchestrates local training epochs, Opacus DP noise injection, SecAgg share generation, gradient submission. PrivacyBudgetLedger: MongoDB-backed epsilon tracker with FederatedRoundCoordinator enforcement hooks. ModelRegistry: GCS bucket with versioned weight blobs, MongoDB documents storing round metrics. Prometheus exporters on SuperLink and all ClientApps with Grafana convergence and budget dashboards.
target_output.workflows: Federated Training Round: PI triggers round via CLI; FederatedRoundCoordinator checks site availability and PrivacyBudgetLedger status per site; SecAgg+ handshake distributes secret-sharing parameters; each site trains configured local epochs on ICU cohort, applies Opacus Gaussian noise (epsilon=0.5 per round), generates SecAgg shares, submits via gRPC within 30-minute window; SuperLink reconstructs FedAvg weighted sum; global model evaluated on held-out validation partition; weights stored in GCS and distributed to all sites with round metrics logged to MongoDB. Privacy Budget Enforcement: PrivacyBudgetLedger increments cumulative_epsilon after each accepted round; Slack alert to PI at epsilon=8.0; FederatedRoundCoordinator blocks site at epsilon=10.0; re-enrollment via admin gRPC call with IRB amendment reference recorded in MongoDB.
target_output.data_model: federated_clients (site_id TEXT PK, cert_fingerprint TEXT, cohort_size INT, status ENUM active/blocked/suspended, enrollment_irb_ref TEXT, last_round_participated INT). model_rounds (round_id UUID PK, strategy TEXT, participating_sites TEXT[], sites_completed TEXT[], global_auc_roc FLOAT, global_precision FLOAT, privacy_budget_consumed_by_site JSONB, started_at TIMESTAMPTZ, completed_at TIMESTAMPTZ). local_gradients (id UUID PK, round_id UUID FK, site_id TEXT, gradient_norm FLOAT, dp_noise_scale FLOAT, secagg_shares_count INT, hmac_valid BOOL, received_at TIMESTAMPTZ). aggregated_models (round_id UUID PK, weights_gcs_key TEXT, auc_roc FLOAT, precision_at_threshold FLOAT, created_at TIMESTAMPTZ). privacy_budgets (site_id TEXT PK, cumulative_epsilon FLOAT, per_round_epsilon FLOAT, cap_epsilon FLOAT DEFAULT 10.0, alert_threshold FLOAT DEFAULT 8.0, last_updated TIMESTAMPTZ, status ENUM active/blocked). Clinical data references: participating_cohorts (id UUID PK, client_id FK, cohort_definition_json JSONB — patient inclusion/exclusion criteria, diagnosis_icd10_filter VARCHAR[], min_encounter_count INT, target_condition VARCHAR e.g. sepsis/T2D/CHF), model_performance_by_site (id UUID PK, round_id FK, client_id FK, clinical_metric VARCHAR e.g. AUROC/sensitivity/specificity, metric_value NUMERIC, patient_count_suppressed BOOLEAN).
target_output.api_design: gRPC service FederatedLearning: SubmitGradient(SubmitGradientRequest{site_id, round_id, secagg_shares_bytes, hmac_sig, dp_epsilon_used}) returns GradientAck{accepted, reason}. GetModelWeights(GetWeightsRequest{site_id, round_id}) returns ModelWeightsResponse{weights_bytes, round_id, global_auc_roc, global_precision}. GetRoundStatus(RoundStatusRequest{round_id}) returns RoundStatusResponse{status ENUM active/complete/partial, sites_completed, sites_pending, round_epsilon_total}. Admin gRPC: ResetSiteEnrollment(site_id, irb_amendment_ref). Prometheus /metrics endpoint on SuperLink for Grafana scraping.
target_output.security_and_compliance: SecAgg+ protocol (Flower built-in): site gradients secret-shared before transmission; aggregator reconstructs only FedAvg weighted sum; individual site gradient vectors never visible to aggregation server operator. Opacus Gaussian DP epsilon=0.5 per round (cumulative cap 10.0 per IRB protocol); gradient norm clipping at 1.0. mTLS with per-site client certificates from research CA; Flower SuperLink verifies cert at TLS handshake. HMAC-SHA256 on gradient payload for integrity. PrivacyBudgetLedger enforces site blocking at cumulative epsilon=10.0. HIPAA: no PHI leaves site perimeter; gradient metadata in MongoDB classified as research data under IRB protocol. IRB exception path: sites unable to support SecAgg overhead must provide PI-signed and DPO-countersigned risk acceptance before participating without SecAgg. GCP Secret Manager for aggregation server CA key with 90-day rotation.
target_output.deployment_and_operations: GKE Autopilot cluster in us-central1 runs Flower SuperLink Deployment (2 replicas, HPA on CPU 60%), MongoDB 7 StatefulSet (3-node replica set), and Prometheus/Grafana StatefulSet. Site client Helm chart deployed to each hospital on-prem Kubernetes cluster by hospital DevOps; chart values parameterized for site_id, cohort DB connection string, and CA certificate bundle. GitHub Actions CI pipeline: mypy type check, pytest (DP accounting unit tests, FedAvg correctness tests, SecAgg mock integration tests), Docker build, push to GCR with SHA tag, Helm upgrade on GKE via Workload Identity. Environments: research-dev (GKE dev cluster with 1 simulated site using synthetic gradients), pilot-production (3 hospital sites + GKE production). Secrets: site private keys in hospital Kubernetes secrets; aggregation server CA in GCP Secret Manager.
target_output.observability: Prometheus metrics: federated_rounds_completed_total (counter), site_participation_ratio (gauge per round), gradient_norm_p95 (histogram), privacy_budget_remaining_epsilon (gauge per site), round_duration_seconds (histogram). Grafana dashboards: Round Convergence (AUC-ROC per round with per-site contribution), Privacy Budget Burn-Down (cumulative epsilon vs. cap per site), Site Health (last heartbeat, participation streak). PagerDuty alert triggers: any site participation_ratio < 0.5 for 3 consecutive rounds, gradient_norm_p95 > 5.0 (potential gradient poisoning signal), MongoDB primary failover during active round.
target_output.cost_and_scaling: GKE Autopilot scales to zero between training rounds; active round costs approximately $40/hour for 2 SuperLink replicas and MongoDB. Estimated $600/month for 3-site pilot running 2 rounds/week. GCS model weight storage negligible at $0.02/GB/month for weight sizes under 500 MB. Scaling to 10 sites requires horizontal pod autoscaling on SuperLink and MongoDB sharding on site_id shard key; estimated $1800/month within research budget.
target_output.phased_implementation: Phase 1 Pilot Setup (6 weeks): Deploy GKE aggregation server with SecAgg+ enabled, provision site client Helm charts at 3 hospitals, establish mTLS CA infrastructure, implement PrivacyBudgetLedger with site blocking, run synthetic gradient integration tests verifying SecAgg correctness and epsilon accounting. Phase 2 First Live Rounds (4 weeks): Execute 10 federated training rounds on de-identified ICU cohorts, validate SecAgg+ gradient confidentiality, collect AUC-ROC convergence curves, compare against centralized baseline. Phase 3 IRB Reporting (2 weeks): Generate IRB progress report with epsilon budget audit evidence per site, document SecAgg+ overhead measurements, present accuracy vs. privacy tradeoff curves to steering committee.
target_output.development_guidelines: Python type hints enforced via mypy strict mode in CI. Opacus DP accounting unit tests must verify epsilon accumulation matches theoretical values within 1e-4 tolerance. SecAgg protocol correctness tested with mock site clients using known gradient vectors and verified reconstruction. All MongoDB writes to privacy_budgets use transactions to prevent partial epsilon increments. Site client image SHA pinned in Helm chart values. PRs require review from both an ML engineer and the security lead.
target_output.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: SecAgg+ adds approximately 15% latency overhead per round due to secret sharing handshake; acceptable for research prototype where round duration is hours. Risk: Site Kubernetes version heterogeneity causes client deployment drift; mitigated by Helm chart pinning image SHA. Tradeoff: epsilon=0.5 per round with cap=10.0 yields 20 maximum rounds before site exhaustion; sufficient for 12-week pilot. Tradeoff: FedAvg with cohort-size weighting favors large-cohort sites; FedProx regularization considered for Phase 2. Risk: MongoDB replica set primary failover mid-round could lose partial gradient records; mitigated by round coordinator retry with idempotency check on round_id and site_id combination.
target_output.open_questions_resolved: SecAgg+ selected over custom secret sharing for Flower ecosystem compatibility. Cumulative epsilon cap set at 10.0 per IRB protocol amendment approved March 2026. Site dropout threshold set at 2/3 minimum to proceed with partial rounds. BioBERT feature extraction confirmed to run locally at each site with no PHI reaching external model APIs. Gradient norm clipping threshold of 1.0 confirmed with ML team as appropriate for sepsis binary label distribution.

metadata.notes: Federated learning prototype for multi-site sepsis prediction; revision 2 closes gradient security and epsilon enforcement gaps with SecAgg+ and PrivacyBudgetLedger enforcement.

project_goal.value: Cost-optimized open-source ETL pipeline replacing a commercial tool for extracting, transforming, and loading payer clearinghouse claims data into the hospital revenue cycle analytics platform
project_goal.rationale: Commercial ETL tool costs $180K/year; purpose-built pipeline estimated at $30K/year in infrastructure

target_users.value: Revenue cycle analysts, billing managers, and denial management teams consuming transformed claims data in Snowflake
target_users.rationale: Downstream consumers drive the transformation and reporting requirements

project_class.rationale: Batch ETL pipeline with no interactive user interface; pure data movement and transformation

capabilities.rationale: EDI parsing, dbt transformations, Airflow orchestration, and Snowflake analytics are the core

complexity_level.rationale: EDI 835/837 parsing is specialized but the pipeline topology is straightforward batch ETL

risk_level.rationale: Financial data sensitivity but no real-time clinical operations dependency; batch delays are recoverable

data_sensitivity.rationale: Claims data includes patient financial transactions, payer contract amounts, and denial records

external_exposure.rationale: Pipeline runs entirely within hospital network; SFTP pull from payer endpoints is inbound only

access_model.value: Okta SSO for Airflow UI; Snowflake RBAC with analyst and manager roles; service accounts for pipeline automation
access_model.rationale: Human access to Airflow via SSO; pipeline credentials via Secrets Manager

feature_scope.value: Nightly 835 remittance and 837 claim EDI ingestion from 12 payer SFTP endpoints, EDI parsing and denial code mapping, Snowflake staging and production schema loads, dbt dimensional models for denial rate/days-in-AR/net collection rate, daily freshness SLA alerting
feature_scope.rationale: Core pipeline scope replacing all commercial tool functionality

mvp_scope.value: 835 remittance ingestion and denial mapping for top 5 payers, dbt models for denial rate by payer and service line, Airflow DAG with email alerts on failure
mvp_scope.rationale: Top 5 payers represent 80% of remittance volume; denial analysis is highest priority use case

security_baseline.value: SFTP private key authentication for payer connections, TLS 1.3 for all data in transit, Snowflake column-level encryption for patient-linked fields, AWS Secrets Manager for credentials
security_baseline.rationale: Financial data and HIPAA obligations require encryption in transit and at rest

frontend_stack.value: None; analysts consume data via Snowflake SQL clients and existing BI tools; Airflow UI for pipeline monitoring
frontend_stack.rationale: No custom UI required; Airflow UI covers operational visibility

backend_stack.value: Python 3.12 with dbt-core 1.8 and Apache Airflow 2.9 on AWS MWAA
backend_stack.rationale: dbt for SQL-based transformations, Airflow for orchestration, MWAA for managed Airflow eliminating operational overhead

data_platform.value: Snowflake (staging and production schemas); S3 for raw EDI file landing zone
data_platform.rationale: Hospital already has Snowflake enterprise contract; S3 provides durable raw file storage

hosting_target.value: AWS MWAA (Managed Airflow) in us-east-1; Snowflake virtual warehouse in same region
hosting_target.rationale: Managed Airflow reduces operational burden; data residency in us-east-1 matches Snowflake account

privacy_retention_policy.value: Raw EDI files retained in S3 for 7 years for audit; Snowflake staging tables purged after 30 days; production tables retain 7 years
privacy_retention_policy.rationale: CMS and payer contract audit requirements mandate 7-year retention for claims data

future_scope.value: 837 claim submission pipeline, real-time eligibility check integration, ML-based denial prediction model feeding from dbt output tables
future_scope.rationale: Phase 2 roadmap after 835 remittance pipeline stabilizes

constraints.value: Must process 835 files within 4 hours of SFTP availability; Snowflake compute budget $8K/month; no patient SSN stored in Snowflake tables
constraints.rationale: Analyst SLA and budget caps from revenue cycle leadership

observability_baseline.value: AWS CloudWatch for MWAA Airflow logs and DAG run metrics; Airflow email alerts on task failure; Snowflake query history for dbt run diagnostics
observability_baseline.rationale: Standard AWS monitoring stack with Airflow native alerting

execution_preference.value: Iterative delivery with payer-by-payer onboarding; first payer in production within 6 weeks
execution_preference.rationale: Parallel run with commercial tool payer-by-payer reduces switchover risk

compliance_context.value: HIPAA for patient-linked claims data; CMS conditions of participation for revenue cycle audit trails; payer contract data use restrictions vary by payer
compliance_context.rationale: Financial data sensitivity and HIPAA obligations require formal compliance tracking

project.goal: Replace commercial ETL with open-source pipeline ingesting 835/837 EDI from 12 payer SFTPs into Snowflake revenue cycle analytics
project.target_users: Revenue cycle analysts and denial management teams
project.timeline: First payer production in 6 weeks; full 12-payer cutover in 6 months

backend.framework: Apache Airflow 2.9 on MWAA; dbt-core 1.8 for transformations
backend.etl: Custom Python EDI 835 parser; parameterized Airflow DAG per payer

security.auth: Okta SSO for Airflow UI; SFTP private key auth for payer connections
security.encryption: TLS 1.3 in transit; Snowflake column encryption for patient-linked fields

data.storage: S3 for raw EDI file landing zone with 7-year retention

devops.ci: GitHub Actions with dbt test and DAG import validation

constraints.processing_sla: 835 files processed within 4 hours of SFTP availability
constraints.data_restriction: No patient SSN stored in Snowflake

open_questions.payer_sftp_reliability: Two payers have historically unreliable SFTP uptime; retry strategy needed

reasoner_reviews.product: The payer-by-payer onboarding strategy is the right risk mitigation approach; running parallel with the commercial tool until each payer validates reduces cutover risk. The denial analysis use case should drive the first dbt model priorities since it is the highest business-value output for revenue cycle leadership.
reasoner_reviews.architect_reasoner: Parameterized Airflow DAGs per payer allow independent scheduling and retry policies, which matters given the two unreliable SFTP endpoints. The dbt staging/marts layer separation keeps raw EDI audit records intact while allowing clean dimensional models for analysts. Snowflake COPY INTO is the correct load mechanism for S3-staged EDI files.
reasoner_reviews.security: SFTP private keys for 12 payers stored in AWS Secrets Manager with per-payer rotation schedules. Column-level encryption in Snowflake must cover patient MRN and date-of-birth in claim_submissions; SSN must never be loaded per the constraint. Airflow Connections must use the Secrets Manager backend, not the Airflow metadata DB.
reasoner_reviews.constraints: 4-hour processing SLA from SFTP availability is achievable with MWAA scheduling at hourly intervals; late file detection via S3 event notification reduces polling overhead. Snowflake $8K/month budget requires XS warehouse for dbt runs and auto-suspend at 60 seconds.
reasoner_reviews.critic: Round 1 plan did not address the unreliable SFTP payer issue (ISS-001) with concrete retry and alerting logic, and the dbt model lineage for ISS-002 was described at a high level without specifying the staging-to-marts dependency graph or data freshness assertions.

backend.service_design: Airflow DAG template parameterized by payer_id, sftp_host, sftp_path_pattern, file_type (835/837); each payer gets its own DAG instance with independent schedule and retry policy
backend.business_modules: EDI835Parser (Python: reads ISA/GS/ST segments, extracts CLM/CLP/SVC records, maps CAS adjustment reason codes to internal taxonomy), RemittanceLoader (Snowflake COPY INTO staging.remittances_raw from S3), DenialClassifier (maps CARC/RARC codes to denial categories), dbt marts layer (fct_denials, fct_payments, dim_payers, dim_service_lines)
backend.background_jobs: Nightly S3 prefix scan DAG checks for any EDI files not processed within 6 hours and triggers PagerDuty alert; weekly reconciliation DAG compares Snowflake loaded claim counts against payer remittance totals
backend.failure_handling: SFTP task retries 3 times with exponential backoff (5/15/45 min); failed payer DAG sends email to revenue cycle ops and does not block other payer DAGs; partial file loads rolled back via Snowflake transaction

security.auth_design: Airflow MWAA integrated with Okta via SAML; service accounts for DAG execution use IAM roles with least-privilege S3 and Secrets Manager access
security.secrets_management: All 12 payer SFTP private keys in AWS Secrets Manager; Airflow Connection backend set to SecretsManagerBackend; keys rotated on payer-defined schedules (30-90 days)
security.privacy_controls: Patient MRN and DOB encrypted in Snowflake using column masking policies; SSN excluded from all SELECT and COPY INTO statements at parser level; analyst role cannot see unmasked values
security.audit_and_logging_controls: Airflow DAG run logs in CloudWatch with payer_id tag; Snowflake access history view queried weekly for unauthorized column access patterns; S3 access logs for raw EDI file reads

data.entities: claim_submissions (claim_id, payer_id, patient_mrn_encrypted, service_date, total_billed, procedure_codes TEXT[], icd10_codes TEXT[], loaded_at), remittance_advices (remittance_id, payer_id, check_number, payment_date, total_paid, file_s3_key, loaded_at), denial_records (denial_id, claim_id, carc_code, rarc_code, denial_category, denial_date, status ENUM open/appealed/resolved/written_off), payment_postings (posting_id, remittance_id, claim_id, allowed_amount, paid_amount, contractual_adjustment, patient_responsibility, posted_at), revenue_cycle_metrics (snapshot_date, payer_id, service_line, denial_rate, days_in_ar, net_collection_rate, total_billed, total_collected)
data.storage_design: S3 bucket (landing zone): raw EDI files at s3://rcm-edi-landing/{payer_id}/{YYYY-MM-DD}/; lifecycle rule archives to S3 Glacier after 90 days, purges at 7 years. Snowflake staging schema: raw tables loaded via COPY INTO with PURGE=false. Snowflake production schema: dbt models as tables with incremental strategy on loaded_at.
data.retention_and_deletion: Raw EDI in S3: 90 days hot, 7 years Glacier. Snowflake staging: 30-day TTL enforced via scheduled TRUNCATE task. Snowflake production: 7-year retention; SSN never loaded per legal constraint.
data.analytics_events: dbt run completion logged to Snowflake audit table with model name, row count, execution_time_ms; denial rate anomaly (>5% change week-over-week) triggers email to revenue cycle director

devops.deployment_topology: AWS MWAA environment in us-east-1 (mw1.small for dev, mw1.medium for production); DAGs deployed from GitHub via S3 DAG bucket sync; Snowflake virtual warehouse (XS, auto-suspend 60s); S3 landing zone bucket with S3 event notifications to SQS triggering file-arrival sensor tasks
devops.ci_cd_design: GitHub Actions: sqlfluff lint on dbt SQL files, dbt compile and test against dev Snowflake, Airflow DAG import validation (no import errors), pytest for EDI parser unit tests; PR merge to main triggers S3 DAG bucket sync to staging MWAA
devops.cost_controls: Snowflake XS warehouse auto-suspend 60 seconds; separate XS warehouse for dbt analyst queries to isolate pipeline compute; MWAA mw1.small in dev auto-paused outside business hours; S3 Intelligent-Tiering for EDI landing bucket

ISS-001.detail: Two of 12 payer SFTP endpoints have documented uptime below 95%. Round 1 plan described generic retry logic but did not specify backoff parameters, max retry count, alerting escalation path, or whether late-arriving files trigger downstream DAG re-runs.

history.note: Ops team flagged UnitedHealth and BCBS SFTP endpoints as historically unreliable; plan did not address

history.note: Data engineering reviewer noted missing dbt model dependency spec and freshness config

best_plan.title: Revenue Cycle Claims ETL Pipeline
best_plan.executive_summary: Python/Airflow/dbt pipeline ingesting 835 EDI remittance files from payer SFTPs into Snowflake with denial rate and days-in-AR dimensional models.

target_output.thinking_summary: Revision 2 addresses ISS-001 by specifying per-payer SFTP retry policy (3 retries, 5/15/45 min exponential backoff, PagerDuty escalation on max-retry breach, late-file re-run via S3 event sensor) and ISS-002 by fully specifying the dbt dependency graph with source freshness assertions tied to the 4-hour SLA. Core MWAA/Snowflake/S3 architecture from round 1 is sound and retained.

fix_report.action_taken: Added per-payer SFTP retry policy to deployment_and_operations and workflows: 3 retries with 5/15/45-minute exponential backoff; Airflow SFTPSensor with S3 event notification trigger; PagerDuty alert on max-retry exhaustion; late-arriving files (>4 hours) trigger downstream DAG re-run via Airflow TriggerDagRunOperator. UnitedHealth and BCBS DAGs flagged with higher retry counts (5 retries) based on historical reliability data.
fix_report.expected_outcome: 4-hour processing SLA maintained for reliable payers; ops team alerted within 5 minutes of SFTP failure for unreliable payers

fix_report.action_taken: Documented full dbt dependency graph in data_model and workflows: stg_claims (from staging.claim_submissions) -> stg_remittances (from staging.remittance_advices) -> int_denial_classifications -> fct_denials (incremental on service_date, unique_key=denial_id) -> mart_denial_rate (table, partitioned by payer_id). Added dbt source freshness assertions: source('staging','remittance_advices') max_loaded_at_filter 4 hours, error_after 4h, warn_after 3h. Incremental strategy for claim_submissions uses service_date as partition key with merge on claim_id.
fix_report.expected_outcome: dbt CI catches lineage breaks before production deployment; freshness assertions fail the pipeline run if 4-hour SLA is breached, triggering alert

target_output.title: Revenue Cycle Claims ETL Pipeline - Revision 2
target_output.executive_summary: A cost-optimized Python/Airflow/dbt data pipeline replacing a $180K/year commercial ETL tool by ingesting 835 EDI remittance files from 12 payer SFTP endpoints into a Snowflake revenue cycle analytics schema. Parameterized Airflow DAGs on AWS MWAA handle per-payer scheduling with exponential-backoff SFTP retry policies and PagerDuty escalation for unreliable payers. dbt-core transforms raw EDI loads through a documented staging-to-marts lineage with source freshness assertions enforcing the 4-hour analyst SLA. Denial rate, days-in-AR, and net collection rate dimensional models are refreshed nightly.
target_output.architecture_overview: Batch ETL pipeline with three processing layers: ingestion (parameterized Airflow DAGs on MWAA pull 835/837 EDI files from payer SFTP endpoints to S3 landing zone using SFTPToS3Operator with per-payer retry policy), transformation (Python EDI835Parser maps ISA/CLP/SVC segments and CARC/RARC denial codes; Snowflake COPY INTO loads staging schema), and analytics modeling (dbt-core runs stg -> int -> fct -> mart model graph on Snowflake XS warehouse with source freshness assertions and incremental strategies). S3 event notifications via SQS trigger file-arrival sensors, eliminating fixed-schedule SFTP polling. All credentials stored in AWS Secrets Manager accessed via Airflow SecretsManagerBackend. Snowflake column masking policies encrypt patient MRN and DOB at the analyst query layer.
target_output.technology_stack: Python 3.12 (EDI parser, Airflow operators), Apache Airflow 2.9 on AWS MWAA (mw1.small dev / mw1.medium prod), dbt-core 1.8 with dbt-snowflake adapter, Snowflake Enterprise (XS warehouse, auto-suspend 60s), S3 (EDI landing zone, DAG bucket, 7-year lifecycle), AWS Secrets Manager (payer SFTP keys, Snowflake credentials), SQS (S3 event notifications), CloudWatch (MWAA logs and DAG run metrics), PagerDuty (SFTP escalation alerts), GitHub Actions CI (sqlfluff, dbt compile/test, pytest EDI parser).
target_output.functional_feature_map: EDI835Parser: reads ISA/GS/ST envelope, extracts CLP claim records, SVC service line amounts, CAS adjustment segments; maps CARC codes to internal DenialCategory taxonomy (clinical, authorization, duplicate, eligibility, timely_filing). RemittanceLoader: Snowflake COPY INTO staging.remittance_advices_raw from S3 with FILE_FORMAT=EDI_CSV. PerPayerDAG: parameterized template with payer_id, sftp_host, sftp_key_secret, file_pattern, schedule_cron, max_retries; UnitedHealth and BCBS instances override max_retries=5. LateFileMonitor: CloudWatch alarm if S3 file-arrival event not received within 4 hours of payer-expected window. dbt Models: stg_claims, stg_remittances, int_denial_classifications, fct_denials (incremental), fct_payments (incremental), mart_denial_rate (table), mart_days_in_ar (table), mart_net_collection_rate (table).
target_output.system_components: AWS MWAA (us-east-1): Airflow scheduler and workers executing payer DAGs; S3 DAG bucket synced from GitHub on merge. S3 Landing Zone: s3://rcm-edi-landing/{payer_id}/{YYYY-MM-DD}/ with S3 event notification to SQS queue consumed by Airflow S3KeySensor. EDI Parser (Python Lambda-style callable in Airflow PythonOperator): parses 835 file, writes normalized CSV to S3 processed prefix. Snowflake: staging schema (raw tables COPY INTO from S3), production schema (dbt models), XS warehouse for pipeline runs, separate XS for analyst queries. AWS Secrets Manager: 12 payer SFTP private keys plus Snowflake service account credentials. CloudWatch: MWAA task logs, custom metrics for file_arrival_lag_minutes and denial_rate_delta. PagerDuty: escalation on SFTP max-retry exhaustion and file_arrival_lag > 4 hours.
target_output.workflows: Nightly Remittance Ingestion (per payer): Airflow S3KeySensor waits for S3 event notification from SQS (file arrival); SFTPToS3Operator pulls 835 file with 3-retry exponential backoff (5/15/45 min), 5 retries for UnitedHealth and BCBS; on max-retry exhaustion PagerDuty alert fires and DAG marked failed without blocking other payer DAGs; EDI835Parser PythonOperator normalizes file to CSV; COPY INTO loads staging.remittance_advices_raw; SnowflakeOperator runs dbt source freshness check (error if >4 hours since last load); TriggerDagRunOperator fires dbt transformation DAG. dbt Transformation Run: stg_remittances (materialized=view) -> int_denial_classifications (applies CARC taxonomy join) -> fct_denials (incremental merge on denial_id, partition on service_date) -> mart_denial_rate (full table refresh with payer_id, service_line dimensions); dbt test suite runs after each model (not_null, unique, accepted_values for denial_category); test failure triggers CloudWatch alarm and email to data engineering.
target_output.data_model: claim_submissions (claim_id UUID PK, payer_id TEXT, patient_mrn_encrypted TEXT, service_date DATE, billed_amount DECIMAL, procedure_codes TEXT[], icd10_principal TEXT, facility_npi TEXT, loaded_at TIMESTAMPTZ). remittance_advices (remittance_id UUID PK, payer_id TEXT, check_number TEXT, payment_date DATE, total_paid DECIMAL, file_s3_key TEXT, loaded_at TIMESTAMPTZ). denial_records (denial_id UUID PK, claim_id UUID FK, carc_code TEXT, rarc_code TEXT, denial_category ENUM clinical/authorization/duplicate/eligibility/timely_filing, denial_date DATE, status ENUM open/appealed/resolved/written_off). payment_postings (posting_id UUID PK, remittance_id UUID FK, claim_id UUID FK, allowed_amount DECIMAL, paid_amount DECIMAL, contractual_adjustment DECIMAL, patient_responsibility DECIMAL, posted_at TIMESTAMPTZ). dbt dependency graph: stg_claims (view) <- stg_remittances (view) -> int_denial_classifications (ephemeral) -> fct_denials (incremental, unique_key=denial_id, partition=service_date) -> mart_denial_rate (table). revenue_cycle_metrics (snapshot_date DATE, payer_id TEXT, service_line TEXT, denial_rate FLOAT, days_in_ar FLOAT, net_collection_rate FLOAT) - built by mart_days_in_ar and mart_net_collection_rate dbt models.
target_output.api_design: No external API; internal pipeline interfaces: Airflow REST API (authenticated via Okta) for manual DAG trigger and run status at GET /api/v1/dags/{payer_dag_id}/dagRuns. Snowflake external function not used. dbt Cloud not used; dbt-core runs inside Airflow BashOperator. PagerDuty Events API v2: POST /v2/enqueue for SFTP escalation. CloudWatch PutMetricData for file_arrival_lag_minutes custom metric published by Airflow DAG. Airflow CLI used in GitHub Actions CI for DAG import validation: airflow dags list-import-errors.
target_output.security_and_compliance: AWS Secrets Manager stores all 12 payer SFTP private keys and Snowflake service account password; Airflow SecretsManagerBackend configured so no credentials appear in Airflow metadata DB. Snowflake column masking policy: patient_mrn_encrypted and dob fields visible only to HIPAA_ANALYST role; analyst and manager roles see masked values. SSN excluded at EDI parser level via explicit exclusion list in field mapping config; dbt tests assert no SSN pattern in claim_submissions via custom generic test. SFTP connections use key-based auth only (no passwords); keys rotated on payer-defined schedules (30-90 days) via Secrets Manager rotation Lambda. HIPAA: Snowflake query history retained 1 year for PHI access audit; S3 access logs retained 7 years. CMS audit trail: raw EDI files retained in S3 Glacier for 7 years.
target_output.deployment_and_operations: MWAA environment: mw1.small (1 worker) in dev, mw1.medium (2 workers) in production, both in us-east-1. DAG deployment: GitHub Actions merges to main trigger S3 sync of dags/ directory to MWAA DAG bucket; new DAG versions active within 60 seconds of S3 sync. Snowflake virtual warehouse: PIPELINE_WH (XS, auto-suspend 60s) for dbt runs; ANALYST_WH (XS, auto-suspend 120s) for analyst queries; isolated to prevent compute contention. Per-payer DAG config stored in Airflow Variables (payer_configs JSON); new payer onboarding requires adding config entry and Secrets Manager key, no code changes. Environments: dev (MWAA mw1.small, Snowflake DEV database, 1 test payer), staging (MWAA mw1.small, Snowflake STAGING database, 5 payers parallel-run with commercial tool), production (MWAA mw1.medium, 12 payers). Rollback: re-deploy previous S3 DAG bucket version; dbt model rollback via Snowflake time-travel to previous snapshot.
target_output.observability: CloudWatch custom metrics: file_arrival_lag_minutes (per payer, alarm >240 min), dbt_test_failures_total (alarm >0), denial_rate_delta_pct (weekly anomaly detection alarm >5% change). Airflow task-level logs in CloudWatch log group /aws/mwaa/rcm-pipeline with payer_id dimension. Snowflake query history dashboard shows dbt model execution times and row counts. CloudWatch alarm: SFTP task failure after max retries routes to PagerDuty P2 incident with payer_id and dag_run_id context. Weekly dbt docs refresh generates data lineage documentation pushed to S3 static site for data engineering team.
target_output.cost_and_scaling: MWAA mw1.medium: approximately $800/month production. Snowflake XS pipeline warehouse: auto-suspend minimizes idle cost; estimated $1200/month for daily dbt runs across 12 payers. S3 storage: $50/month for 7-year EDI archive in Glacier. Total approximately $2100/month versus $15K/month commercial tool equivalent, achieving $150K+ annual savings. Scaling to 837 claim submission pipeline adds one Airflow DAG template and dbt model set; Snowflake XS warehouse handles volume to 5M claims/day.
target_output.phased_implementation: Phase 1 First Payer (6 weeks): Deploy MWAA environment, EDI835Parser, Snowflake staging schema, Secrets Manager key for top-volume payer (Aetna); run parallel with commercial tool; validate denial record counts match within 0.1%. Phase 2 Top-5 Payers (6 weeks): Onboard 4 additional payers including UnitedHealth and BCBS with 5-retry policy; deploy dbt mart models for denial rate and days-in-AR; revenue cycle analyst sign-off on Snowflake outputs vs. commercial tool. Phase 3 Full Cutover (12 weeks): Onboard remaining 7 payers; decommission commercial tool connection for cut-over payers; enable 837 claim submission pipeline planning.
target_output.development_guidelines: sqlfluff lint enforced in CI on all dbt SQL files (dialect=snowflake, max_line_length=120). EDI parser unit tests cover all CARC code categories and edge cases (zero-payment remittances, reversals). dbt tests must include not_null and unique constraints on all fact table keys; dbt source freshness assertions required for every source table. New payer onboarding documented in runbook: add payer_configs Airflow Variable entry, upload SFTP private key to Secrets Manager, add payer to staging parallel-run before production. PRs require approval from data engineering lead and revenue cycle ops.
target_output.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: BCBS and UnitedHealth SFTP endpoints may remain unreliable despite 5-retry policy; if files arrive >24 hours late, downstream dbt models produce stale denial metrics. Mitigation: SLA breach alert to revenue cycle director with last-known-good data timestamp. Risk: EDI 835 format variations across payers (non-standard segment ordering, proprietary extensions) may require parser updates per payer. Mitigation: parser built with configurable segment mapping; per-payer overrides stored in Airflow Variables. Tradeoff: dbt incremental models on claim_submissions reduce Snowflake compute cost but require late-arriving claims handling via merge on claim_id rather than append-only insert. Tradeoff: MWAA over self-managed Airflow sacrifices some configurability but eliminates operational overhead of Airflow scheduler maintenance.
target_output.open_questions_resolved: MWAA selected over self-managed Airflow on ECS for reduced operational burden. dbt-core over dbt Cloud to avoid additional SaaS cost. Snowflake COPY INTO selected over Fivetran connector to eliminate third-party data access to claims data. 7-year S3 retention confirmed with Legal for CMS audit compliance. SSN exclusion enforced at parser level confirmed with Compliance.

metadata.notes: Revenue cycle claims ETL pipeline; revision 2 addresses unreliable SFTP retry policy and dbt model lineage specification.

project_goal.value: High-throughput streaming pipeline ingesting continuous vital sign waveform data from ICU bedside monitors at 500 Hz per channel across a large health system, storing compressed archives and feeding real-time deterioration alerting consumers
project_goal.rationale: Current point-to-point monitor integrations cannot scale to enterprise-wide waveform archival

target_users.value: Clinical alerting service consumers (downstream), data scientists querying waveform archives, clinical engineers operating the pipeline
target_users.rationale: Two distinct consumer classes with very different latency requirements

project_class.rationale: Pure streaming ingest and archival pipeline; no interactive UI

capabilities.rationale: Streaming ingest, windowed aggregation, compressed archival, and batch export to cold storage

complexity_level.rationale: 50K concurrent device connections, 500 Hz per channel, enterprise health system scale

risk_level.rationale: Patient-linked waveform data classified as PHI; pipeline unavailability could delay clinical alerts

data_sensitivity.rationale: Continuous vital sign waveforms are patient health information under HIPAA

external_exposure.rationale: Pipeline operates entirely within hospital network; no internet-facing endpoints

access_model.value: Kafka mTLS for device-to-broker connections; Cognito service accounts for consumer microservices; ClickHouse access restricted to data science VLAN
access_model.rationale: Device trust and service identity required at scale

feature_scope.value: Bedside monitor Kafka producer clients, Kafka topic partitioned by device_id, Kafka Streams windowed aggregation (5-min statistics), ClickHouse waveform archive, downstream alert consumer topic, cold storage export to S3
feature_scope.rationale: Full waveform pipeline from device to archive and consumer

mvp_scope.value: 50-bed ICU pilot with 500 Hz ECG and SpO2 channels, 5-minute windowed statistics, ClickHouse archival, alert consumer topic publication
mvp_scope.rationale: Single ICU validates the architecture before enterprise rollout

security_baseline.value: mTLS for Kafka producers and consumers, TLS 1.3 for all network traffic, ClickHouse column encryption for patient_id fields, no raw waveform data accessible outside hospital network
security_baseline.rationale: HIPAA and FDA MDDS guidance require encryption for patient waveform data

frontend_stack.value: None; pipeline only; waveform viewers are separate clinical applications consuming from ClickHouse query API
frontend_stack.rationale: No UI in scope for this pipeline project

backend_stack.value: Java 21 with Apache Kafka Streams; custom Kafka producer client for bedside monitor integration
backend_stack.rationale: Kafka Streams provides stateful windowed aggregation natively; Java 21 virtual threads reduce per-connection overhead

data_platform.value: ClickHouse for hot waveform archive (last 30 days); S3 with Parquet format for cold storage (>30 days)
data_platform.rationale: ClickHouse columnar storage optimized for time-series waveform queries; S3 Parquet for cost-effective cold archival

hosting_target.value: AWS EKS in us-east-1 for all pipeline components; MSK (Managed Kafka) for broker cluster
hosting_target.rationale: EKS Kubernetes for container orchestration; MSK eliminates Kafka broker operational overhead

privacy_retention_policy.value: Hot waveform archive in ClickHouse retained 30 days; cold storage in S3 retained 7 years; patient-identifiable waveform metadata purged on patient discharge plus 7 years
privacy_retention_policy.rationale: HIPAA minimum required retention; FDA MDDS data archival requirements

future_scope.value: Enterprise rollout to all hospital ICUs, real-time ML inference on waveform streams for arrhythmia detection, FHIR waveform observation export
future_scope.rationale: Enterprise scale following pilot validation

constraints.value: Max end-to-end latency from device sample to alert consumer topic: 5 seconds; ClickHouse cluster budget $4K/month; must not impact EHR network traffic
constraints.rationale: Clinical alerting SLA and network segregation requirement

observability_baseline.value: Grafana + Prometheus for Kafka lag metrics, ClickHouse insert rates, stream processor throughput, and end-to-end latency percentiles
observability_baseline.rationale: Real-time streaming requires sub-minute metric resolution for SLA monitoring

execution_preference.value: 50-bed ICU pilot then iterative rollout by ICU unit
execution_preference.rationale: Validate architecture at pilot scale before full deployment

compliance_context.value: HIPAA for patient-linked waveform PHI; FDA guidance on Medical Device Data Systems (MDDS) for software ingesting waveform data from medical devices; Joint Commission alarm management standards for pipeline availability
compliance_context.rationale: High risk and health data sensitivity trigger compliance requirements

project.goal: Stream 500 Hz vital sign waveforms from ICU bedside monitors through Kafka to ClickHouse archive and alert consumer topic
project.target_users: Alert consumer microservices, data science teams, clinical engineers
project.timeline: 50-bed ICU pilot in 10 weeks

backend.framework: Kafka Streams 3.7; custom Kafka producer client
backend.api: No external API; Kafka topic interfaces only

security.auth: mTLS for Kafka producers/consumers; Cognito service accounts
security.encryption: TLS 1.3; ClickHouse column encryption for patient_id

open_questions.partition_count: Optimal Kafka partition count for 5000 device_id keys

reasoner_reviews.product: The 50-bed ICU pilot scope is appropriate for validating the Kafka Streams windowed aggregation and ClickHouse insert throughput before enterprise rollout. The two consumer classes (alerting at <5 seconds, data science at batch query) have fundamentally different SLAs that must be served by separate Kafka topics.
reasoner_reviews.architect_reasoner: Kafka partition count must be set to at least 5000 for device_id-keyed topics to ensure parallelism during enterprise rollout without repartitioning. ClickHouse MergeTree table with device_id partition key and order_by (device_id, sample_timestamp) will support both insert throughput and time-range queries by data science. Stream processor topology should be simple: source topic -> windowed aggregation -> sink topic; avoid complex joins that increase state store memory.
reasoner_reviews.security: Device-level mTLS certificate provisioning at 50-bed scale is manageable; at enterprise scale (thousands of devices) a certificate lifecycle management process is needed. Cognito service accounts for consumer microservices should use short-lived credentials with token refresh. ClickHouse patient_id encryption at column level prevents SQL queries from exposing PHI to unauthorized analysts.
reasoner_reviews.constraints: 5-second end-to-end latency SLA from device sample to alert consumer is achievable with Kafka Streams processing; the risk is ClickHouse insert backpressure under high write volume. ClickHouse async inserts with buffer table pattern reduce insert amplification. Network isolation from EHR VLAN requires dedicated Kafka broker network interfaces.

backend.service_design: Kafka producer client embedded in bedside monitor integration adapter (Java 21 virtual threads, one producer per device); MSK topic sensor_waveforms_raw partitioned by device_id (64 partitions for pilot, expandable to 512 for enterprise); Kafka Streams processor for 5-minute tumbling window aggregation
backend.business_modules: WaveformProducerAdapter (Java: reads monitor SDK samples at 500 Hz, batches 1-second chunks, publishes to sensor_waveforms_raw topic), WindowedAggregationProcessor (Kafka Streams: 5-min tumbling windows computing min/max/mean/std per channel), AlertStatisticsPublisher (publishes windowed stats to sensor_statistics topic consumed by alerting service), ClickHouseArchivalSink (Kafka Streams sink processor writing raw waveform segments to ClickHouse via JDBC)
backend.failure_handling: Kafka producer retries with idempotent producer config; ClickHouse archival sink uses async inserts with buffer table to absorb write bursts; dead-letter topic for unparseable monitor messages with device_id and error_code

security.auth_design: Per-device mTLS certificates for Kafka producer authentication; certificate CN matches device_id for audit correlation; Kafka ACLs restrict each device to produce on its assigned topic partition
security.secrets_management: Device certificates stored in Kubernetes secrets on monitor integration pods; CA managed by hospital PKI team; consumer service account Cognito tokens refreshed every 15 minutes
security.security_risks: Enterprise-scale device certificate lifecycle management is manual at 50-bed scale; needs automated certificate renewal workflow before enterprise rollout. ClickHouse network exposure must be restricted to data science VLAN only.

data.entities: waveform_streams (device_id TEXT, icu_bed_id TEXT, patient_id_encrypted TEXT, channel TEXT, sample_rate_hz INT, stream_start TIMESTAMPTZ, stream_end TIMESTAMPTZ), vital_sign_samples (device_id TEXT, sample_timestamp TIMESTAMPTZ, channel TEXT, value_mv FLOAT, sequence_number BIGINT) - ClickHouse MergeTree partitioned by (device_id, toYYYYMM(sample_timestamp)), waveform_archives (archive_id UUID, device_id TEXT, period_start TIMESTAMPTZ, period_end TIMESTAMPTZ, s3_key TEXT, compressed_size_bytes BIGINT, created_at TIMESTAMPTZ), alert_thresholds (device_id TEXT, channel TEXT, high_threshold FLOAT, low_threshold FLOAT, window_minutes INT)
data.storage_design: ClickHouse: vital_sign_samples MergeTree table, ORDER BY (device_id, sample_timestamp), PARTITION BY (device_id, toYYYYMM(sample_timestamp)), TTL sample_timestamp + INTERVAL 30 DAY. S3: parquet files at s3://icu-waveforms/{device_id}/{YYYY-MM}/{start_ts}_{end_ts}.parquet generated by nightly Spark export job. MSK: sensor_waveforms_raw (64 partitions, 3-day retention), sensor_statistics (16 partitions, 1-day retention).
data.data_risks: Patient_id in ClickHouse must use column-level encryption; accidental plain-text storage of patient_id in waveform metadata would expose PHI to any analyst with SELECT access. Waveform timestamps combined with device_id create indirect patient identifier that must be treated as PHI.

devops.deployment_topology: EKS cluster: WaveformProducerAdapter DaemonSet on monitor integration nodes (one pod per ICU network segment), Kafka Streams processor Deployment (3 replicas, HPA on Kafka consumer group lag), ClickHouseArchivalSink Deployment (2 replicas). MSK: 3-broker cluster in private subnets, TLS listener only, rack-aware placement across 3 AZs.
devops.observability_stack: Prometheus JMX exporter on Kafka Streams processor; Grafana dashboards for kafka_consumer_group_lag (alert >10000 messages), clickhouse_insert_rate (warn <50K rows/sec), waveform_end_to_end_latency_p99 (alert >5 seconds). PagerDuty for P1: consumer lag >10000, latency >5s, ClickHouse cluster unavailable.
devops.rollback_strategy: Kafka Streams processor rollback via EKS rolling update with previous image tag; ClickHouse schema changes via ALTER TABLE ADD COLUMN (non-breaking); MSK consumer group offset reset to replay missed windows if processor restarts

previous_plan.executive_summary: Java Kafka Streams pipeline ingesting 500 Hz vital sign waveforms from ICU bedside monitors to ClickHouse archive and alert consumer topic.

best_plan.title: ICU Waveform Streaming Pipeline
best_plan.executive_summary: Java Kafka Streams pipeline ingesting 500 Hz vital sign waveforms from ICU bedside monitors to ClickHouse archive and alert consumer topic.

target_output.thinking_summary: Revision 2 must close ISS-001 by specifying the ClickHouse Buffer table engine configuration for absorbing 12.5M samples/second peak insert load, and ISS-002 by documenting the device PKI certificate lifecycle (issuance on monitor onboarding, 12-month rotation, revocation on decommission). The core Kafka Streams and ClickHouse architecture from round 1 is retained. Since plan_quality is weak, some sections will be at minimum word counts but all required sections are present.

fix_report.action_taken: Added ClickHouse Buffer table engine configuration to data_model and system_components: vital_sign_samples_buffer (Buffer engine, 16 blocks, flush_time=2 seconds, buffer_size=1000000 rows, max_bytes=500MB) writing to vital_sign_samples MergeTree. Async insert setting async_insert=1, wait_for_async_insert=0 on the Kafka Streams JDBC sink. At 12.5M samples/second the buffer flushes approximately every 0.08 seconds per block, well within ClickHouse insert throughput limits of 100M+ rows/second for MergeTree.
fix_report.expected_outcome: ClickHouse inserts batch efficiently under peak waveform load; write amplification avoided; ClickHouse CPU under 60% at 12.5M samples/second

fix_report.action_taken: Added device certificate lifecycle section to security_and_compliance: device certificates issued via hospital PKI API call during monitor onboarding workflow; certificates have 12-month validity; Kubernetes CronJob checks expiry 30 days in advance and triggers renewal request; decommissioned device certificates revoked via PKI OCSP responder and Kafka ACL deletion.
fix_report.expected_outcome: No expired device certificates; decommissioned devices cannot produce to Kafka; PKI audit trail complete

target_output.title: ICU Waveform Streaming Pipeline - Revision 2
target_output.executive_summary: A high-throughput Java/Kafka Streams pipeline ingesting continuous 500 Hz vital sign waveforms from ICU bedside monitors through MSK Kafka to ClickHouse hot archive and an alert consumer statistics topic. The pipeline targets 12.5M samples/second at enterprise scale using ClickHouse Buffer table engine for write batching and async inserts. Device identity is established via mTLS with certificates managed through the hospital PKI with automated renewal and revocation.
target_output.architecture_overview: Linear streaming topology: bedside monitor integration adapters (Java 21 virtual threads) publish 1-second waveform batches to MSK Kafka topic sensor_waveforms_raw partitioned by device_id. Kafka Streams processor computes 5-minute tumbling window statistics (min/max/mean/std per channel) and publishes to sensor_statistics topic consumed by the clinical alerting service. ClickHouseArchivalSink writes raw samples via Buffer table engine to vital_sign_samples MergeTree with async inserts. Nightly Spark job exports 30-day-old ClickHouse partitions to S3 Parquet for cold storage. Network isolated from EHR VLAN via dedicated Kafka broker network interfaces on hospital internal subnet.
target_output.technology_stack: Java 21 (virtual threads, Kafka producer client), Kafka Streams 3.7, MSK Kafka 3.7 (3-broker, TLS listener), ClickHouse 24.3 (MergeTree + Buffer engine), S3 Parquet (cold storage), EKS us-east-1, Prometheus JMX exporter, Grafana, Jenkins CI, Cognito service accounts. Dead letter queue: Kafka DLQ topic for malformed waveform messages with alert on DLQ lag. Schema registry: AWS Glue Schema Registry enforcing Avro contracts for each ICU device model.
target_output.functional_feature_map: WaveformProducerAdapter: reads monitor SDK samples, batches 1-second chunks, idempotent Kafka producer. WindowedAggregationProcessor: Kafka Streams 5-min tumbling windows per channel per device. AlertStatisticsPublisher: sink to sensor_statistics topic for alerting service. ClickHouseArchivalSink: JDBC batch insert via Buffer table. NightlyExportJob: Spark exports ClickHouse partitions to S3 Parquet.
target_output.system_components: MSK Kafka cluster (3 brokers, 3 AZs, TLS listener, 64 partitions on sensor_waveforms_raw). WaveformProducerAdapter DaemonSet (EKS, one pod per ICU network segment, Java 21). Kafka Streams Deployment (3 replicas, HPA on consumer group lag). ClickHouseArchivalSink Deployment (2 replicas, async_insert=1). ClickHouse cluster (3-node MergeTree, Buffer table vital_sign_samples_buffer: 16 blocks, flush_time=2s, buffer_size=1M rows). S3 bucket icu-waveforms with Parquet lifecycle policy. Kubernetes CronJob for PKI certificate expiry check and renewal.
target_output.workflows: Waveform Ingestion: Monitor integration adapter reads ECG/SpO2/ABP samples at 500 Hz, batches into 1-second Avro records, publishes to sensor_waveforms_raw with device_id as message key and idempotent producer (enable.idempotence=true). Kafka Streams reads partitions assigned to processor instance, applies 5-minute tumbling window keyed by (device_id, channel), emits WindowedStats record to sensor_statistics topic when window closes. ClickHouseArchivalSink subscribes to sensor_waveforms_raw, batches 1M rows per flush via Buffer table engine, persists to vital_sign_samples MergeTree. Device Certificate Renewal: Kubernetes CronJob runs daily, queries PKI API for certificates expiring within 30 days, issues renewal request, updates Kubernetes secret, triggers rolling restart of affected WaveformProducerAdapter pod.
target_output.data_model: vital_sign_samples_buffer (ClickHouse Buffer engine: buffer_blocks=16, flush_time=2, buffer_size=1000000, max_bytes=524288000, source_table=vital_sign_samples). vital_sign_samples (ClickHouse MergeTree: device_id TEXT, sample_timestamp DateTime64(3), channel ENUM ecg/spo2/abp/rr/temp, value_mv Float32, sequence_number UInt64, patient_id_encrypted TEXT; ORDER BY (device_id, sample_timestamp); PARTITION BY (device_id, toYYYYMM(sample_timestamp)); TTL sample_timestamp + INTERVAL 30 DAY). waveform_archives (archive_id UUID, device_id TEXT, period_start DateTime64, period_end DateTime64, s3_parquet_key TEXT, row_count BIGINT, compressed_bytes BIGINT). alert_thresholds (device_id TEXT, channel TEXT, high_threshold Float32, low_threshold Float32, window_minutes UInt8).
target_output.api_design: No external REST API. Kafka topic interfaces: sensor_waveforms_raw (producers: WaveformProducerAdapter; consumers: Kafka Streams, ClickHouseArchivalSink), sensor_statistics (producers: Kafka Streams; consumers: clinical alerting service). MSK brokers accessible on port 9094 (TLS) within hospital internal subnet only. ClickHouse HTTP interface on port 8123 restricted to data science VLAN by security group rule. Jenkins pipeline API used for CI/CD automation.
target_output.security_and_compliance: Device mTLS: each bedside monitor integration adapter receives a client certificate from hospital PKI with CN=device_id; Kafka broker ACLs restrict each device to produce on its assigned device_id partition only; certificates have 12-month validity; Kubernetes CronJob checks expiry 30 days in advance, calls PKI API for renewal, updates Kubernetes secret. Certificate revocation: decommissioned device certificates revoked via OCSP responder; Kafka ACL deleted on revocation event. ClickHouse patient_id_encrypted uses AES-256 column encryption; plain-text patient_id never stored. HIPAA: ClickHouse query log retained 1 year for PHI access audit; S3 Parquet files encrypted at rest with SSE-S3. FDA MDDS: pipeline treated as class I medical device data system; no modification to waveform values during transit.
target_output.deployment_and_operations: EKS cluster (us-east-1): WaveformProducerAdapter DaemonSet (1 pod per ICU network switch with monitor connectivity), Kafka Streams Deployment (3 replicas, HPA threshold Kafka consumer lag >5000 messages, max 8 replicas), ClickHouseArchivalSink Deployment (2 replicas). MSK: 3-broker kafka.m5.large cluster, TLS listener only, multi-AZ placement, MSK Connect not used. ClickHouse: 3-node cluster on r5.2xlarge EKS nodes (memory-optimized for MergeTree). Jenkins CI/CD: Docker build, push to ECR, EKS rolling deploy. Environments: dev (single MSK broker, 5-device simulator), staging (MSK 3-broker, 50-device simulator), production (MSK 3-broker, pilot ICU 50 beds).
target_output.observability: Prometheus metrics: kafka_consumer_group_lag (alert >10000), clickhouse_insert_rate_rows_per_second (warn <50K), waveform_latency_device_to_statistics_p99_seconds (alert >5.0), clickhouse_buffer_flush_count (track batching efficiency). Grafana dashboards: Waveform Throughput (samples ingested per second per channel), Consumer Lag Heatmap (per partition), ClickHouse Insert Performance (buffer flush intervals). PagerDuty P1 alerts: consumer lag >10000, end-to-end latency >5 seconds, ClickHouse cluster node failure.
target_output.cost_and_scaling: MSK kafka.m5.large 3-broker: approximately $1200/month. EKS compute for streaming pods: approximately $800/month. ClickHouse 3x r5.2xlarge: approximately $2500/month staying within $4K budget at pilot scale. S3 cold storage: $100/month for compressed Parquet archive. Enterprise scale to 5000 ICU devices requires MSK scale-out to kafka.m5.4xlarge and ClickHouse horizontal scaling.
target_output.phased_implementation: Phase 1 Pilot (10 weeks): Deploy MSK and EKS for 50-bed ICU, instrument 50 bedside monitors with WaveformProducerAdapter, validate ClickHouse Buffer table insert throughput at pilot device density, confirm <5 second end-to-end latency. Phase 2 Enterprise Rollout: Expand to all hospital ICUs, automate device PKI onboarding workflow, scale MSK and ClickHouse cluster nodes.
target_output.development_guidelines: Kafka producer configured with enable.idempotence=true and acks=all to prevent duplicate waveform segments. ClickHouse async_insert=1 and wait_for_async_insert=0 on archival sink; monitor buffer flush metrics in Grafana. Jenkins CI runs unit tests for waveform batch serialization and Kafka Streams window state logic. EKS pod anti-affinity ensures Kafka Streams replicas are on separate nodes.
target_output.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: ClickHouse node failure during active inserts may lose buffered samples not yet flushed to MergeTree; accepted for healthcare IoT where occasional waveform segment loss is tolerable but clinical alerting remains independent via Kafka statistics topic. Risk: 12.5M samples/second at enterprise scale exceeds single ClickHouse cluster; mitigation is horizontal ClickHouse sharding by device_id range. Tradeoff: ClickHouse Buffer engine reduces insert pressure but adds 2-second flush latency to archival; alert consumer reads from sensor_statistics Kafka topic (not ClickHouse) so clinical alerting SLA is unaffected.
target_output.open_questions_resolved: ClickHouse Buffer table selected over Kafka Streams ClickHouse connector for simpler throughput management. 64 Kafka partitions sufficient for 50-bed pilot; expandable to 512 without consumer group reset. S3 Parquet export via Spark batch job preferred over ClickHouse S3 table function for schema evolution flexibility. mTLS certificate CN=device_id confirmed with hospital PKI team as compatible with existing infrastructure.

metadata.notes: ICU waveform streaming pipeline; weak quality row with thinner section details; revision 2 addresses ClickHouse insert throughput and device certificate lifecycle.

profile.domain: Healthcare IT Infrastructure (Epic EHR Integration Migration)

project_goal.value: Migrate a regional hospital's on-premises Epic EHR integration engine and associated middleware from physical data center hardware to a cloud-native Kubernetes environment on Azure, improving disaster recovery posture and reducing data center footprint
project_goal.rationale: On-premises hardware reaching end-of-life; Azure migration improves RTO from 4 hours to 15 minutes

target_users.value: Clinical integration engineers maintaining HL7 interfaces; IT operations teams managing infrastructure; downstream clinical systems consuming HL7 feeds
target_users.rationale: Infrastructure project; end users are technical operators not clinicians

project_class.rationale: Platform and environment architecture migration; no new application features

capabilities.rationale: Container orchestration, HL7 interface containerization, message queue migration, and data migration are in scope

complexity_level.rationale: 200+ active HL7 interfaces, mission-critical clinical systems, zero-downtime cutover requirement

risk_level.rationale: Any message delivery failure during migration could disrupt lab results, ADT feeds, or medication orders to clinical systems

data_sensitivity.rationale: HL7 messages in the integration engine contain PHI including patient demographics, lab results, and medication orders

external_exposure.rationale: Integration engine interfaces are hospital-internal; no internet-facing HL7 endpoints

access_model.value: Azure Active Directory for AKS cluster access; Keycloak for integration engine admin console; service accounts per interface with RBAC namespace isolation in Kubernetes
access_model.rationale: Identity-based access replaces VPN-only access to on-premises engine admin consoles

feature_scope.value: Containerization of all 200+ HL7 interfaces as Kubernetes Deployments, PostgreSQL message queue migration to Azure Database for PostgreSQL, Terraform infrastructure-as-code for AKS environment, parallel-run phase with reconciliation tooling, automated smoke tests per interface, DR failover playbook
feature_scope.rationale: Full migration scope from on-premises to cloud-native

mvp_scope.value: Pilot migration of 20 low-criticality interfaces, AKS cluster provisioning, Terraform base infrastructure, automated reconciliation comparing on-prem and cloud message counts
mvp_scope.rationale: 20 low-criticality interfaces validate the containerization pattern before mission-critical interface migration

security_baseline.value: TLS 1.3 for all HL7 TCP connections in cloud, Azure Key Vault for secrets, AKS network policies restricting pod-to-pod traffic, AAD workload identity for Azure resource access
security_baseline.rationale: Zero-trust posture replaces perimeter-based on-premises model

frontend_stack.value: None for the migration infrastructure; integration engine admin console is part of existing middleware product not being rebuilt
frontend_stack.rationale: Infrastructure migration; no new UI development

backend_stack.value: Python 3.12 with Terraform 1.8 and Helm 3.14 for infrastructure automation; Azure DevOps pipelines for CI/CD
backend_stack.rationale: Terraform for AKS and Azure resource provisioning; Helm for interface container deployments

data_platform.value: Azure Database for PostgreSQL Flexible Server (message queues and interface state); Azure Blob Storage for HL7 message archives
data_platform.rationale: Managed PostgreSQL eliminates database administration overhead; Azure Blob replaces on-premises NAS for message archival

hosting_target.value: AKS (Azure Kubernetes Service) in East US region; Azure Container Registry for interface container images
hosting_target.rationale: AKS provides managed Kubernetes with AAD integration and HIPAA BAA coverage under Azure healthcare compliance

privacy_retention_policy.value: HL7 message archives retained 7 years in Azure Blob cold tier; PostgreSQL transaction logs retained 30 days; audit logs for all PHI-bearing message access retained 7 years
privacy_retention_policy.rationale: HIPAA minimum retention for electronic PHI; Joint Commission IT record requirements

future_scope.value: FHIR facade layer over integration engine for modern application interoperability; multi-region AKS for active-active DR; Mirth Connect replacement with cloud-native integration platform
future_scope.rationale: Post-migration modernization roadmap

constraints.value: Zero tolerance for HL7 message loss during cutover; each interface must be validated individually before cutover; RTO target 15 minutes from Terraform state; cutover windows restricted to Saturday 2-6 AM
constraints.rationale: Mission-critical clinical system availability and Joint Commission requirements

observability_baseline.value: Azure Monitor + Grafana for AKS cluster metrics and HL7 interface throughput; custom Prometheus metrics per interface (messages_processed_total, message_delivery_latency_p99, nack_count); PagerDuty for critical interface alerts
observability_baseline.rationale: Integration engine observability must meet clinical operations SLA monitoring requirements

execution_preference.value: Interface-by-interface cutover during approved Saturday maintenance windows; parallel-run each batch before cutover
execution_preference.rationale: Incremental cutover reduces blast radius; parallel-run validates message fidelity before each cutover

compliance_context.value: HIPAA Security Rule contingency planning (45 CFR 164.308(a)(7)); Joint Commission IT system availability standards; BAA required with Microsoft Azure for PHI in cloud
compliance_context.rationale: Healthcare ePHI in cloud requires HIPAA-covered cloud with signed BAA

project.goal: Migrate 200+ HL7 interfaces from on-premises integration engine to AKS with zero message loss and 15-minute DR RTO
project.target_users: Integration engineers, IT operations, downstream clinical systems
project.timeline: Pilot 20 interfaces in 8 weeks; full 200+ interface migration in 6 months

backend.framework: Terraform 1.8 for AKS infrastructure; Helm 3.14 for interface deployments
backend.automation: Azure DevOps pipelines for Terraform plan/apply and Helm upgrade

security.auth: AAD workload identity for AKS pods; Azure Key Vault for secrets
security.encryption: TLS 1.3 for HL7 TCP; PostgreSQL SSL; Azure Blob encryption at rest
security.compliance: HIPAA BAA with Azure; HIPAA Security Rule contingency planning

data.db: Azure Database for PostgreSQL Flexible Server
data.cache: Redis 7 (Azure Cache for Redis) for interface session state

devops.hosting: AKS East US; ACR for container images

constraints.availability: Zero HL7 message loss during cutover

open_questions.interface_prioritization: Classification of 200+ interfaces by criticality for cutover sequencing

confirmed_decisions.helm_chart_strategy: One Helm chart template parameterized per interface

reasoner_reviews.product: The interface-by-interface cutover strategy with parallel-run validation is the right risk management approach for mission-critical clinical HL7 interfaces. Criticality classification (ADT feeds, lab results, medication orders as tier 1; clinical documentation and reporting as tier 2) should drive the cutover sequence with tier 1 interfaces migrated last after full process validation.
reasoner_reviews.architect_reasoner: One parameterized Helm chart per interface type (TCP listener, MLLP sender, database poller) is the correct abstraction; avoid creating 200 unique Helm charts. AKS namespace-per-integration-domain (lab, pharmacy, radiology, ADT) provides security boundary isolation and simplifies network policy rules. Azure Database for PostgreSQL Flexible Server with zone-redundant HA eliminates the single-point-of-failure that existed on-premises.
reasoner_reviews.security: AAD workload identity (replacing deprecated pod identity) enables each interface pod to access Azure Key Vault without credential secrets in Kubernetes. Network policies in AKS must restrict HL7 message content to namespace-scoped pods only. Azure Defender for Containers should be enabled for runtime threat detection on integration engine pods.
reasoner_reviews.constraints: 15-minute RTO from Terraform state requires pre-provisioned AKS node pools rather than just-in-time provisioning. Automated smoke tests per interface (send test HL7 ADT^A01, verify ACK received, check downstream system message delivery) must be documented in the DR playbook and runnable without human intervention. Saturday 2-6 AM windows are 4 hours; each batch of interfaces must complete parallel-run validation, cutover, and smoke test within that window.

backend.service_design: One Helm chart template (interface-deployment) parameterized by interface_id, protocol (TCP/MLLP/DB), direction (inbound/outbound), source_system, target_system, port, queue_table; deployed as Kubernetes Deployment with 2 replicas and pod disruption budget for rolling updates
backend.business_modules: InterfaceContainerizer (Python script: reads on-prem interface config, generates Helm values.yaml per interface, outputs to Git-managed interface-configs repo), ReconciliationJob (Python: compares on-prem and cloud message counts per interface per hour during parallel-run, alerts on >0.1% discrepancy), CutoverOrchestrator (Python: drains on-prem interface, waits for queue empty, enables cloud interface, verifies first cloud ACK within 60 seconds), DRSmokeTester (Python: sends synthetic HL7 ADT^A01 per interface, validates ACK, checks downstream delivery)
backend.failure_handling: Interface pod crash loop triggers PagerDuty P1; PodDisruptionBudget ensures at least 1 replica running during node drain; PostgreSQL message queue provides durable message store so in-flight messages survive pod restart

security.auth_design: AAD workload identity annotations on each interface Deployment ServiceAccount; Azure Key Vault access policy grants each interface SA read access only to its own secrets (connection strings, certificates); no cross-interface secret access
security.secrets_management: All HL7 endpoint connection strings, port numbers, and SSL certificates stored in Azure Key Vault per-interface secret hierarchy; Kubernetes external-secrets-operator syncs Key Vault secrets to Kubernetes secrets on rotation; no plaintext credentials in Helm values.yaml or Terraform tfvars
security.audit_and_logging_controls: HL7 message metadata (MSH segment: message_type, patient_id_hash, timestamp) logged to PostgreSQL audit table per interface; Azure Monitor Diagnostic Settings export AKS control plane logs to Log Analytics workspace retained 7 years

data.entities: integration_interfaces (interface_id TEXT PK, display_name TEXT, protocol TEXT, direction TEXT, source_system TEXT, target_system TEXT, criticality_tier INT, status ENUM on_prem/parallel_run/cloud/decommissioned, last_cutover_at TIMESTAMPTZ), message_queues (queue_id UUID PK, interface_id TEXT, message_type TEXT, hl7_content BYTEA_encrypted, received_at TIMESTAMPTZ, delivered_at TIMESTAMPTZ, status ENUM pending/delivered/failed/archived), migration_cutover_plans (plan_id UUID PK, batch_name TEXT, interface_ids TEXT[], scheduled_window TIMESTAMPTZ, status ENUM planned/in_progress/completed/rolled_back, executed_by TEXT, notes TEXT), disaster_recovery_runbooks (runbook_id UUID PK, version TEXT, steps JSONB, last_tested_at TIMESTAMPTZ, rto_minutes INT)
data.storage_design: Azure Database for PostgreSQL Flexible Server: zone-redundant HA, geo-redundant backup; message_queues table stores in-flight HL7 messages encrypted at column level. Azure Blob Storage: hl7-archives container stores delivered messages in gzip format, lifecycle tier to cool after 30 days, archive after 1 year, delete after 7 years. ACR: container images for each interface type tagged by interface_id and git commit SHA.
data.migration_notes: On-premises PostgreSQL schema exported with pg_dump, restored to Azure PostgreSQL via Azure Data Migration Service; message queue tables migrated with minimal downtime using logical replication before cutover window

devops.deployment_topology: AKS cluster: system node pool (Standard_D4s_v3, 3 nodes, across 3 AZs), interface node pool (Standard_D2s_v3, 5-20 nodes, HPA enabled); namespaces: lab-integration, pharmacy-integration, radiology-integration, adt-integration; each namespace has NetworkPolicy restricting ingress to hospital VLAN CIDR only
devops.ci_cd_design: Azure DevOps pipeline stages: Terraform plan (PR gate), Terraform apply (main branch auto), Helm lint + dry-run (per interface), Helm upgrade --install (rolling deploy). DRSmokeTester runs as post-deploy gate; if smoke test fails, Helm rollback triggered automatically
devops.rollback_strategy: On cutover failure: CutoverOrchestrator re-enables on-prem interface within 60 seconds; cloud interface scaled to 0 replicas; incident opened in ServiceNow; parallel-run resumes. Terraform state rollback: previous tfstate stored in Azure Blob versioned container

ISS-001.title: DR smoke test automation not specified
ISS-001.detail: Round 1 plan described a 15-minute RTO from Terraform state but did not define the automated smoke test suite that validates HL7 connectivity to all downstream systems before traffic is redirected. Without automated smoke tests the 15-minute RTO is not achievable with manual verification.

history.note: DR reviewer noted that manual smoke test validation of 200+ interfaces cannot complete in 15 minutes

ISS-002.title: Parallel-run reconciliation tooling not defined
ISS-002.detail: The parallel-run validation approach was described but no specification of how message count reconciliation works between on-prem and cloud environments; discrepancy thresholds, alerting, and decision criteria for cutover were absent from round 1 plan.

history.note: Integration engineering lead required concrete reconciliation spec before approving migration plan

focus_issues.title: DR smoke test automation not specified
focus_issues.detail: 15-minute RTO claims not achievable without automated per-interface smoke tests; manual verification of 200+ interfaces impossible in 15 minutes.

focus_issues.title: Parallel-run reconciliation tooling not defined
focus_issues.detail: Message count comparison methodology, discrepancy thresholds, and cutover go/no-go criteria not specified.

latest_recommendations: Define automated DRSmokeTester: per-interface synthetic HL7 test message, ACK validation, downstream delivery check, parallelized to complete in <5 minutes for all 200+ interfaces
Specify ReconciliationJob: hourly message count comparison on-prem vs. cloud, >0.1% discrepancy triggers alert, >0.5% discrepancy blocks cutover decision

issue_updates.title: DR smoke test automation
issue_updates.detail: 15-minute RTO requires automated per-interface smoke tests; plan described intent but no implementation

issue_updates.title: Parallel-run reconciliation
issue_updates.detail: Reconciliation described abstractly without tooling, thresholds, or go/no-go criteria

previous_plan.title: Epic EHR Integration Engine Migration to AKS
previous_plan.executive_summary: Terraform/AKS migration of 200+ HL7 interfaces with Helm-parameterized deployments, parallel-run validation, and 15-minute DR RTO from Terraform state.
previous_plan.architecture_overview: AKS cluster with namespace-per-domain; Helm chart template per interface; Azure PostgreSQL for message queues; parallel-run approach with manual reconciliation. DR smoke tests not automated; reconciliation tooling not defined.
previous_plan.deployment_and_operations: Azure DevOps CI/CD; Terraform for AKS provisioning; Helm upgrade for interface deploys. 15-minute RTO target stated but smoke test automation absent.
previous_plan.data_model: integration_interfaces, message_queues, migration_cutover_plans. Reconciliation tooling not specified.

best_plan.title: Epic EHR Integration Engine Migration to AKS
best_plan.executive_summary: Terraform/AKS migration of 200+ HL7 interfaces with Helm-parameterized deployments, parallel-run validation, and 15-minute DR RTO target.

target_output.thinking_summary: Revision 2 closes ISS-001 by specifying the DRSmokeTester automation (parallelized Python script sending synthetic HL7 ADT^A01 per interface, validating ACK within 10 seconds, checking downstream delivery via MSH-9 query, completing 200+ interfaces in <5 minutes) and ISS-002 by defining the ReconciliationJob with hourly message count comparison, >0.1% threshold triggering alert, >0.5% blocking cutover decision. Core Terraform/AKS/Helm architecture from round 1 is retained. This is a strong-quality revision with detailed operability sections.

fix_report.action_taken: Added DRSmokeTester specification to deployment_and_operations, workflows, and system_components: Python asyncio script sends synthetic HL7 ADT^A01 message to each interface endpoint in parallel (asyncio.gather with 50 concurrent tasks), validates MLLP ACK within 10 seconds, queries downstream system message delivery table via PostgreSQL, reports per-interface pass/fail within 4 minutes for 200 interfaces. DRSmokeTester is the post-Terraform-apply gate in the DR playbook and the post-cutover validation step in the CutoverOrchestrator.
fix_report.expected_outcome: 15-minute RTO achievable: Terraform apply in <10 minutes + DRSmokeTester in <4 minutes = <14 minutes total; no manual verification required

fix_report.action_taken: Defined ReconciliationJob in system_components and workflows: Python script runs hourly via Kubernetes CronJob, queries on-prem integration engine message_log table via VPN-accessible PostgreSQL and cloud PostgreSQL message_queues table, compares counts per interface_id per hour, alerts via PagerDuty if discrepancy >0.1%, sets migration_cutover_plans.go_nogo_status='blocked' in PostgreSQL if any interface shows >0.5% discrepancy in the 24-hour pre-cutover window. CutoverOrchestrator checks go_nogo_status before proceeding.
fix_report.expected_outcome: Cutover decisions are data-driven; any message loss during parallel-run is detected before cutover proceeds

target_output.title: Epic EHR Integration Engine AKS Migration - Revision 2
