The diversion advisory threshold configuration ownership is unresolved - recommend hospital administrator controls the threshold with bed coordinator view-only, since a premature diversion advisory has patient safety implications. The housekeeping task notification feature has significant operational value (turning dirty beds faster improves throughput) but is correctly deferred to Phase 2 given the scope constraints.
reasoner_reviews.architect_reasoner: Go with gorilla/websocket is the right choice for 500 concurrent WebSocket connections - a Node.js alternative would require more careful memory management. The Redis pub/sub fan-out for multi-instance WebSocket events is essential for ECS horizontal scaling - without it, WebSocket connections on different Fargate tasks cannot receive events from ADT processing on other instances. The ADT HL7 listener should be a separate ECS service from the main API to isolate HL7 parsing failures from the web application. PostgreSQL LISTEN/NOTIFY or Redis pub/sub for ADT event distribution to the WebSocket hub - Redis pub/sub is preferred given existing Redis dependency.
reasoner_reviews.security: Patient placement data in the bed grid shows patient names and room assignments - this is clearly PHI. All WebSocket messages containing patient placement data must be validated against the user's role before dispatch: charge nurses should only receive updates for their assigned unit's beds. CloudFront must have caching disabled for all API responses containing patient data. Cognito JWT expiry at 1 hour is appropriate; WebSocket connection should re-validate the JWT on reconnect rather than keeping a long-lived unauthenticated socket.
reasoner_reviews.constraints: 500 concurrent WebSocket connections is achievable on 2-3 Go Fargate tasks with gorilla/websocket. The 5-second ADT event propagation latency budget breaks down as: Epic ADT transmission <1s, HL7 listener processing <0.5s, Redis pub/sub fan-out <0.1s, WebSocket dispatch to all clients <0.5s - leaving 3s buffer. PostgreSQL write for bed state update should not be in the critical path for the 5-second SLA; write asynchronously after WebSocket dispatch.

frontend.app_structure: React 18 SPA with React Router v6. Top-level routes: /dashboard (campus bed map), /units/:unitId (unit bed grid), /placements (patient placement queue), /analytics (occupancy trends), /admin/thresholds (diversion configuration). WebSocket connection managed as Zustand store singleton; reconnection with exponential backoff. React Query for all REST data fetching with 30-second stale time for non-realtime data.
frontend.pages_and_flows: CampusBedMap: SVG-based hospital floor plan per campus with bed slots colored by status (green/occupied, gray/available, yellow/dirty, red/blocked, orange/discrepancy). Clicking a bed opens BedDetailPanel with patient placement summary. UnitBedGrid: tabular view of all beds in a clinical_unit with last ADT event timestamp and patient flow metrics. PlacementQueue: ordered list of patients awaiting bed assignment with admission diagnosis, requested unit, and wait time. OccupancyTrendDashboard: Recharts LineChart of census_snapshots over time by unit and campus, with 7-day rolling occupancy rate.
frontend.state_management: Zustand WebSocketStore: connection status, last_event_timestamp, reconnect_attempt_count. React Query BedStatusQuery: fetches initial bed state on mount, WebSocket events update React Query cache directly via queryClient.setQueryData for optimistic UI updates. No Redux; React Query + Zustand sufficient for this data pattern.
frontend.performance_notes: Bed grid re-renders optimized with React.memo on BedCell component; only cells with changed status re-render on WebSocket update. SVG campus map uses canvas fallback for >500 bed campuses. Recharts occupancy charts use data windowing for the 2-year trend dataset to avoid rendering 700K data points.

backend.service_design: Two ECS services: CapacityAPI (Go Gin) and AdtListenerService (Go, separate container). CapacityAPI hosts WebSocket hub, REST endpoints, and occupancy analytics queries. AdtListenerService runs HL7 MLLP server on port 2575, parses Epic ADT messages, updates bed state in PostgreSQL, publishes BedStatusEvent to Redis pub/sub channel capacity:bed-events. WebSocketHub subscribes to Redis channel and fans out to all connected WebSocket clients.
backend.api_patterns: WebSocket: ws://api/ws - client sends {type:auth, token:JWT} on connect; server validates and registers connection with unit scope. Server pushes {type:bed_update, bed_id, status, patient_id_hash, timestamp} messages. REST: GET /api/v1/campuses/:id/beds (initial bed state load), GET /api/v1/units/:id/beds (unit-level snapshot), GET /api/v1/placements?status=pending (placement queue), GET /api/v1/analytics/occupancy?campus=&start=&end= (census trend data), POST /api/v1/placements/:id/assign-bed (bed assignment action), GET /api/v1/admin/thresholds (diversion config).
backend.business_modules: BedStateManager: processes ADT A01 (admission -> bed occupied), A02 (transfer -> source bed available, target bed occupied), A03 (discharge -> bed dirty), A08 (update -> bed status change). DiversionAdvisoryEngine: evaluates campus occupancy against threshold after each ADT event; triggers diversion_advisory record and WebSocket notification to administrators when threshold exceeded. OccupancySnapshotJob: scheduled every 15 minutes, writes capacity_snapshots record for each campus/unit for trend analytics.
backend.scaling_notes: gorilla/websocket Hub manages client registry as map[*Client]bool with sync.RWMutex. Redis pub/sub subscription per CapacityAPI instance; each instance fans out Redis messages to its own registered WebSocket clients. Horizontal scaling to 3 ECS tasks supports 1,500 concurrent connections.

security.auth_design: Cognito User Pools with SAML IdP integration to hospital Active Directory. React frontend uses Cognito Hosted UI for login redirect flow. ID token passed as Authorization header to API and as query param for initial WebSocket upgrade (ws://api/ws?token=JWT). Go API validates Cognito JWT against JWKS endpoint on each WebSocket auth message and each REST request.
security.authorization_model: Role claims in Cognito ID token: bed_coordinator (all campuses), charge_nurse (unit_ids[] claim), administrator (all campuses + analytics write), transfer_coordinator (read-only, no patient names - only anonymized placement counts). WebSocket server filters bed_update messages to charge_nurse clients by their unit_ids claim to prevent cross-unit PHI disclosure.
security.abuse_prevention: CloudFront WAF rule: hospital CIDR allowlist blocks external access. ALB security group: only CloudFront origin IPs for HTTPS, only ADT listener on port 2575 from Epic integration CIDR. Rate limiting: 100 REST req/min per Cognito user. WebSocket: max 5 simultaneous connections per user.

data.entities: campuses (id UUID PK, name VARCHAR, city VARCHAR, total_licensed_beds SMALLINT). clinical_units (id UUID PK, campus_id FK, unit_name VARCHAR, unit_type ENUM icu/med_surg/ed/telemetry/obs, total_beds SMALLINT, diversion_threshold_pct NUMERIC). beds (id UUID PK, unit_id FK, bed_label VARCHAR, room_number VARCHAR, bed_status ENUM occupied/available/dirty/blocked, last_status_change TIMESTAMPTZ, last_adt_event_type VARCHAR). patient_placements (id UUID PK, bed_id FK, patient_id_hash CHAR(64), admission_diagnosis VARCHAR, requested_unit_id FK, assigned_at TIMESTAMPTZ, discharged_at TIMESTAMPTZ, placement_status ENUM pending/assigned/admitted/discharged). capacity_snapshots (id UUID PK, campus_id FK, unit_id FK, snapshot_at TIMESTAMPTZ, occupied_count SMALLINT, available_count SMALLINT, dirty_count SMALLINT, occupancy_pct NUMERIC). transfer_requests (id UUID PK, patient_id_hash FK, origin_campus_id FK, destination_campus_id FK, request_status ENUM pending/approved/transferred/cancelled, created_at TIMESTAMPTZ). housekeeping_tasks (id UUID PK, bed_id FK, assigned_to VARCHAR, status ENUM pending/in_progress/completed, created_at TIMESTAMPTZ, completed_at TIMESTAMPTZ).
data.storage_design: PostgreSQL 16 RDS Multi-AZ (us-east-1) for all relational data. Redis 7 ElastiCache cluster: two uses - (1) pub/sub channel capacity:bed-events for ADT event fan-out to WebSocket hubs, (2) hash cache of current bed statuses keyed by bed_id for fast initial connection snapshot load. capacity_snapshots partitioned by month for 2-year analytics retention.
data.analytics_events: OccupancySnapshotJob writes capacity_snapshots every 15 minutes per unit. DiversionAdvisory events written to diversion_advisories table when threshold crossed. ADT event counts per hour per campus written to adt_event_metrics for feed health monitoring.
data.data_risks: Epic ADT feed interruption leaves bed status stale indefinitely without detection. Mitigation: adt_event_metrics tracks last_event_at per campus; ADT health check alerts if no events received in 10-minute window.

devops.deployment_topology: Two ECS Fargate services in us-east-1: CapacityAPI (target 2-5 tasks, ALB target group) and AdtListenerService (1 task, NLB for HL7 MLLP TCP port 2575). CloudFront distribution for React SPA from S3 origin with WAF hospital IP allowlist. ALB with WAF for CapacityAPI HTTPS traffic. ElastiCache Redis cluster and RDS PostgreSQL Multi-AZ in private subnets.
devops.ci_cd_design: GitHub Actions: go test with 80% coverage, go vet, staticcheck linter, Docker build and ECR push for both services. React: npm test, npm run build, S3 sync for CloudFront. ECS service update via rolling deployment (minimum 50% healthy). Database migrations via golang-migrate run as ECS one-off task before service rollout.
devops.observability_stack: Datadog APM Go tracer on CapacityAPI and AdtListenerService. React Datadog Browser SDK for frontend RUM. Custom Datadog metrics: websocket.connections.active gauge, adt.events.processed_per_minute counter, adt.feed.last_event_age_seconds gauge per campus, bed.status_update.propagation_latency_ms histogram. PagerDuty: adt.feed.last_event_age_seconds >600 (10-min no ADT events), websocket.connections.active drops >50% in 1 minute.

target_output.thinking_summary: This is a real-time web application where the 5-second ADT event propagation SLA drives the entire architecture. The Go WebSocket hub with Redis pub/sub fan-out is the correct pattern for multi-instance ECS horizontal scaling - each Fargate task subscribes to the same Redis channel and fans out to its own connected clients. Separating the ADT listener into its own ECS service isolates HL7 parsing failures from the web application. The key design decision is that PostgreSQL writes for bed state updates happen asynchronously after WebSocket dispatch to keep them out of the 5-second SLA critical path. Role-scoped WebSocket filtering for charge nurses is a security requirement, not a nice-to-have, given that unit bed data includes patient names.
target_output.title: Hospital Capacity Management Platform
target_output.executive_summary: React 18 and Go 1.22 web application delivering real-time bed availability visualization and patient flow coordination across a 3-campus health system. Live bed status updates propagate from Epic ADT HL7 events to all connected bed coordinators within 5 seconds via a Go WebSocket hub with Redis pub/sub fan-out across ECS Fargate instances. Cognito User Pools with hospital SAML federation provides role-scoped access: charge nurses see only their assigned unit beds, administrators see all campuses. Occupancy trend analytics serve capacity planning from 2 years of 15-minute capacity snapshots. Diversion advisory automation triggers when campus occupancy crosses configurable thresholds.
target_output.architecture_overview: React 18 SPA served via CloudFront from S3, communicating via HTTPS REST and WebSocket to a Go 1.22 Gin API on ECS Fargate behind an ALB with CloudFront WAF (hospital CIDR allowlist). A separate AdtListenerService ECS task runs a Go HL7 MLLP server receiving Epic ADT A01/A02/A03/A08 messages on port 2575 via an NLB. AdtListenerService parses HL7 messages, updates bed state in PostgreSQL 16, and publishes BedStatusEvent to Redis pub/sub channel capacity:bed-events. Each CapacityAPI Fargate task subscribes to this Redis channel and fans out events via WebSocket to its registered clients, enabling horizontal scaling without sticky sessions. Redis also caches current bed status hashes for fast initial WebSocket connection snapshot. Cognito validates JWT on WebSocket auth message and each REST request; WebSocket server filters bed_update events by role (charge_nurse clients receive only their unit_ids).
target_output.technology_stack: Frontend: React 18 TypeScript, React Query v5, Zustand 4.5 for WebSocket state, React Router v6, Recharts 2.12 for occupancy trend charts, Datadog Browser SDK for RUM. Backend: Go 1.22 with Gin 1.9, gorilla/websocket 1.5 for WebSocket hub, GORM v2 with lib/pq for PostgreSQL, go-redis/redis v9 for Redis pub/sub. ADT Listener: Go 1.22 with HL7apy-equivalent Go hl7 package for MLLP parsing. Database: PostgreSQL 16 RDS Multi-AZ. Cache/Pub-Sub: Redis 7 ElastiCache. Auth: Cognito User Pools with SAML IdP, lestrrat-go/jwx v2 for Go JWT validation. Infrastructure: ECS Fargate, ALB with WAF, CloudFront with WAF, S3, NLB for MLLP, ECR. Observability: Datadog APM Go tracer and Browser SDK.
target_output.functional_feature_map: Live Campus Bed Map: SVG-based floor plan per campus; beds color-coded by status (occupied/available/dirty/blocked); updates via WebSocket within 5 seconds of ADT event; bed detail panel shows patient placement summary for authorized roles. Unit Bed Grid: tabular bed list for a clinical_unit with current status, last ADT event type and timestamp, time-in-status, and running occupancy percentage. Patient Placement Queue: pending patient placements ordered by wait time with admission diagnosis and requested unit; bed assignment action triggers ADT write-back to Epic. Occupancy Trend Dashboard: Recharts LineChart of capacity_snapshots over selectable 7/30/90/365-day windows by campus and unit; 7-day rolling average overlay. Diversion Advisory: DiversionAdvisoryEngine evaluates campus occupancy after every ADT event; auto-generates advisory record and pushes DivertingNow WebSocket notification to administrators when occupancy exceeds configurable threshold. Configurable threshold managed by administrator role in /admin/thresholds.
target_output.system_components: CloudFront Distribution: React SPA delivery from S3 origin, hospital CIDR WAF allowlist, caching disabled for /api/* paths. ALB with WAF: HTTPS termination for CapacityAPI, CloudFront origin IP restriction on security group. CapacityAPI (ECS Fargate 2-5 tasks): Gin REST API, WebSocket Hub (gorilla/websocket + Redis sub), DiversionAdvisoryEngine, OccupancySnapshotJob (15-min cron via go-cron). AdtListenerService (ECS Fargate 1 task): HL7 MLLP TCP server on port 2575, BedStateManager parsing ADT A01/A02/A03/A08, Redis pub/sub publisher for capacity:bed-events. NLB: TCP passthrough for MLLP port 2575 from Epic ADT feed IP range. Redis 7 ElastiCache cluster: pub/sub for ADT event fan-out and bed status hash cache. PostgreSQL 16 RDS Multi-AZ: primary data store for beds, patient_placements, capacity_snapshots, clinical_units. S3: React SPA static assets. Cognito User Pool: SAML IdP integration, role-mapped group claims.
target_output.workflows: ADT Event to Bed Status Update: Epic sends HL7 A03 (discharge) MLLP message to NLB port 2575. AdtListenerService parses message, extracts PID/PV1 segments, identifies bed_id from room/bed fields. BedStateManager updates beds.bed_status to dirty in PostgreSQL. Publishes {type:bed_update, bed_id, status:dirty, unit_id, timestamp} to Redis channel capacity:bed-events. Each CapacityAPI Fargate task's WebSocket Hub goroutine receives the pub/sub message. Hub iterates registered client connections, filters by role (charge_nurse receives update only if unit_id in their unit_ids claim), and writes WebSocket message to each eligible client goroutine. Total propagation target: <5 seconds from Epic sending ADT to React bed grid cell updating status. PostgreSQL OccupancySnapshotJob runs asynchronously, not in the ADT processing path. Diversion Advisory Trigger: DiversionAdvisoryEngine goroutine processes BedStatusEvents from Redis. After each event, computes current occupied_count / total_beds for each campus. If occupancy_pct > clinical_units.diversion_threshold_pct, writes diversion_advisories record and publishes {type:diversion_advisory, campus_id, occupancy_pct, threshold_pct} to Redis. CapacityAPI WebSocket Hub dispatches to all administrator-role and bed_coordinator-role connections. Administrator reviews in dashboard alert panel and initiates diversion communication to EMS.
target_output.data_model: campuses (id UUID PK, name VARCHAR, city VARCHAR, total_licensed_beds SMALLINT, created_at TIMESTAMPTZ). clinical_units (id UUID PK, campus_id UUID FK, unit_name VARCHAR, unit_type ENUM icu/med_surg/ed/telemetry/obs, total_beds SMALLINT, diversion_threshold_pct NUMERIC(4,1) DEFAULT 95.0). beds (id UUID PK, unit_id UUID FK, bed_label VARCHAR(10), room_number VARCHAR(10), bed_status ENUM occupied/available/dirty/blocked, last_status_change TIMESTAMPTZ, last_adt_event_type VARCHAR(3), last_adt_received_at TIMESTAMPTZ). patient_placements (id UUID PK, bed_id UUID FK, patient_id_hash CHAR(64), admission_diagnosis VARCHAR, requested_unit_id UUID FK, placement_status ENUM pending/assigned/admitted/discharged, assigned_at TIMESTAMPTZ, admitted_at TIMESTAMPTZ, discharged_at TIMESTAMPTZ). capacity_snapshots (id UUID PK, campus_id UUID FK, unit_id UUID FK, snapshot_at TIMESTAMPTZ, occupied_count SMALLINT, available_count SMALLINT, dirty_count SMALLINT, blocked_count SMALLINT, occupancy_pct NUMERIC(4,1)) PARTITION BY RANGE (snapshot_at). transfer_requests (id UUID PK, patient_id_hash CHAR(64), origin_campus_id UUID FK, destination_campus_id UUID FK, transfer_reason VARCHAR, request_status ENUM pending/approved/transferred/cancelled, created_at TIMESTAMPTZ). diversion_advisories (id UUID PK, campus_id UUID FK, triggered_at TIMESTAMPTZ, occupancy_pct_at_trigger NUMERIC, threshold_pct NUMERIC, resolved_at TIMESTAMPTZ, resolved_by VARCHAR).
target_output.api_design: WebSocket ws://api/ws: client sends {type:'auth',token:'JWT'} on connect; server validates Cognito JWT and registers connection with role and unit_ids claims. Server pushes: {type:'bed_update',bed_id,status,unit_id,campus_id,timestamp}, {type:'diversion_advisory',campus_id,occupancy_pct,threshold_pct}, {type:'adt_feed_health',campus_id,last_event_age_seconds}. REST (all require Authorization: Bearer JWT): GET /api/v1/campuses/:id/snapshot returns current bed status grid for all units (initial load). GET /api/v1/units/:id/beds returns unit-level bed list with patient placement summaries. GET /api/v1/placements?status=pending returns placement queue. POST /api/v1/placements/:id/assign body {bed_id} assigns bed to pending placement. GET /api/v1/analytics/occupancy?campus_id=&unit_id=&start=&end=&granularity=15m|1h|1d returns capacity_snapshots time series. GET /api/v1/admin/thresholds returns per-unit diversion thresholds. PATCH /api/v1/admin/thresholds/:unit_id body {threshold_pct} updates threshold (administrator role required).
target_output.security_and_compliance: Cognito User Pools with hospital Active Directory SAML federation. React app uses Cognito Hosted UI with PKCE flow; ID token stored in memory only (not localStorage). Go API validates Cognito JWT via lestrrat-go/jwx against Cognito JWKS endpoint with key caching. Role enforcement: bed_coordinator and administrator see all campuses including patient_id_hash; charge_nurse sees only their unit_ids (enforced in SQL WHERE clause and WebSocket filter by unit_ids claim); transfer_coordinator receives anonymized census counts only, no patient_id_hash in responses. CloudFront WAF: hospital CIDR allowlist blocks all external requests; caching disabled for /api/* to prevent PHI in CDN cache. TLS 1.3 for all traffic; CloudFront enforces HTTPS redirect. No PHI in CloudWatch Logs or Datadog traces: patient_id_hash and admission_diagnosis excluded from Go structured log fields. HIPAA BAA with AWS. capacity_snapshots contain no patient identifiers; patient_placements retained 7 years.
target_output.deployment_and_operations: ECS Fargate in us-east-1: CapacityAPI service (2 min tasks, 5 max, CPU-based autoscaling at 70%), AdtListenerService (1 task, single-AZ acceptable for MLLP failover window). CloudFront distribution with S3 origin for React SPA; invalidation triggered on each deployment. ALB with WAF in two availability zones; ALB security group allows inbound 443 from CloudFront managed prefix list only. NLB for MLLP in two AZs with TCP port 2575; security group allows inbound from Epic ADT server CIDR. ElastiCache Redis 7 cluster mode disabled (single primary + replica) in private subnets. RDS PostgreSQL 16 Multi-AZ db.r6g.large in private subnets. GitHub Actions CI: go test (80% coverage), go vet, staticcheck, react-scripts test, docker build, ECR push. ECS rolling deployment with minimum 50% healthy tasks. Database migrations via golang-migrate ECS one-off task before service update. Environments: dev (LocalStack for AWS services, Docker Compose PostgreSQL and Redis), staging (full AWS stack, Epic ADT test feed from Epic sandbox), production (with change approval for ECS task definition updates).
target_output.observability: Datadog APM Go tracer on CapacityAPI and AdtListenerService with service-level SLO: 99.5% of ADT event processing <3 seconds. Datadog Browser SDK on React app for frontend RUM: page load time, WebSocket connection success rate, time-to-first-bed-update. Custom Datadog metrics: websocket.connections.active gauge tagged by fargate_task, adt.events.processed_per_minute counter tagged by campus_id and adt_event_type, adt.feed.last_event_age_seconds gauge per campus_id (staleness indicator), bed.status_update.propagation_latency_ms histogram (ADT receive to WebSocket dispatch), diversion_advisory.triggered_count counter. Datadog monitors: PagerDuty on adt.feed.last_event_age_seconds >600 for any campus (ADT feed down), websocket.connections.active drops >50% in 2 minutes (WebSocket hub crash), bed.status_update.propagation_latency_ms p99 >4000 (approaching SLA breach).
target_output.cost_and_scaling: ECS Fargate CapacityAPI (2-5 tasks, 0.5 vCPU 1GB each) ~$150-300/month at average 3 tasks. AdtListenerService (1 task) ~$30/month. RDS PostgreSQL Multi-AZ db.r6g.large ~$320/month. ElastiCache Redis r6g.large single-AZ ~$100/month. CloudFront data transfer for SPA ~$20/month. S3 static hosting <$5/month. Total estimated ~$630-800/month. Scaling path: CapacityAPI autoscales on CPU; 5 tasks support 2,500 concurrent WebSocket connections at 500 connections/task. capacity_snapshots monthly partitions archived to S3 Intelligent-Tiering after 6 months, reducing RDS storage growth.
target_output.phased_implementation: Phase 1 - MVP Core Capacity (Q3 2026, 10 weeks): Live bed status grid for 3 campuses via Epic ADT A01/A02/A03/A08 integration, Go WebSocket hub with Redis pub/sub fan-out, React campus bed map and unit grid with 5-second update SLA, patient placement queue with bed assignment, Cognito SAML federation with role-scoped WebSocket filtering, OccupancySnapshotJob and 30-day occupancy trend dashboard, diversion advisory automation with administrator-configurable threshold, Datadog APM and ADT feed health alerting. Phase 2 - Coordination Workflows (Q4 2026, 6 weeks): Transfer request workflow with partner facility capacity view, housekeeping task queue integrated with bed dirty/clean status lifecycle, bed coordinator mobile-responsive layout for tablet use. Phase 3 - Predictive Analytics (Q2 2027): Census forecasting model using 2-year capacity_snapshots history, staffing demand prediction by unit, integration with surgical schedule for planned admission surge prediction.
target_output.development_guidelines: Go: all exported functions documented; error wrapping via fmt.Errorf with %w; no panic in request handlers; goroutine leak detection via goleak in integration tests. React: TypeScript strict mode; no useEffect for data fetching - React Query only; WebSocket event handlers update React Query cache via queryClient.setQueryData, not component state. No patient_id_hash values in any log.Info or log.Error calls - use bed_id and unit_id for operational context. Integration tests for AdtListenerService use HL7 message fixtures for all four ADT event types. BedStateManager requires 90% unit test coverage given its criticality to the real-time SLA. PR changes to DiversionAdvisoryEngine threshold logic require review from a hospital patient flow director.
target_output.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: Epic ADT feed interruption leaves bed status stale with no indication to bed coordinators that data is outdated. Mitigation: AdtListenerService writes last_adt_received_at per campus to Redis; CapacityAPI pushes adt_feed_health WebSocket message every 60 seconds; React dashboard shows stale data warning banner if last_event_age >300 seconds for any campus. Risk: Redis pub/sub message delivery is at-most-once; a WebSocket Hub goroutine crash between pub/sub receive and client dispatch loses that bed update. Mitigation: WebSocket clients poll GET /api/v1/campuses/:id/snapshot every 60 seconds as fallback reconciliation. Tradeoff: SVG-based campus floor plan requires manual SVG updates when physical floor layouts change. Accepted for MVP; Phase 2 will evaluate dynamic floor plan editor. Risk: Cognito SAML federation downtime prevents all logins. Mitigation: Cognito has 99.9% SLA; fallback to local Cognito user accounts for break-glass administrator access during SAML outage.
target_output.open_questions_resolved: Diversion advisory threshold configuration: hospital administrators control threshold via /admin/thresholds endpoint (administrator role required); bed coordinators see the threshold value but cannot modify it. This prevents unauthorized threshold changes that could suppress legitimate diversion advisories. Epic ADT MLLP vs sFTP: confirmed MLLP direct connection via NLB for sub-second ADT event delivery vs sFTP file polling which would introduce 5-15 minute delays. Patient name display for charge nurses: charge nurses see patient_id_hash only, not patient name, in bed grid - full patient name displayed only for bed_coordinator and administrator roles with access to patient_placements join.

metadata.notes: First-pass hospital capacity management platform: Go WebSocket hub with Redis pub/sub fan-out for 5-second ADT event propagation SLA across ECS horizontal scaling.

project_goal.value: API-first integration platform infrastructure for a specialty cancer center: standardized Kong API gateway and developer portal exposing clinical and research data services to internal teams and external research partners
project_goal.rationale: Eliminates point-to-point integrations between 12 internal services and 6 research partners; enforces consistent auth, rate limiting, and audit logging across all API traffic

target_users.value: Internal engineering teams (API consumers and publishers), external research partners and academic institutions, platform administrators managing API policies and developer portal
target_users.rationale: Three distinct user classes with different auth models and access levels identified in platform scoping

project_class.rationale: Platform infrastructure (Kong Gateway, Keycloak, developer portal) rather than an application serving end users

capabilities.rationale: Kong Gateway, Keycloak OAuth 2.0, PostgreSQL for gateway config and audit, GitLab CI CD, and upstream service integrations

complexity_level.rationale: Multi-tenant API gateway, dynamic service registration via CI CD, Keycloak multi-realm config, and HIPAA-compliant audit trail for partner-facing PHI endpoints

risk_level.rationale: Partner-facing endpoints expose de-identified clinical data; incorrect access control configuration could expose PHI to unauthorized research partners

data_sensitivity.rationale: Clinical and tumor registry data routed through the gateway may contain PHI depending on de-identification status

external_exposure.rationale: Research partner organizations authenticate and consume APIs via the public-facing gateway endpoint

access_model.value: Keycloak multi-realm: internal realm (hospital SSO via LDAP federation for engineering teams), partner realm (OAuth 2.0 client credentials for research partner applications). Kong enforces JWT validation and scope-based route authorization per upstream service
access_model.rationale: Separate Keycloak realms provide authentication isolation between internal staff and external partners; Kong scopes enforce data use agreement compliance per partner

feature_scope.value: Kong Gateway with per-route authentication, rate limiting, and request logging plugins; Keycloak with internal LDAP realm and partner OAuth 2.0 client credentials realm; developer portal for API catalog, partner application registration, and documentation; CI/CD auto-registration of new service OpenAPI specs; audit event stream for all gateway traffic; partner data use agreement (DUA) scope enforcement
feature_scope.rationale: Complete platform from gateway policy enforcement through developer experience and regulatory audit trail

mvp_scope.value: Kong Gateway on GKE Autopilot with JWT validation and rate limiting plugins, Keycloak internal and partner realms, developer portal for partner application registration and API key issuance, auto-registration CI/CD step for OpenAPI spec sync, audit event log to PostgreSQL
mvp_scope.rationale: Minimum needed to onboard first research partner and retire the first point-to-point integration

security_baseline.value: mTLS between Kong and all upstream services within GKE cluster, TLS 1.3 for external gateway traffic, Keycloak JWT RS256 signed tokens, WAF in front of partner-facing gateway endpoint, DUA scope validation per API route
security_baseline.rationale: Partner-facing PHI exposure requires defense-in-depth: mTLS for service mesh, JWT for API auth, WAF for external traffic, DUA scopes for data access compliance

frontend_stack.value: N/A - infrastructure project; developer portal built on Kong Konnect Developer Portal or Backstage for API catalog
frontend_stack.rationale: No custom browser application; developer portal is a configured instance of existing portal software

backend_stack.value: Go 1.22 custom Kong plugins for DUA scope validation and PHI audit logging; Kong Gateway 3.6 on GKE; Keycloak 24 for OAuth 2.0
backend_stack.rationale: Kong 3.6 LTS on GKE for gateway; Go plugins for custom DUA logic not available in Kong built-in plugins

data_platform.value: PostgreSQL 16 (Cloud SQL) for Kong Gateway configuration DB, Keycloak realm DB, audit_events, partner_organizations, and api_routes; Redis 7 (Cloud Memorystore) for Kong rate limiting counters
data_platform.rationale: Kong and Keycloak both require PostgreSQL backends; Cloud SQL for managed HA; Redis for distributed rate limit counters across Kong replicas

hosting_target.value: GKE Autopilot in GCP us-central1; Kong and Keycloak deployed as Helm charts; external HTTPS load balancer for partner-facing gateway endpoint
hosting_target.rationale: Cancer center on GCP; GKE Autopilot reduces node management overhead; Helm for reproducible Kong and Keycloak versioning

privacy_retention_policy.value: Gateway audit_events retained 7 years per HIPAA for all routes tagged data_sensitivity=health; partner access logs retained 3 years per data use agreement terms; Keycloak audit events retained 2 years
privacy_retention_policy.rationale: HIPAA audit trail requirements for PHI-adjacent API traffic and DUA contractual retention

future_scope.value: GraphQL gateway for tumor registry queries, API monetization and usage-based billing for commercial partners, gRPC transcoding for internal microservice communication
future_scope.rationale: GraphQL and gRPC deferred pending internal service readiness; monetization out of scope for cancer center mission

constraints.value: No PHI may flow through the gateway in unencrypted form; research partner API credentials must be tied to a signed data use agreement on file; CI/CD OpenAPI auto-registration must not require manual gateway configuration changes for new service routes
constraints.rationale: Data compliance, legal DUA requirement, and developer experience automation goals

observability_baseline.value: Grafana dashboards on Prometheus metrics from Kong and Keycloak, distributed tracing with OpenTelemetry for gateway request flow, alert on partner API error rate >1% or DUA scope violation attempts
observability_baseline.rationale: Platform observability and security monitoring requirements

execution_preference.value: GitLab CI sprints with infrastructure changes reviewed by platform team lead before merge to main
execution_preference.rationale: Infrastructure changes affecting partner access require senior review to prevent accidental policy misconfigurations

compliance_context.value: HIPAA BAA with GCP for PHI-tagged routes; data use agreement compliance per ONC information blocking provisions 45 CFR 171; research data access per IRB-approved protocols
compliance_context.rationale: PHI in clinical data routes, ONC information blocking non-discrimination requirements, and IRB protocol compliance for research data access

project.goal: Kong API gateway and developer portal infrastructure standardizing all internal and partner-facing API traffic at the cancer center
project.target_users: Internal engineering teams, external research partners, platform administrators
project.timeline: MVP with first partner onboarding Q3 2026

backend.api: Kong Admin API and Deck for declarative configuration

security.auth: Keycloak multi-realm OAuth 2.0; Kong JWT plugin for token validation
security.transport: TLS 1.3 external, mTLS internal service mesh
security.phi_controls: No PHI in gateway access logs; DUA scopes per partner route

data.db: PostgreSQL 16 Cloud SQL for Kong and Keycloak backends, audit_events
data.cache: Redis 7 Cloud Memorystore for Kong rate limiting counters

devops.environments: dev (kind cluster), staging (GKE Autopilot), production (GKE Autopilot)

constraints.dua: Partner credentials tied to signed DUA
constraints.automation: CI/CD auto-registration for new service OpenAPI specs

open_questions.dua_enforcement: How does the platform verify a DUA is signed before issuing partner credentials?

reasoner_reviews.product: The auto-registration CI/CD step for OpenAPI specs is the feature with the highest developer experience value - it directly addresses the root cause of stale API documentation and manual gateway configuration drift. The DUA verification workflow before partner credential issuance needs to be codified in the developer portal onboarding flow, not left as an out-of-band manual process. Round 1 had an informal DUA check; Round 2 must make it a blocking gate in the partner_applications approval workflow.
reasoner_reviews.architect_reasoner: Round 1 used Kong Deck YAML files checked into the GitLab repo for declarative configuration, but the OpenAPI auto-registration step in CI/CD generated Kong service and route configs that conflicted with existing Deck-managed routes when the same upstream service had both manually defined and auto-generated routes. Round 2 must establish a clear ownership rule: all Kong routes are auto-generated from OpenAPI specs via CI/CD; manual Deck YAML files are used only for gateway-level infrastructure (rate limit policies, plugin configurations) not for service routes. This eliminates the config conflict.
reasoner_reviews.security: The mTLS requirement between Kong and upstream services in the contract was not implemented in Round 1 - internal service communication used plaintext HTTP within the GKE cluster. Round 2 must implement cert-manager with Let's Encrypt for internal cluster mTLS using GKE Workload Identity for certificate automation. DUA scope validation currently happens at the application layer in upstream services; it must be enforced by the Kong DUA plugin at the gateway layer so a misconfigured upstream cannot accidentally return PHI to an unauthorized partner.
reasoner_reviews.constraints: The CI/CD auto-registration constraint requires a well-defined OpenAPI spec standard: all internal services must include x-kong-tags (data_sensitivity, upstream_service_name) in their OpenAPI spec for the auto-registration step to correctly configure Kong rate limit and DUA plugins per route. Services without these tags should fail the CI/CD step with a clear error, not silently register without security policies.

backend.service_design: Kong Gateway 3.6 deployed as Helm chart in kong namespace on GKE Autopilot. Two gateway nodes (minimum) with Cloud SQL PostgreSQL as Kong config DB backend (traditional mode, not DB-less, for dynamic route registration). Custom Go Kong plugins: DuaScopeValidatorPlugin validates partner JWT scope claim against api_routes.required_dua_scopes; PhiAuditPlugin writes audit_events for routes tagged data_sensitivity=health. KongAutoRegistrar service: Go binary in CI/CD step that reads OpenAPI spec, generates Kong Service/Route/Plugin declarative config, applies via Kong Admin API.
backend.api_patterns: Kong Admin API (internal only, no external exposure): POST /services, POST /routes, POST /plugins used by KongAutoRegistrar. Developer portal REST API (internal): POST /api/partner-applications (register app), GET /api/partner-applications/{id}/credentials (issue API key after DUA verification), POST /api/services/register (OpenAPI spec upload triggering auto-registration). Partner-facing gateway: /api/v1/{upstream_service_name}/{route_path} - Kong routes traffic to upstream Kubernetes services based on auto-registered routes.
backend.business_modules: DuaScopeValidatorPlugin: reads partner JWT, extracts dua_scope claim, checks against api_routes.required_dua_scopes; returns 403 with DUA_SCOPE_VIOLATION audit event if mismatch. PhiAuditPlugin: for data_sensitivity=health routes, writes {partner_org_id, api_route_id, timestamp, response_status} to audit_events via PostgreSQL; never logs request/response body. KongAutoRegistrar: parses x-kong-tags from OpenAPI spec, creates Kong Service pointing to Kubernetes service DNS, creates Kong Route with path prefix, attaches jwt plugin, rate-limit plugin, and DuaScopeValidatorPlugin with required_dua_scopes from x-kong-required-dua-scopes spec extension.
backend.failure_handling: Kong replica failure: GKE Autopilot restarts pod; Cloud SQL maintains Kong config so new replica comes up with full route configuration. Keycloak downtime: Kong caches validated JWTs in Redis for 5 minutes (jwt plugin cache_ttl); partner requests continue serving cached tokens. Auto-registration failure: CI/CD step exits non-zero; deployment of new service is blocked until OpenAPI spec passes validation. Kong Admin API unreachable: KongAutoRegistrar retries 3 times with 10-second backoff before failing CI step.

security.auth_design: Keycloak 24 deployed on GKE in keycloak namespace with Cloud SQL backend. Two realms: hospital-internal realm (LDAP federation to hospital Active Directory for engineering staff login to developer portal admin); partner realm (OAuth 2.0 client credentials for research partner applications; each developer_application record maps to one Keycloak client with data-use-agreement-specific scope claims). Kong JWT plugin validates RS256 tokens against Keycloak JWKS endpoint per realm.
security.authorization_model: Partner access: Keycloak client_credentials token with dua_scopes claim (e.g., tumor_registry:read:deidentified, clinical_trials:read:deidentified). Kong DuaScopeValidatorPlugin checks claim against api_routes.required_dua_scopes. Internal access: hospital-internal realm JWT with service_name claim; Kong routes internal traffic without DUA check. Admin access to developer portal: hospital-internal realm with platform_admin role.
security.secrets_management: Keycloak client secrets for partner applications stored in GCP Secret Manager; never in Kubernetes Secrets. Kong Admin API secured by mTLS client certificate; only KongAutoRegistrar service account can call Admin API. cert-manager manages cluster-internal certificates for mTLS between Kong and upstream services using GKE Workload Identity for certificate automation.
security.audit_and_logging_controls: PhiAuditPlugin writes to audit_events table for every request through data_sensitivity=health routes: partner_org_id, developer_application_id, api_route_id, http_method, response_status, timestamp. Request and response bodies never logged. Keycloak audit events (login, token issuance, client creation) forwarded to Cloud Logging. Kong access logs stripped of Authorization headers before Cloud Logging forwarding.
security.security_risks: DUA scope misconfiguration risk: a route configured with incorrect required_dua_scopes could expose PHI to a partner with a less restrictive DUA. Mitigation: route configuration in KongAutoRegistrar auto-registration is generated from x-kong-required-dua-scopes in the upstream service's OpenAPI spec, reviewed in code review, never manually edited in Kong Admin.

data.entities: api_routes (id UUID PK, upstream_service_name VARCHAR, route_path VARCHAR, http_methods VARCHAR[], kong_route_id VARCHAR, data_sensitivity ENUM none/internal/health, required_dua_scopes VARCHAR[], rate_limit_per_min SMALLINT, registered_at TIMESTAMPTZ, registered_by_pipeline VARCHAR). developer_applications (id UUID PK, partner_org_id UUID FK, app_name VARCHAR, keycloak_client_id VARCHAR, dua_scopes_granted VARCHAR[], dua_document_id UUID FK, status ENUM pending_dua/active/suspended/revoked, created_at TIMESTAMPTZ, activated_at TIMESTAMPTZ). partner_organizations (id UUID PK, org_name VARCHAR, institution_type ENUM academic/pharma/government, primary_contact_email VARCHAR, dua_signed BOOLEAN, dua_signed_at TIMESTAMPTZ, irb_protocol_number VARCHAR). data_use_agreements (id UUID PK, partner_org_id FK, document_s3_key TEXT, permitted_scopes VARCHAR[], effective_date DATE, expiry_date DATE, signed_by VARCHAR). audit_events (id UUID PK, partner_org_id UUID FK, developer_application_id UUID FK, api_route_id UUID FK, http_method VARCHAR(7), response_status SMALLINT, event_timestamp TIMESTAMPTZ) PARTITION BY RANGE (event_timestamp). rate_limit_policies (id UUID PK, policy_name VARCHAR, requests_per_minute SMALLINT, requests_per_day INTEGER, applies_to_partner_tier ENUM academic/commercial/internal).
data.storage_design: Cloud SQL PostgreSQL 16 High Availability instance: Kong config DB (kong schema), Keycloak realm DB (keycloak schema), platform DB (api_routes, developer_applications, partner_organizations, data_use_agreements, audit_events). Redis 7 Cloud Memorystore Standard tier: Kong rate limiting counters (per partner_application per route) and JWT cache (5-minute TTL). audit_events partitioned monthly; archived to Cloud Storage Coldline after 2 years.
data.retention_and_deletion: audit_events for data_sensitivity=health routes retained 7 years via partition archival to Cloud Storage. developer_applications retained 5 years after revocation for audit. Partner API keys revoked immediately on DUA expiry via Keycloak client disable automation.

devops.deployment_topology: GKE Autopilot cluster in us-central1 with three namespaces: kong (Kong Gateway replicas, KongAutoRegistrar job), keycloak (Keycloak StatefulSet with Cloud SQL proxy sidecar), developer-portal (portal service). GCP External HTTPS Load Balancer with Cloud Armor WAF for partner-facing gateway endpoint. Internal load balancer for Kong Admin API accessible only within VPC. Cloud SQL (private IP) and Cloud Memorystore (private service access) in VPC-native GKE network.
devops.ci_cd_design: GitLab CI pipeline per upstream service repository: validate OpenAPI spec (spectral lint for x-kong-tags and x-kong-required-dua-scopes extensions), run KongAutoRegistrar in dry-run mode (validate Kong route generation without applying), on merge to main: KongAutoRegistrar applies routes via Kong Admin API, updates api_routes table, syncs OpenAPI spec to developer portal catalog. Gateway infrastructure changes (rate limit policies, Kong plugin upgrades) managed via separate GitLab repo with Helm chart and Terraform; changes require platform team lead MR approval. GitLab CI also runs Keycloak realm export diff to detect unauthorized realm config changes.
devops.observability_stack: Prometheus scrapes Kong metrics (/metrics endpoint): kong_http_requests_total by route and status, kong_bandwidth_bytes by direction, kong_latency_ms histogram. Keycloak Prometheus metrics: token_issuance_total by realm, authentication_failure_total. Custom metrics from PhiAuditPlugin: audit_events written per route per hour (forwarded via Cloud Monitoring). Grafana dashboards: API traffic by partner_org, rate limit violations, DUA scope violation attempts, Keycloak token issuance health. PagerDuty: DUA scope violation rate >0 per hour (potential unauthorized access attempt), Keycloak token issuance failure rate >5% for 5 minutes, Kong request error rate >2% on any data_sensitivity=health route.
devops.devops_risks: Kong config drift: manually applied Kong config changes (via Admin API outside CI/CD) will not be tracked in GitLab and will be overwritten on next KongAutoRegistrar run. Mitigation: Kong Admin API mTLS cert issued only to KongAutoRegistrar service account; engineers have no direct Admin API access in staging or production.

ISS-001.title: Kong routes partially managed by Deck YAML and partially auto-generated, causing config conflicts
ISS-001.detail: Round 1 had both manually-maintained Kong Deck YAML files for service routes and auto-generated routes from CI/CD. When the same upstream service had both, KongAutoRegistrar overwrote manual Deck configs, stripping DUA plugin configurations from previously working routes.

history.note: Identified by architect_reasoner: dual ownership of Kong route config creates inconsistency

ISS-002.title: mTLS between Kong and upstream services not implemented; internal traffic plaintext
ISS-002.detail: Contract specifies mTLS for Kong-to-upstream internal communication but Round 1 used plaintext HTTP within GKE cluster. A compromised internal service could intercept unencrypted PHI flowing through the gateway to upstream services.

history.note: Security reviewer identified missing mTLS in internal service mesh section

ISS-003.title: DUA verification is manual out-of-band process before partner credential issuance
ISS-003.detail: Round 1 described DUA verification as a manual email-based confirmation before a platform admin issues API keys in Keycloak. This has no enforcement mechanism; a platform admin could issue credentials without a signed DUA on file.

history.note: Identified by product reviewer: no gating mechanism between DUA and credential issuance

focus_issues.title: Kong routes partially managed by Deck YAML and partially auto-generated
focus_issues.detail: Dual ownership of Kong route config causes KongAutoRegistrar to overwrite manually-maintained Deck configs, stripping DUA plugin configurations.

focus_issues.title: mTLS between Kong and upstream services not implemented
focus_issues.detail: Internal Kong-to-upstream traffic uses plaintext HTTP within GKE cluster, violating the contract mTLS requirement.

latest_recommendations: Establish single source of truth: all Kong service/route configs auto-generated from OpenAPI specs via CI/CD; Deck YAML limited to gateway-level infrastructure policies only
Implement cert-manager with GKE Workload Identity for internal cluster mTLS certificates; configure Kong to use mTLS client cert for upstream connections
Add DUA gating to developer portal: developer_applications cannot transition to active status until data_use_agreements.dua_signed=true for the partner organization
revision_memory.latest_plan_title: Cancer Center API Integration Platform - Round 1

convergence_state.reason: Two high-severity issues remain: config ownership conflict blocks safe CI/CD and missing mTLS violates security contract

previous_audits.summary: Solid Kong and Keycloak foundation with correct multi-realm auth design and auto-registration concept, but two high-severity issues prevent production go-live: config ownership conflict makes auto-registration unsafe and missing mTLS violates the security contract for PHI-adjacent routes.
strengths: Keycloak dual-realm design correctly separates internal staff and partner OAuth flows
KongAutoRegistrar concept with OpenAPI spec x-kong-tags is the right approach for CI/CD-driven route management
DUA scope concept is architecturally sound even though enforcement is not yet gated
concerns: Deck YAML and KongAutoRegistrar have overlapping ownership of Kong route configs
Internal service traffic is plaintext HTTP despite contract requiring mTLS
DUA verification relies on platform admin discipline rather than system enforcement
blocking_issues: ISS-001: Config ownership conflict means KongAutoRegistrar strips DUA plugin from manually-managed routes on each CI/CD run
ISS-002: Plaintext internal traffic violates contract mTLS requirement; PHI could be intercepted in a cluster breach scenario
recommendations: Retire all Deck YAML service/route configurations; migrate to 100% KongAutoRegistrar ownership of service routes
Deploy cert-manager with GKE Workload Identity; configure Kong service objects to use client TLS certificate for upstream connections
Add DUA gating state machine to developer portal: pending_dua -> active transition blocked until dua_signed=true

issue_updates.title: Dual Kong route config ownership
issue_updates.detail: deployment_and_operations section described both Deck sync and KongAutoRegistrar as active configuration mechanisms for service routes

issue_updates.title: Missing internal mTLS
issue_updates.detail: security_and_compliance section mentioned mTLS as future state; system_components confirmed plaintext HTTP to upstream services

previous_plan.title: Cancer Center API Integration Platform - Round 1
previous_plan.executive_summary: Kong Gateway 3.6 on GKE Autopilot with Keycloak 24 dual-realm auth for internal and partner API access. KongAutoRegistrar CI/CD step for OpenAPI spec-driven route registration. Deck YAML for gateway infrastructure policies. Custom Go DuaScopeValidatorPlugin for partner DUA enforcement. PhiAuditPlugin for health-sensitivity route audit logging.
previous_plan.architecture_overview: Kong Gateway on GKE with Cloud SQL PostgreSQL backend. Keycloak hospital-internal realm (LDAP) and partner realm (client credentials). KongAutoRegistrar and Deck YAML both manage Kong config (config ownership conflict - ISS-001). Internal service communication via plaintext HTTP (mTLS not implemented - ISS-002).
previous_plan.security_and_compliance: Keycloak JWT RS256 validation via Kong jwt plugin. DuaScopeValidatorPlugin enforces DUA scopes on partner routes. DUA verification manual out-of-band process before credential issuance. No mTLS for internal cluster traffic.

target_output.thinking_summary: Round 2 must resolve ISS-001 (Kong config ownership conflict) and ISS-002 (missing mTLS). For ISS-001: the fix is a clean ownership rule - KongAutoRegistrar is the sole owner of all Kong service/route/plugin configurations; Deck YAML is retired for service routes and limited strictly to gateway-level infra (rate limit policies, global plugins). This requires a one-time migration of any remaining manually-managed service routes to OpenAPI spec format. For ISS-002: cert-manager with GKE Workload Identity automates internal cluster TLS certificate lifecycle; Kong service objects are configured with client certificate for mTLS upstream connections. ISS-003 (manual DUA gating) is also addressed by adding a state machine to the developer portal: developer_applications remain in pending_dua status until data_use_agreements.dua_signed=true for the partner_org, blocking Keycloak client creation until this gate is passed.

fix_report.action_taken: Retired all Deck YAML service/route configuration files. Migrated 4 remaining manually-managed service routes to OpenAPI spec format with x-kong-tags and x-kong-required-dua-scopes extensions. KongAutoRegistrar is now the sole mechanism for Kong service and route management. Deck YAML retained only for gateway-level infrastructure: global rate_limit_policies, Kong proxy_cache plugin configuration, and Kong CORS plugin. Kong Admin API mTLS certificate issued exclusively to KongAutoRegistrar GitLab service account; no engineer direct Admin API access in staging or production.
fix_report.expected_outcome: CI/CD auto-registration no longer strips DUA plugins from previously-registered routes; every service route has a canonical OpenAPI spec as its configuration source of truth; config drift detectable by diffing api_routes table against registered Kong routes.

fix_report.action_taken: Deployed cert-manager 1.14 as Helm chart in cert-manager namespace with GKE Workload Identity for certificate issuance. Created ClusterIssuer for GKE-internal CA. Each upstream service namespace has a Certificate resource generating a TLS cert/key pair mounted to the service pod. Kong service objects updated to use tls.certificate_id referencing the Kong-internal CA certificate for upstream mTLS handshake. Kong verifies upstream server certificate against cluster CA. Upstream services verify Kong client certificate against cluster CA.
fix_report.expected_outcome: All Kong-to-upstream service communication encrypted with mTLS; a compromised internal pod cannot intercept PHI in transit between Kong and clinical data services; contract mTLS requirement satisfied.

target_output.title: Cancer Center API Integration Platform - Round 2
target_output.executive_summary: Kong Gateway 3.6 on GKE Autopilot with Keycloak 24 dual-realm OAuth 2.0 providing a standards-based API integration platform for the cancer center. Round 2 resolves both high-severity issues: KongAutoRegistrar becomes the sole source of truth for all service route configuration (retiring Deck YAML service routes), and cert-manager with GKE Workload Identity implements full mTLS between Kong and all upstream services. A DUA gating state machine in the developer portal prevents partner credential issuance until a signed data_use_agreements record is on file. Custom Go DuaScopeValidatorPlugin enforces DUA scope claims per route; PhiAuditPlugin writes a HIPAA-compliant audit trail for all health-tagged API routes. CI/CD auto-registration of OpenAPI specs eliminates manual gateway configuration for new services.
target_output.architecture_overview: Kong Gateway 3.6 deployed as Helm chart on GKE Autopilot (us-central1, kong namespace) with Cloud SQL PostgreSQL 16 as the Kong config backend (traditional mode for dynamic route registration). GCP External HTTPS Load Balancer with Cloud Armor WAF provides the partner-facing endpoint; internal GCP Internal Load Balancer serves Kong Admin API within the VPC. Keycloak 24 runs as a StatefulSet (keycloak namespace) with Cloud SQL backend and Cloud Memorystore Redis for session caching. KongAutoRegistrar is a Go binary invoked in GitLab CI that parses service OpenAPI specs, generates Kong service/route/plugin configurations, and applies them via the Kong Admin API - it is the exclusive configuration mechanism for service routes. cert-manager 1.14 (cert-manager namespace) issues internal cluster TLS certificates via GKE Workload Identity; Kong service objects use mTLS client certificates for all upstream service connections. DuaScopeValidatorPlugin validates partner JWT dua_scope claims against api_routes.required_dua_scopes before proxying. PhiAuditPlugin writes audit_events to PostgreSQL for all data_sensitivity=health routes.
target_output.technology_stack: Gateway: Kong Gateway 3.6 on GKE Autopilot, Kong Helm chart 2.30, Kong Admin API with mTLS client cert access control. Auth: Keycloak 24, Helm chart, Cloud SQL backend, lestrrat-go/jwx for Kong JWT plugin JWKS validation. Custom Plugins: Go 1.22 Kong PDK plugins - DuaScopeValidatorPlugin and PhiAuditPlugin. CI/CD Config Tool: KongAutoRegistrar (Go 1.22 binary), Deck 1.38 for gateway infrastructure policies only. Certificate Management: cert-manager 1.14 with GKE Workload Identity ClusterIssuer. Database: Cloud SQL PostgreSQL 16 HA with private IP. Cache: Cloud Memorystore Redis 7 Standard for Kong rate limit counters and Keycloak sessions. Infrastructure-as-Code: Terraform for GCP resources (Cloud SQL, Cloud Memorystore, GKE Autopilot cluster, load balancers, Cloud Armor). GitLab CI for pipeline automation. Observability: Prometheus operator, Grafana 10.4 on GKE.
target_output.functional_feature_map: API Gateway Enforcement: Kong routes all internal and partner API traffic; JWT validation, rate limiting (per partner_application per route), DUA scope enforcement, and PHI audit logging applied per route via plugins configured by KongAutoRegistrar. Developer Portal: Partner organization registration with DUA gating (pending_dua -> active state machine); API catalog showing all registered OpenAPI specs; developer_application credential issuance after DUA verification; API key rotation and revocation. CI/CD Auto-Registration: Each upstream service CI pipeline includes KongAutoRegistrar step that reads OpenAPI spec, validates x-kong-tags and x-kong-required-dua-scopes extensions via spectral lint, and registers/updates Kong routes with correct plugins. Audit Trail: PhiAuditPlugin writes audit_events for every request through health-tagged routes; monthly archival to Cloud Storage Coldline. DUA Lifecycle: data_use_agreements tracked with effective_date and expiry_date; automated Keycloak client suspension on DUA expiry.
target_output.system_components: GCP External HTTPS Load Balancer + Cloud Armor WAF: TLS termination and hospital CIDR allowlist for partner-facing traffic. Kong Gateway (GKE kong namespace, 2-5 replicas via HorizontalPodAutoscaler): proxy and plugin execution. KongAutoRegistrar (GitLab CI job): OpenAPI spec parser, Kong Admin API client, api_routes table updater. Kong Admin API Internal Load Balancer: VPC-internal only, mTLS client cert authentication. Keycloak 24 (GKE keycloak namespace, 2 replicas): hospital-internal LDAP realm and partner OAuth 2.0 realm. cert-manager (GKE cert-manager namespace): ClusterIssuer for GKE-internal CA, Certificate resources per upstream service namespace. Cloud SQL PostgreSQL 16 HA: Kong config DB, Keycloak realm DB, platform tables (api_routes, developer_applications, partner_organizations, data_use_agreements, audit_events). Cloud Memorystore Redis 7: Kong rate limit counters and JWT cache, Keycloak session store. Developer Portal Service (GKE developer-portal namespace): REST API for partner onboarding, DUA state machine, API catalog sync. Prometheus + Grafana (GKE monitoring namespace): Kong and Keycloak metrics scraping and dashboards.
target_output.workflows: Service Route Auto-Registration: Internal team deploys a new tumor registry query service. CI/CD pipeline runs spectral lint on OpenAPI spec checking for x-kong-tags (data_sensitivity=health) and x-kong-required-dua-scopes (tumor_registry:read:deidentified). Lint passes. KongAutoRegistrar runs: creates Kong Service pointing to Kubernetes service DNS (tumor-registry-svc.clinical.svc.cluster.local), creates Kong Route with path /api/v1/tumor-registry/*, attaches jwt plugin (Keycloak JWKS), rate-limit plugin (100/min per developer_application), DuaScopeValidatorPlugin (required_scopes: tumor_registry:read:deidentified), PhiAuditPlugin. KongAutoRegistrar inserts api_routes record. KongAutoRegistrar pushes OpenAPI spec to developer portal catalog. Total automation: zero manual Kong configuration changes. Partner Credential Issuance with DUA Gate: Research partner from University of Chicago Comprehensive Cancer Center registers in developer portal. Platform admin uploads signed DUA document; data_use_agreements record created with permitted_scopes=[tumor_registry:read:deidentified], effective_date, expiry_date. developer_applications status transitions from pending_dua to active. Developer portal calls Keycloak Admin API to create client in partner realm with dua_scope claim. Partner receives client_id and client_secret from Secret Manager. Partner calls /auth/realms/partner/protocol/openid-connect/token with client credentials, receives JWT with dua_scope=tumor_registry:read:deidentified. Partner calls /api/v1/tumor-registry/cohort-query, DuaScopeValidatorPlugin validates scope, PhiAuditPlugin writes audit_event, Kong proxies to tumor-registry service over mTLS.
target_output.data_model: api_routes (id UUID PK, upstream_service_name VARCHAR, route_path VARCHAR, http_methods VARCHAR[], kong_service_id VARCHAR, kong_route_id VARCHAR, data_sensitivity ENUM none/internal/health, required_dua_scopes VARCHAR[], rate_limit_per_min SMALLINT, registered_at TIMESTAMPTZ, registered_by_pipeline VARCHAR, openapi_spec_version VARCHAR). developer_applications (id UUID PK, partner_org_id UUID FK, app_name VARCHAR, keycloak_client_id VARCHAR, dua_scopes_granted VARCHAR[], dua_document_id UUID FK, status ENUM pending_dua/active/suspended/revoked, created_at TIMESTAMPTZ, activated_at TIMESTAMPTZ, revoked_at TIMESTAMPTZ). partner_organizations (id UUID PK, org_name VARCHAR, institution_type ENUM academic/pharma/government, primary_contact_email VARCHAR, created_at TIMESTAMPTZ). data_use_agreements (id UUID PK, partner_org_id UUID FK, document_gcs_key TEXT, permitted_scopes VARCHAR[], effective_date DATE, expiry_date DATE, signed_by VARCHAR, uploaded_by VARCHAR, uploaded_at TIMESTAMPTZ). audit_events (id UUID PK, partner_org_id UUID FK, developer_application_id UUID FK, api_route_id UUID FK, http_method VARCHAR(7), response_status SMALLINT, upstream_latency_ms SMALLINT, event_timestamp TIMESTAMPTZ) PARTITION BY RANGE (event_timestamp). rate_limit_policies (id UUID PK, policy_name VARCHAR, requests_per_minute SMALLINT, requests_per_day INTEGER, applies_to_partner_tier ENUM academic/commercial/internal). api_keys (id UUID PK, developer_application_id UUID FK, key_prefix VARCHAR(8), key_hash CHAR(64), created_at TIMESTAMPTZ, last_used_at TIMESTAMPTZ, revoked_at TIMESTAMPTZ).
target_output.api_design: Partner-Facing Kong Proxy (External HTTPS LB): /api/v1/{upstream_service_name}/{path} - Kong routes to upstream service based on auto-registered routes; all require Bearer JWT from Keycloak partner realm. Kong Admin API (Internal LB, mTLS required): POST /services, POST /routes, POST /plugins (used exclusively by KongAutoRegistrar CI service account). Keycloak Token Endpoint (Internal LB for internal realm, External LB for partner realm): POST /auth/realms/partner/protocol/openid-connect/token - client_credentials grant for partner applications. Developer Portal REST API (GKE internal): POST /api/partner-orgs (register organization), POST /api/partner-orgs/{id}/dua (upload signed DUA document), POST /api/developer-applications (register application, validates dua_signed=true), GET /api/developer-applications/{id}/credentials (returns client_id, triggers Secret Manager client_secret generation), GET /api/catalog (returns all OpenAPI specs from api_routes table). All developer portal admin endpoints require Keycloak hospital-internal realm JWT with platform_admin role.
target_output.security_and_compliance: mTLS Internal Service Mesh: cert-manager 1.14 with GKE Workload Identity ClusterIssuer issues x.509 certificates per upstream service namespace. Kong service objects configured with tls.certificate_id for client certificate presentation; upstream services validate Kong client cert against cluster CA. All internal API traffic encrypted in transit. External TLS: Cloud Armor + External HTTPS LB terminates TLS 1.3 for partner traffic. WAF: Cloud Armor security policy with pre-configured XSS and SQLi rules plus cancer center CIDR allowlist for internal API management access. Keycloak JWT RS256: Kong jwt plugin validates token signature against Keycloak JWKS endpoint; 5-minute Redis JWT cache. DUA Enforcement: DuaScopeValidatorPlugin compares JWT dua_scope claim against api_routes.required_dua_scopes; mismatch returns HTTP 403 and writes DUA_VIOLATION audit_event. DUA Expiry Automation: GitLab CI daily job queries data_use_agreements WHERE expiry_date < NOW(); calls Keycloak Admin API to disable expired partner clients; updates developer_applications.status to suspended. HIPAA: PhiAuditPlugin audit_events table for all health-tagged routes retained 7 years; partner access logs stripped of PHI content. BAA with GCP for Cloud SQL storing PHI-adjacent audit records. ONC Information Blocking: api_routes table design supports non-discriminatory access - required_dua_scopes enforced consistently; no partner-specific route exclusions without IRB documentation.
target_output.deployment_and_operations: Terraform manages all GCP infrastructure: GKE Autopilot cluster, Cloud SQL PostgreSQL 16 HA (private IP, Cloud SQL Auth Proxy sidecar on all pods), Cloud Memorystore Redis 7, External and Internal HTTPS Load Balancers, Cloud Armor WAF policy. GitLab CI Infrastructure Pipeline (infra/ repo): Terraform plan with Atlantis for PR preview, manual approval required for production Terraform apply, Helm upgrade for Kong, Keycloak, cert-manager, and developer portal. Service Auto-Registration Pipeline (per upstream service repo): spectral lint (x-kong-tags and x-kong-required-dua-scopes validation), KongAutoRegistrar dry-run (validates Kong route generation), on merge to main: KongAutoRegistrar apply via Kong Admin API mTLS, api_routes INSERT, developer portal catalog sync. Environments: dev (kind cluster with LocalStack for Cloud SQL/Redis simulation), staging (GKE Autopilot with Keycloak test partner realm and synthetic partner organization), production (GKE Autopilot, Cloud SQL HA, full Kong and Keycloak). Production change policy: Kong plugin configuration changes require platform team lead MR approval; Keycloak realm configuration exports checked into infra/ repo and diffed in CI.
target_output.observability: Prometheus Operator scrapes Kong metrics endpoint (/metrics): kong_http_requests_total by service and route, kong_bandwidth_bytes, kong_latency_ms (upstream, Kong processing, total) histograms. Keycloak Prometheus provider: keycloak_logins_total by realm, keycloak_client_authentications_total by client_id, keycloak_failed_client_authentications_total. Custom metrics from PhiAuditPlugin via Prometheus Pushgateway: audit_events_written_total by api_route_id, dua_violation_attempts_total by partner_org_id. Grafana dashboards: API traffic by partner organization (requests/min, error rate, p99 latency), Keycloak token issuance health by realm, DUA violation heatmap by partner and route, cert-manager certificate expiry countdown. PagerDuty: dua_violation_attempts_total >0 per hour (potential unauthorized access), Keycloak partner realm token issuance failure rate >5% for 3 minutes, Kong upstream error rate >2% on any data_sensitivity=health route, cert-manager certificate expiry <7 days for any production certificate.
target_output.cost_and_scaling: GKE Autopilot Kong pods (2-5 replicas, 0.5 vCPU 512MB each) ~$150-350/month autoscaling. Keycloak pods (2 replicas, 1 vCPU 2GB each) ~$200/month. Cloud SQL PostgreSQL HA (db-custom-4-8192) ~$400/month. Cloud Memorystore Redis Standard 1GB ~$80/month. Cloud Armor WAF per-request pricing ~$30/month at 10M requests. External HTTPS LB ~$40/month. Total platform ~$900-1100/month. Scaling: Kong HPA scales on CPU; Cloud SQL read replica for analytics queries against audit_events without impacting transactional Kong config DB.
target_output.phased_implementation: Phase 1 - Core Platform MVP (Q3 2026, 12 weeks): Kong Gateway 3.6 on GKE Autopilot with Cloud SQL backend, KongAutoRegistrar CI/CD step (sole route config mechanism, Deck YAML limited to infra policies), cert-manager internal mTLS, Keycloak dual-realm with LDAP federation and partner client credentials, DuaScopeValidatorPlugin and PhiAuditPlugin Go plugins, developer portal with DUA gating state machine, first upstream service auto-registered (tumor registry query API), first research partner onboarded (University of Chicago CCSG). Phase 2 - Platform Hardening (Q4 2026, 6 weeks): DUA expiry automation, developer portal API catalog with full OpenAPI spec rendering, partner usage analytics dashboard, Terraform drift detection alerts, Kong plugin version upgrade pipeline. Phase 3 - Advanced Capabilities (Q2 2027): Kong GraphQL proxy for tumor registry queries, gRPC transcoding for internal microservice communication, API monetization for commercial pharmaceutical partners, Keycloak Identity Brokering for federated academic institution SSO.
target_output.development_guidelines: Go Kong plugins must implement the PDK interface with full unit test coverage (mock PDK in tests); DuaScopeValidatorPlugin requires 95% coverage given its security criticality. KongAutoRegistrar: all OpenAPI spec parsing and Kong config generation functions unit tested with fixture specs; integration tests run against Kong in Docker Compose. Infrastructure-as-Code: all GCP resources in Terraform; no clickops in staging or production; Terraform state in Cloud Storage with versioning and object lock. Kong Deck YAML limited to: global plugins, rate_limit_policies configuration, CORS plugin - annotated with comment prohibiting service/route definitions. Spectral OpenAPI lint rules checked into rules/kong-tags.yaml and enforced in all upstream service CI pipelines before merge. ADR required for any change to DuaScopeValidatorPlugin scope taxonomy or Keycloak realm configuration.
target_output.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: KongAutoRegistrar applying routes in CI/CD can cause brief routing disruptions if a service's OpenAPI spec changes its route paths (old routes exist until KongAutoRegistrar removes them on the next run). Mitigation: KongAutoRegistrar compares api_routes table to Kong-registered routes and removes stale routes as part of the apply step; dry-run step in CI surfaces removals before merge approval. Risk: cert-manager certificate rotation causes a brief mTLS handshake failure window when Kong's cached upstream certificate expires before the new cert is loaded. Mitigation: cert-manager renews certificates 30 days before expiry; Kong reloads certificates on SIGHUP triggered by cert-manager post-issuance hook. Tradeoff: Traditional mode Kong (with Cloud SQL config DB) vs DB-less mode. Traditional mode chosen to support KongAutoRegistrar dynamic route registration without pod restarts; tradeoff is Cloud SQL dependency adding ~$400/month. Risk: Keycloak partner realm client secret compromise. Mitigation: client secrets stored in Secret Manager with automatic 90-day rotation; old secrets invalidated by Keycloak client secret rotation on expiry.
target_output.open_questions_resolved: DUA verification workflow: platform admin uploads signed PDF to developer portal; developer_applications.status remains pending_dua until data_use_agreements record with dua_signed=true exists for the partner_org; Keycloak client creation is blocked until status transitions to active. This is system-enforced, not process-enforced. Kong config ownership: all Kong service and route configurations are owned by KongAutoRegistrar and generated from OpenAPI specs; Deck YAML files are limited to global gateway policies and annotated to prohibit service/route definitions. Kong Admin API access for engineers: read-only GET access to Kong Admin API permitted for debugging; write access (POST/PUT/DELETE) restricted to KongAutoRegistrar GitLab CI service account via mTLS client certificate.

metadata.notes: Round 2 cancer center API platform: Kong config ownership consolidation and cert-manager mTLS resolve the high-severity gateway config conflict and missing internal encryption; DUA gating state machine closes the medium-severity compliance gap.

reasoner_reviews.product: Event schedule, health screening listings, and registration CTAs are essential. The static approach is appropriate given the purely informational nature of this site. CMS-driven content means the marketing team can update event details without engineering involvement.
reasoner_reviews.architect_reasoner: Astro 4 with zero-JS-by-default island architecture is the correct choice here. CDN edge delivery absorbs the 50K+ registration spike without origin load. Contentful webhook-triggered rebuilds via GitHub Actions keep deployment lag under 3 minutes for content changes.
reasoner_reviews.security: No server-side attack surface simplifies the security posture significantly. CSP headers and subresource integrity for third-party analytics scripts are the primary controls needed. GA4 IP anonymization satisfies GDPR for EU visitors without a consent management platform.
reasoner_reviews.constraints: Firebase Hosting free tier handles 10GB/month bandwidth; peak event windows estimated at 3-5GB for 50K visitors at 100KB average page weight. Well under budget. Lighthouse 95 gate in CI prevents performance regressions before each deploy.
reasoner_reviews.critic: First-round plan omitted Contentful webhook integration details and did not address the CI Lighthouse gate configuration with explicit thresholds. These are operationally critical gaps that must be closed in revision.

frontend.app_structure: Astro 4 static site with island hydration only for the countdown timer and Google Maps embed components; all event schedule and screening content fully server-rendered at build time from Contentful

frontend.performance_notes: Astro partial hydration limits client JS to ~8KB; images served via Cloudinary with responsive srcsets and WebP/AVIF formats; Tailwind purged CSS; total page weight target under 120KB for Lighthouse >=95
frontend.frontend_risks: Contentful API rate limits during rapid content editing could delay rebuild triggers; mitigate with webhook debounce and manual rebuild escape hatch in GitHub Actions

security.auth_design: No authentication; site is fully public
security.abuse_prevention: Firebase Hosting DDoS protection at CDN edge; registration links redirect to Eventbrite which handles its own abuse controls
security.privacy_controls: GA4 with anonymize_ip: true; no cookies beyond GA4 analytics; GDPR cookie consent banner for EU visitors using a lightweight 2KB inline script

devops.ci_cd_design: Pipeline: bun install, astro build (fetches Contentful Delivery API), Lighthouse CI with >=95 performance threshold gate, firebase deploy --only hosting; Contentful webhook triggers same pipeline via repository_dispatch event
devops.environments: main branch deploys to production; PR branches auto-deploy to Firebase preview channels with unique URLs for stakeholder content review
devops.observability_stack: Firebase Hosting dashboard for bandwidth and request metrics; GA4 for user behavior and CTA tracking; GitHub Actions build status for pipeline health

target_output.thinking_summary: Round 2 revision for a static landing page. The core architecture (Astro 4, Firebase Hosting, Contentful) is correct and unchanged. Fixing ISS-001 by specifying the Contentful webhook-to-GitHub Actions repository_dispatch integration with debounce, and ISS-002 by detailing the lighthouserc.json configuration with per-category thresholds as a required CI gate before deploy.

fix_report.action_taken: Added detailed Contentful webhook integration: POST to GitHub Actions repository_dispatch endpoint with event_type content_updated triggers same build pipeline as git push, including 60-second debounce to prevent duplicate builds on rapid CMS edits.
fix_report.expected_outcome: Content editors publish event updates in Contentful and see changes live within 3 minutes without engineering intervention.

fix_report.action_taken: Added explicit Lighthouse CI configuration referencing lighthouserc.json with performance >=95, accessibility >=90, best-practices >=90, SEO >=95 thresholds enforced as a required CI check before Firebase deploy step.
